topics.title: Quality, Value, and Engineering – Quality Systems; Quality Engineering in Product Design and Production Process – System Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Quality, Value, and Engineering – Quality Systems; Quality Engineering in Product Design and Production Process – System Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Quality — The Multi-Dimensional Construct

### 1.1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality** is the totality of characteristics of an entity that bear on its ability to satisfy stated and implied needs (ISO 8402 / ISO 9000:2005).

In KTU terminology aligned with the 2024 scheme, **Quality** is a *multidimensional, customer-driven, conformance-oriented attribute* that unifies **Fitness for Use** (Juran), **Conformance to Requirements** (Crosby), and **Predictable Uniformity** (Deming). It is not a single variable but a synthesis of:

- **Intrinsic Quality** — performance, reliability, durability
- **Aesthetic Quality** — finish, appearance, feel
- **Perceived Quality** — brand reputation, customer impression

### 1.1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Restaurant Metaphor"**
>
> Imagine two restaurants serving identical biryani.
>
> - **Restaurant A** — Uses premium basmati rice, follows a standardized 14-step recipe card (Bill of Materials), measures spice weight to **±0.5 g** (tolerance control), conducts daily hygiene audits (prevention cost), and customer complaints are tracked in a register (appraisal cost).
> - **Restaurant B** — Uses random quantities, no recipe standard, occasional food-poisoning cases (failure cost).
>
> Both sell "biryani" — but **Quality differentiates them**. Restaurant A exhibits *conformance to standards*, *predictable uniformity*, and *low cost of poor quality*. This is precisely what manufacturing industries call **Quality Engineering**.

### 1.1.3 Quality — Definitions by Quality Gurus (KTU Board Favourite)

| Guru | Year | Definition of Quality | Keyword |
|------|------|----------------------|---------|
| **Joseph M. Juran** | 1951 | *Fitness for Use* | Customer satisfaction |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 1982 | *Predictable uniformity and dependability* | Variation reduction |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | 1979 | *Conformance to Requirements* | Zero defects |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | 1956 | *Total Quality Control* — the customer-determined composite | Customer-centric |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | 1985 | *Quality is a continuous process of improvement* | Company-wide |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | 1986 | *Quality is the loss imparted to society from the time the product is shipped* | Loss function |
| **ISO 9000** | 2005 | *Degree to which a set of inherent characteristics fulfils requirements* | Standards-based |

---

## 1.2 Value — The Economic Currency of Quality

### 1.2.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Value (V)** is the *worth, desirability, or utility* of a product/service as perceived by the customer relative to its cost and competing alternatives.

Mathematically expressed as the **Value Equation**:

$$
V = \frac{F}{C}
$$

Where:
- **F** = Function performed (performance, reliability, aesthetics)
- **C** = Cost incurred (purchase + ownership + maintenance)

### 1.2.2 Types of Value (KTU Classification)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Four Pillars of Value Engineering**
>
> 1. **Use Value (U.V.)** — Function derived from utility (e.g., a hammer driving nails)
> 2. **Esteem Value (E.V.)** — Possession, pride, status (e.g., Rolex watch)
> 3. **Cost Value (C.V.)** — Monetary worth of components/inputs
> 4. **Exchange Value (Ex.V.)** — Resale/trade-in worth

The fundamental **Value Equation** consolidates these:

$$
V_{total} = \frac{U.V. + E.V.}{C.V. + Ex.V.}
$$

### 1.2.3 Intuitive Analogy — "The Smartphone"

A ₹15,000 smartphone vs. a ₹1,50,000 flagship phone — both *make calls*. But:
- The flagship has a **better camera (F↑)**, longer software support, prestige (E.V.↑).
- Its cost is **higher (C↑)**.
- The *Value-per-rupee ratio* determines whether the customer buys it.

**Quality increases F**, **Value Engineering reduces C**, and the **Value Quotient** is engineered.

---

## 1.3 Engineering — The Discipline of Realization

### 1.3.1 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering** is the application of scientific, mathematical, and empirical knowledge to *design*, *develop*, *manufacture*, and *improve* products, processes, and systems that satisfy human needs.

Within the Quality context, **Quality Engineering (QE)** is the *operational branch* of engineering that ensures the product:

1. Is **designed right the first time** (Design Quality)
2. Is **manufactured consistently** (Conformance Quality)
3. **Performs reliably** in the field (Use-Phase Quality)

---

## 1.4 Quality Systems — The Structural Backbone

### 1.4.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> A **Quality System** is the *organizational structure, responsibilities, procedures, processes, and resources* needed to implement quality management (ISO 9000).

### 1.4.2 Hierarchy of Quality Systems (KTU Block Diagram)

> A quality system is a **4-tier pyramid**:
>
> ```
>           ┌─────────────────────┐
> Tier 4:   │  Quality Standards  │  (ISO 9001, AS9100, IATF 16949)
>           ├─────────────────────┤
> Tier 3:   │   Quality System    │  (Documentation, audits, records)
>           ├─────────────────────┤
> Tier 2:   │  Quality Control    │  (Inspection, testing, SPC)
>           ├─────────────────────┤
> Tier 1:   │  Quality Assurance  │  (Process discipline, prevention)
>           └─────────────────────┘
> ```

### 1.4.3 ISO 9000 Family (KTU 2024 High-Yield)

| Standard | Scope | Key Clause |
|----------|-------|------------|
| **ISO 9000** | Fundamentals & vocabulary | Definitions |
| **ISO 9001** | Requirements (certifiable) | 7 QM principles, 10 clauses |
| **ISO 9004** | Performance improvement guidance | Self-assessment |
| **ISO 19011** | Auditing guidelines | Internal/external audits |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Seven Quality Management Principles (ISO 9001:2015)**
>
> 1. Customer focus
> 2. Leadership
> 3. Engagement of people
> 4. Process approach
> 5. Improvement
> 6. Evidence-based decision making
> 7. Relationship management

---

## 1.5 Quality Engineering in Product Design

### 1.5.1 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Engineering in Product Design** is the systematic integration of *customer requirements*, *technical specifications*, *tolerances*, *reliability targets*, and *manufacturability* constraints into the *concept*, *prototype*, and *production-ready* stages.

### 1.5.2 The Design for Quality (DFQ) Workflow

```
Customer Voice (VOC) 
        ↓
Quality Function Deployment (QFD)
        ↓
Design Specifications (DS)
        ↓
Tolerances & Robust Design (Taguchi)
        ↓
Design for Manufacturability (DFM)
        ↓
Design for Assembly (DFA)
        ↓
Prototype Validation
        ↓
Production Release
```

---

## 1.6 Quality Engineering in Production Process

### 1.6.1 Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Production Process Quality Engineering** involves controlling, monitoring, and improving the *manufacturing process* to ensure that output consistently meets design specifications.

Core tools include:

- **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** — Shewhart control charts
- **Process Capability Indices** — **Cp**, **Cpk**
- **Acceptance Sampling** — MIL-STD-105E, ISO 2859
- **Failure Mode and Effects Analysis (FMEA)**
- **Poka-Yoke** (Mistake Proofing)

---

## 1.7 System Design (Quality-Centric)

### 1.7.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **System Design** is the *holistic integration* of inputs (materials, labour, machines, methods, environment) through a process to produce outputs (products/services) with feedback loops for continuous improvement.

In quality engineering, system design follows the **SIPOC + Feedback** model:

$$
System = \{Input, Process, Output, Feedback, Control\}
$$

The classical **Juran Trilogy** (Quality Planning → Quality Control → Quality Improvement) is the operational implementation of System Design.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Quality–Value–Cost Triangle (Deep Analysis)

### 2.1.1 Logical Breakdown

> **Step 1 — Quality Decomposition**
> Quality is decomposed into:
> - *Intrinsic* (design-driven)
> - *Conformance* (process-driven)
> - *Use-phase* (reliability-driven)

> **Step 2 — Value Equation Expansion**
> Value is the ratio of Function (F) to Cost (C). To increase V, we either:
> - Increase F (better engineering, robust design)
> - Decrease C (lean manufacturing, value analysis)
> - Or both (the ideal Quality Engineering outcome)

> **Step 3 — Cost of Quality (COQ) Integration**
> The cost of ensuring/assuring quality is itself a function of quality level. This creates the **COQ Curve**.

---

## 2.2 Cost of Quality (COQ) — The PAF Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **PAF Model (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure)** is the *foundational quality costing framework* approved by KTU 2024 Scheme and mandatory in ISO 9001 audits.

The total Cost of Quality is:

$$
COQ = C_{P} + C_{A} + C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

Where:
- **C_P** = Prevention Cost
- **C_A** = Appraisal Cost
- **C_IF** = Internal Failure Cost
- **C_EF** = External Failure Cost

### 2.2.1 Components of PAF (Detailed Bullet Logic)

> - **Prevention Costs (C_P)**
>   * Why: Stop defects *before* they occur
>   * How: Quality training, supplier audits, design reviews, FMEA workshops
>   * Examples: ₹ X spent on quality education → reduces defect rate by Y%
>
> - **Appraisal Costs (C_A)**
>   * Why: Detect defects *during* production
>   * How: Inspection, testing, calibration of gauges, SPC charts
>   * Examples: Incoming material inspection, in-process checks
>
> - **Internal Failure Costs (C_IF)**
>   * Why: Defects found *before* shipment
>   * How: Scrap, rework, reinspection, downtime
>   * Examples: Rejected batch destroyed → ₹ Z loss
>
> - **External Failure Costs (C_EF)**
>   * Why: Defects found *after* shipment
>   * How: Warranty claims, recalls, customer complaints, lawsuits
>   * Examples: Product recall cost, brand damage

### 2.2.2 The Optimal Quality Cost Point (The "Acceptable Quality Level" Decision)

The COQ curve has a **U-shape**:

```
   Cost
    │\
    │ \  Prevention + Appraisal
    │  \___________
    │   \         /
    │    \       /  Internal + External Failure
    │     \_____/
    │      ↓
    └──────────────────── Quality Level
            ↑
       Optimum (minimum COQ)
```

The **optimal point** is where the marginal cost of prevention equals the marginal savings from reduced failure.

---

## 2.3 Value Engineering (VE) — Lawrence Miles Methodology

### 2.3.1 The Job Plan (Six-Phase VE Job Plan)

| Phase | Name | Key Output |
|-------|------|------------|
| 1 | **Information Phase** | Collect cost & functional data |
| 2 | **Speculation (Creative) Phase** | Brainstorm alternatives |
| 3 | **Analysis (Evaluation) Phase** | Rank ideas by V ratio |
| 4 | **Development Phase** | Detailed cost proposals |
| 5 | **Recommendation Phase** | Present to management |
| 6 | **Implementation Phase** | Execute approved changes |

### 2.3.2 Function Analysis System Technique (FAST)

> [!NOTE]
> **FAST Diagram** is the *graphical tool* in VE that decomposes a product into its basic and secondary functions arranged in a left-to-right logical sequence.

```
[Higher Order] → [Basic Function] → [Secondary Function] → [Component]
   WHY?              WHAT?              HOW?                  WHERE?
```

---

## 2.4 Quality Function Deployment (QFD) — House of Quality

### 2.4.1 Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **QFD** is a structured planning tool that translates *customer requirements* (the Whats) into *technical specifications* (the Hows) using a matrix called the **House of Quality (HOQ)**.

### 2.4.2 The 6 Rooms of the House of Quality

1. **Customer Requirements (Whats)** — Voice of Customer
2. **Technical Requirements (Hows)** — Engineering metrics
3. **Relationship Matrix (Centre)** — Strong/Moderate/Weak correlations
4. **Roof (Top)** — Technical correlations (+, –, ×)
5. **Right Wing** — Competitive benchmarking
6. **Left Wing** — Importance weighting of customer requirements

---

## 2.5 Robust Design — Genichi Taguchi Philosophy

### 2.5.1 The Taguchi Loss Function

> [!NOTE]
> **Taguchi's Loss Function** quantifies the *societal loss* a product causes when its quality characteristic deviates from the target value.

$$
L(y) = k(y - m)^2
$$

Where:
- **L(y)** = Loss incurred at quality value y
- **k** = Quality loss coefficient (cost constant)
- **y** = Actual quality value
- **m** = Target value

### 2.5.2 Derivation of k

If the specification limits are $\Delta_0$ from target and the cost of replacement at that limit is $A_0$:

$$
k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}
$$

Therefore:

$$
L(y) = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}(y - m)^2
$$

### 2.5.3 Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratios

Taguchi classifies problems into three categories with specific S/N ratios:

| Type | S/N Formula | Goal |
|------|-------------|------|
| **Smaller-the-better** | $\eta = -10 \log\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)$ | Minimize response |
| **Larger-the-better** | $\eta = -10 \log\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum \frac{1}{y_i^2}\right)$ | Maximize response |
| **Nominal-the-best** | $\eta = 10 \log\left(\frac{\bar{y}^2}{s^2}\right)$ | Hit target exactly |

---

## 2.6 Tolerance Design & Design Margin

### 2.6.1 Bilateral vs. Unilateral Tolerances

- **Bilateral:** $m \pm \Delta$ (e.g., $10 \pm 0.1$ mm)
- **Unilateral:** $m^{+}_{0}$ or $m_{0}^{+}$ (e.g., $10^{+0.2}_{0}$ mm)

### 2.6.2 Tolerance Stack-Up (Worst Case)

For n components in series:

$$
T_{assembly} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i
$$

### 2.6.3 Statistical Tolerance Stack-Up (RSS Method)

$$
T_{assembly} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^2}
$$

(RSS = Root Sum of Squares; assumes normal distributions)

---

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Quick Reference)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **FORMULA SHEET — KTU Module 1 (Quality, Value & Engineering)**

| # | Formula | Meaning | Units |
|---|---------|---------|-------|
| 1 | $V = \frac{F}{C}$ | Value Index | Dimensionless |
| 2 | $COQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Cost of Quality | ₹ / $ |
| 3 | $L(y) = k(y-m)^2$ | Taguchi Loss | ₹ / unit |
| 4 | $k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$ | Loss Coefficient | ₹ / unit² |
| 5 | $C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Process Capability | Dimensionless |
| 6 | $C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma}, \frac{\mu-LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process Capability Index | Dimensionless |
| 7 | $T_{wc} = \sum T_i$ | Tolerance Stack-up (Worst) | mm |
| 8 | $T_{rss} = \sqrt{\sum T_i^2}$ | Tolerance Stack-up (RSS) | mm |
| 9 | $\eta_{STB} = -10\log\left(\frac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right)$ | S/N Smaller-Better | dB |
| 10 | $\eta_{NTB} = 10\log\left(\frac{\bar{y}^2}{s^2}\right)$ | S/N Nominal-Best | dB |
| 11 | $V_{tot} = \frac{U.V. + E.V.}{C.V. + Ex.V.}$ | Total Value Index | Dimensionless |
| 12 | $DPMO = \frac{D \times 10^6}{U}$ | Defects per Million Opportunities | ppm-equiv |
| 13 | $RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i$ | Rolled Throughput Yield | Decimal |

---

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Tool Used |
|----------|-------------|-----------|
| Automotive (e.g., Tata Motors) | Crash test optimization | Robust Design, DOE |
| Software (e.g., TCS, Infosys) | Defect reduction | Six Sigma DMAIC |
| Aerospace (ISRO, Boeing) | Mission-critical reliability | FMEA, FTA, ISO 9001/AS9100 |
| Pharma | Validation of drug purity | Statistical Sampling |
| Electronics (Smartphone) | Display calibration | Tolerance Design, SPC |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Taguchi Loss Coefficient (k)

### 3.1.1 Given

- Target value of quality characteristic: $m$
- Functional tolerance limit: $m \pm \Delta_0$
- Average loss to society when deviation reaches $\Delta_0$: $A_0$ (cost of replacing/scraping the product)

### 3.1.2 Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Taguchi assumed a quadratic loss function:} \\
& L(y) = k(y - m)^2 \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{At } y = m + \Delta_0 \text{, loss equals } A_0: \\
& L(m + \Delta_0) = k(\Delta_0)^2 = A_0 \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Solve for } k: \\
& k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} \\
\\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Therefore the complete loss function is:} \\
& L(y) = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}(y - m)^2
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conversion Logic — Step-by-Step Text**:
> The Taguchi quadratic assumption means *loss grows parabolically* with deviation. By plugging the boundary condition (loss $A_0$ at deviation $\Delta_0$), we can algebraically isolate $k$. This constant then quantifies *every unit's loss contribution* regardless of whether the deviation is small or large.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical — Taguchi Loss Calculation

### 3.2.1 Problem Statement

> An axle manufacturer specifies the diameter as **$50.000 \pm 0.020$ mm**. When the deviation reaches **$0.020$ mm**, the customer returns the part at a cost of **₹ 400 per unit** (replacement + shipping + goodwill loss). Calculate the loss for a measured diameter of **$50.015$ mm**.

### 3.2.2 Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Identify given values:} \\
& m = 50.000 \text{ mm}, \quad y = 50.015 \text{ mm}, \quad \Delta_0 = 0.020 \text{ mm}, \quad A_0 = 400 \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Calculate } k: \\
& k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} = \frac{400}{(0.020)^2} = \frac{400}{0.0004} = 1{,}000{,}000 \text{ ₹ / mm}^2 \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Calculate deviation:} \\
& (y - m)^2 = (50.015 - 50.000)^2 = (0.015)^2 = 0.000225 \text{ mm}^2 \\
\\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Calculate loss:} \\
& L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2 = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 0.000225 = 225 \text{ ₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer:** A deviation of just $0.015$ mm (still within tolerance!) causes a loss of **₹ 225 per unit** to society. **This proves Taguchi's principle: traditional "within-spec" is NOT zero-loss.**

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — Value Engineering Decision

### 3.3.1 Problem Statement

> A product currently costs ₹ 800 to produce, and the customer value function is rated **5/10**. Engineering proposes two alternatives:
>
> - **Alternative A:** Reduces cost to ₹ 600, maintains function at 5.
> - **Alternative B:** Increases function to 8, increases cost to ₹ 950.
>
> Compute the Value Index for current and alternatives. Recommend the best.

### 3.3.2 Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Current Value Index:} \\
& V_{current} = \frac{F}{C} = \frac{5}{800} = 0.00625 \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Alternative A:} \\
& V_A = \frac{5}{600} = 0.00833 \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Alternative B:} \\
& V_B = \frac{8}{950} = 0.00842 \\
\\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Decision:} \\
& V_B > V_A > V_{current} \\
& \text{Alternative B is recommended (highest value ratio).}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering Insight:** Even though Alternative B costs **more**, the additional function justifies the cost increase — a textbook **Value Engineering** decision.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical — Cost of Quality (COQ) Analysis

### 3.4.1 Problem Statement

> A company's quality costs for the year are:
>
> | Category | Amount (₹ Lakhs) |
> |----------|------------------|
> | Prevention | 2.0 |
> | Appraisal | 3.0 |
> | Internal Failure | 6.0 |
> | External Failure | 9.0 |
>
> Calculate total COQ and the **ratio of conformance to non-conformance cost**.

### 3.4.2 Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Conformance Cost} = C_P + C_A = 2.0 + 3.0 = 5.0 \text{ Lakh} \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Non-Conformance Cost} = C_{IF} + C_{EF} = 6.0 + 9.0 = 15.0 \text{ Lakh} \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Total COQ} = 5.0 + 15.0 = 20.0 \text{ Lakh} \\
\\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Ratio} = \frac{\text{Conformance}}{\text{Non-Conformance}} = \frac{5.0}{15.0} = \frac{1}{3}
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The company is spending **3× more on failure than on prevention** — a classic *fire-fighting* quality culture. Recommendation: **shift budget from failure to prevention** (Juran's "Gold Mine" principle).

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparing Six-Sigma, TQM, and ISO 9000

| Dimension | Six Sigma | TQM | ISO 9001 |
|-----------|-----------|-----|----------|
| **Origin** | Motorola (1986) | Feigenbaum/Ishikawa | ISO (1987) |
| **Focus** | Variation reduction | Customer satisfaction | Documentation |
| **Methodology** | DMAIC / DMADV | Continuous improvement | PDCA |
| **Metric** | DPMO (≤ 3.4) | Customer satisfaction index | Audit pass rate |
| **Statistical Rigor** | Very High | Medium | Low |
| **Certification** | Internal (Black Belt) | None | External (Auditor) |
| **KTU 2024 Relevance** | High (DMAIC) | High (philosophy) | High (systems) |

---

## 3.6 Derivation — Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)

### 3.6.1 Concept

> Each process step has a yield $Y_i$. The overall yield is multiplicative.

$$
RTY = \prod_{i=1}^{n} Y_i
$$

### 3.6.2 Numerical Example

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Process:} \quad & \text{Step 1 yield} = 0.95 \\
& \text{Step 2 yield} = 0.92 \\
& \text{Step 3 yield} = 0.98 \\
\\
\text{Step-by-step:} \quad & RTY = 0.95 \times 0.92 \times 0.98 \\
& = 0.95 \times 0.9016 \\
& = 0.8565 \\
\\
\text{Insight:} \quad & \text{First-pass yield drops from 95\% to 85.6\% after 3 steps.}
\end{aligned}
$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 System Design Block Diagram — Quality Engineering Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Customer Needs VOC] --> B[Quality Planning]
    B --> C[Product Design]
    C --> D[Process Design]
    D --> E[Production]
    E --> F[Quality Control SPC]
    F --> G{Conformance Check}
    G -- Pass --> H[Customer Delivery]
    G -- Fail --> I[Rework or Scrap]
    I --> J[Failure Analysis]
    J --> K[Corrective Action]
    K --> B
    H --> L[Customer Feedback]
    L --> B
```

> **Node mapping:**
> - `A` = Voice of Customer input
> - `B` = Juran's Quality Planning
> - `C` = Design Phase (QFD, Robust Design)
> - `D` = Process Phase (Tolerance, FMEA)
> - `E` = Manufacturing
> - `F` = SPC monitoring
> - `G` = Decision gate
> - `H`, `I` = Downstream branches
> - `K` → `B` = **Feedback loop (PDCA closure)**

---

## 4.2 Quality System Architecture (4-Tier Pyramid)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Q1[Tier 1 Quality Assurance Prevention Focus] --> Q2[Tier 2 Quality Control Detection Inspection]
    Q2 --> Q3[Tier 3 Quality System Documentation Audits]
    Q3 --> Q4[Tier 4 Quality Standards ISO 9001 AS9100 IATF 16949]
```

---

## 4.3 House of Quality (QFD) — Block-Level Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Left Wing Customer Requirements Whats] --> R2[Center Relationship Matrix Strong Moderate Weak]
    R2 --> R3[Right Wing Competitive Benchmark]
    R3 --> R4[Bottom Technical Requirements Hows]
    R4 --> R5[Top Roof Technical Correlations]
    R5 --> R1
```

---

## 4.4 Value Engineering Job Plan Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Phase 1 Information Gathering] --> P2[Phase 2 Speculation Brainstorm]
    P2 --> P3[Phase 3 Analysis Evaluation]
    P3 --> P4[Phase 4 Development Detailed Cost]
    P4 --> P5[Phase 5 Recommendation to Management]
    P5 --> P6[Phase 6 Implementation and Verification]
    P6 -- Feedback --> P1
```

---

## 4.5 Taguchi Loss Function Curve (Conceptual Block Map)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Target Value m] --> L2{Deviation Occurs}
    L2 --> L3[Small Deviation Small Loss k times small squared]
    L2 --> L4[Large Deviation Large Loss k times large squared]
    L3 --> L5[Within Spec NOT Zero Loss]
    L4 --> L6[Beyond Spec Catastrophic Loss]
```

---

## 4.6 PAF Cost Model — Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    D1[Total Quality Spend] --> D2{Type of Cost}
    D2 --> D3[Prevention Cost]
    D2 --> D4[Appraisal Cost]
    D2 --> D5[Internal Failure Cost]
    D2 --> D6[External Failure Cost]
    D3 --> E1[Conformance Cost]
    D4 --> E1
    D5 --> E2[Non Conformance Cost]
    D6 --> E2
    E1 --> F1[Optimal Quality Zone]
    E2 --> F1
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Define Quality according to Juran and Crosby. Compare their perspectives.**

**Model Answer:**

> **Joseph Juran** defined Quality as **"Fitness for Use"** — the product must satisfy customer needs in intended use conditions. This includes availability, reliability, and maintainability.
>
> **Philip Crosby** defined Quality as **"Conformance to Requirements"** — quality is achieved when the product strictly adheres to the documented specifications. Crosby famously advocated **"Zero Defects"** and **"Quality is Free"** — arguing that prevention costs are always less than failure costs.
>
> **Comparison:**
>
> | Aspect | Juran | Crosby |
> |--------|-------|--------|
> | Customer-centric | Yes | Indirect (specs represent customer) |
> | Defect philosophy | Acceptable level of defects | Zero defects |
> | Focus | Fitness | Conformance |
> | Cost view | Quality costs money but pays back | Quality is free (prevention saves money) |

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

**What is a Quality System? List the hierarchy of quality systems.**

**Model Answer:**

> A **Quality System** is the organizational framework comprising structure, responsibilities, procedures, processes, and resources required to implement quality management.
>
> **Hierarchy (4 Tiers):**
> 1. **Quality Standards** — ISO 9001, AS9100 (Top tier)
> 2. **Quality System** — Documentation, audits
> 3. **Quality Control** — Inspection, SPC
> 4. **Quality Assurance** — Process discipline (Foundation tier)

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain the Cost of Quality (COQ) model with a neat sketch. Discuss the four cost categories and the optimal COQ point.**

**Model Solution:**

> The **Cost of Quality (COQ)** model, also called the **PAF Model**, categorizes all quality-related expenditure into four categories:
>
> **1. Prevention Cost (C_P)** — Costs incurred to *prevent* defects.
> Examples: Quality training, design reviews, supplier audits, FMEA workshops.
>
> **2. Appraisal Cost (C_A)** — Costs incurred to *detect* defects.
> Examples: Incoming inspection, in-process testing, gauge calibration, SPC.
>
> **3. Internal Failure Cost (C_IF)** — Costs of defects found *before* shipment.
> Examples: Scrap, rework, reinspection, production downtime.
>
> **4. External Failure Cost (C_EF)** — Costs of defects found *after* shipment.
> Examples: Warranty, recalls, complaints, lawsuits, brand damage.
>
> **Formula:**
> $COQ = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$
>
> **Sketch (U-curve):**
>
> ```
>   Cost │  \
>        │   \   Prevention+Appraisal
>        │    \____
>        │     \   /
>        │      \_/  Internal+External Failure
>        └─────────────── Quality Level
>                  ↑
>               OPTIMUM
> ```
>
> **Optimal Point:** The minimum of the U-curve, where the marginal cost of prevention equals marginal savings from reduced failures. Beyond this point, spending more on prevention does not reduce failure costs proportionately.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**A company reports the following annual quality costs:**
> - Prevention: ₹ 1,50,000
> - Appraisal: ₹ 2,00,000
> - Internal Failure: ₹ 4,50,000
> - External Failure: ₹ 6,00,000
>
> **Calculate (i) Total COQ, (ii) Conformance-to-Non-Conformance ratio, (iii) Suggest one strategic improvement.**

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Total COQ} = 1{,}50{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}50{,}000 + 6{,}00{,}000 \\
& = 14{,}00{,}000 \text{ ₹} \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Conformance Cost} = 1.5 + 2.0 = 3.5 \text{ Lakh} \\
& \text{Non-Conformance Cost} = 4.5 + 6.0 = 10.5 \text{ Lakh} \\
& \text{Ratio} = \frac{3.5}{10.5} = \frac{1}{3} \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Strategic Recommendation:} \\
& \text{Shift 30\% of failure budget to prevention (training, FMEA, design reviews).} \\
& \text{This typically reduces Internal Failure by 50\% within 2 years.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - June 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Explain Taguchi's Loss Function. Derive the loss coefficient k.**

**Model Solution:**

> **Concept:** Taguchi argued that *any* deviation from the target — even within specification limits — causes loss to society. This is captured by the **Quadratic Loss Function**:
>
> $$L(y) = k(y - m)^2$$
>
> **Derivation of k:**
>
> Let the specification limit be $m \pm \Delta_0$, and the average loss at this limit be $A_0$.
>
> Applying the boundary condition:
>
> $$L(m + \Delta_0) = k(\Delta_0)^2 = A_0$$
>
> Solving:
>
> $$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}$$
>
> Hence:
>
> $$L(y) = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}(y - m)^2$$
>
> **Engineering Significance:**
>
> - **Zero loss** only at $y = m$ (target).
> - Loss grows quadratically — a deviation of $2\Delta_0$ causes **4× the loss** of $\Delta_0$.
> - Quality engineering should aim for **target-centred design**, not just "within specs."

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**For a component, the target diameter is 25.000 mm. If the specification limit is $\pm 0.025$ mm and the cost of replacement at this limit is ₹ 625, find the loss for a measured diameter of 25.010 mm. Comment on the result.**

**Model Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1:} \quad & m = 25.000, \quad y = 25.010, \quad \Delta_0 = 0.025, \quad A_0 = 625 \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} = \frac{625}{(0.025)^2} = \frac{625}{0.000625} = 1{,}000{,}000 \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & (y - m)^2 = (25.010 - 25.000)^2 = (0.010)^2 = 0.0001 \\
\\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & L(y) = 1{,}000{,}000 \times 0.0001 = 100 \text{ ₹} \\
\\
\text{Comment:} \quad & \text{Although 25.010 mm is WITHIN the specification range (±0.025),} \\
& \text{it causes a ₹ 100 loss per unit. This contradicts the traditional "OK" mindset} \\
& \text{and demonstrates Taguchi's philosophy — on-target production is the only true zero-loss state.}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones (Module 1)**
>
> 1. **Confusing Quality Assurance with Quality Control** — Students often write them interchangeably. **QA = process-oriented (prevention); QC = product-oriented (detection).** Marks deducted if interchanged.
>
> 2. **Skipping the derivation steps in Taguchi Loss Function** — Examiners allocate 4–5 marks specifically for the algebraic boundary-condition substitution. Writing only the final formula loses marks.
>
> 3. **PAF classification errors** — *Training cost* is **Prevention**, not Appraisal. *Warranty claim* is **External Failure**, not Internal. Many students swap these.
>
> 4. **Value Equation mistake** — Writing $V = F \times C$ instead of $V = F/C$. This single error loses **3 marks**.
>
> 5. **ISO clause numbering** — KTU specifically tests ISO 9001:2015's 10-clause structure (Clauses 4–10). Writing the *old* ISO 9001:2008 8-clause version attracts partial marking only.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MODULE 1 RAPID-REVISION CHECKLIST**
>
> - [ ] Quality definitions by **Juran, Deming, Crosby, Feigenbaum, Ishikawa, Taguchi, ISO**
> - [ ] Value Equation: **$V = F/C$** — never F × C
> - [ ] Four types of Value: **Use, Esteem, Cost, Exchange**
> - [ ] **PAF Model** — Prevention, Appraisal, Internal Failure, External Failure
> - [ ] COQ = $C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ — U-shaped curve, optimum point
> - [ ] **Quality System Hierarchy** — 4-tier pyramid
> - [ ] **ISO 9001:2015** — 10 clauses, 7 QM principles
> - [ ] **Taguchi Loss Function** — $L = k(y-m)^2$, $k = A_0 / \Delta_0^2$
> - [ ] **QFD House of Quality** — 6 rooms, Whats/Hows matrix
> - [ ] **VE Job Plan** — 6 phases (Information → Implementation)
> - [ ] **S/N Ratios** — Smaller-better, Larger-better, Nominal-best
> - [ ] **Tolerance stack-up** — Worst case (sum) vs. RSS (root-sum-squares)
> - [ ] **RTY** = product of individual yields
> - [ ] **TQM** vs. **Six Sigma** vs. **ISO 9000** — know the differences
> - [ ] **QA vs. QC** — QA = process prevention; QC = product detection
> - [ ] **Design for Quality workflow** — VOC → QFD → Robust Design → DFM → DFA → Production
> - [ ] **SIPOC model** for System Design
> - [ ] **Juran Trilogy** — Quality Planning, Quality Control, Quality Improvement
