topics.title: Parameter Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Parameter Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Parameter Design** is the second stage of Genichi Taguchi's **Three-Stage Design System** (following System Design and preceding Tolerance Design). It is the engineering methodology of *deliberately selecting the optimum levels of controllable design factors* (parameters) so that a product or process becomes **robust** — i.e., its performance and variability are minimally affected by uncontrollable **Noise Factors** (raw material variation, ambient temperature, operator skill, machine wear, environmental humidity, etc.) present in actual field-use conditions.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, Parameter Design is positioned as the heart of **Robust Engineering** and the bridge between *System Design* (concept selection) and *Tolerance Design* (specification tightening).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Taguchi's Axiom:** *"A product or process should be designed such that it is insensitive to the variation in the uncontrollable noise factors in its intended operating environment."*

The two principal pillars of Parameter Design are:
1. **Quality Loss Function (QLF)** — quantifying the economic loss to society due to deviation from target.
2. **Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio** — a single performance metric combining *mean shift* and *variability reduction*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine you are designing a **wooden chair for outdoor cafés in Kerala**. Two realities confront you:

- ☀️ **Controllable Factors** — leg thickness, type of wood polish, joinery angle, varnish coats (you can set them in the factory).
- 🌧️ **Uncontrollable Noise** — monsoon humidity swings (70% → 95% RH), coastal salt air, uneven floor tiles in cafés, customers of different weights.

A *naïve* engineer sets the parameters at face value. A *Taguchi-trained* engineer, however, asks:
*"At which combination of leg thickness × polish type × joinery angle does the chair remain wobble-free AND aesthetically consistent across the *entire range* of humidity and load — without needing expensive, ultra-tight tolerance on each input?"*

That optimal, robustness-driven sweet-spot of controllable settings is what **Parameter Design** hunts for. It replaces the old "set-point" mentality with a **"design for variation"** mentality.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Takeaway:** Parameter Design does **not** tighten tolerances to fight variation — it **moves the operating point** of controllable factors to a region of *insensitivity* to noise.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Terminology in Parameter Design

| Term | Symbol | Standard Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Quality Loss | **L(y)** | Economic loss to society when quality characteristic deviates from target |
| Loss Coefficient | **k** | Constant converting deviation² into monetary loss |
| Target Value | **m** | Nominal/ideal value of the quality characteristic |
| Deviation | **(y − m)** | Difference between observed and target quality |
| Signal-to-Noise Ratio | **η (eta)** | Combined metric of mean & variance (in **dB**) |
| Control Factor | **C** | Design parameter whose level is *controllable* by the engineer |
| Noise Factor | **N** | Uncontrollable environmental/operational factor |
| Inner Array | OA | Orthogonal Array (OA) carrying the *control factors* |
| Outer Array | OA | Array carrying the *noise factors* (in classic Taguchi crossed-array design) |
| Robustness | — | Insensitivity of performance to noise factors |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ⚙️ Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Parameter Design

### 🧩 Pillar 1: Quality Loss Function (QLF)

Taguchi replaced the classical "**inside-spec-is-good, outside-spec-is-bad**" step function (binary acceptance) with a smooth, continuous, **quadratic loss function**. Loss to society begins the *instant* quality deviates from the target, not when it crosses the spec limit.

The general form is:

$$
L(y) = k \, (y - m)^{2}
$$

Where:
- **L(y)** = Quality loss experienced when the quality characteristic takes value *y*
- **m** = Target value (most desired value)
- **k** = Loss coefficient (in monetary units / unit²)
- **(y − m)** = Deviation from target

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Loss is **always positive** and grows as a **parabola**. Even if a value is "within specification," society still suffers a *non-zero loss* — this is Taguchi's revolutionary departure from acceptance-sampling theory.

### Derivation of the Loss Coefficient *k*

Suppose the customer's loss is **A₀** at the functional tolerance limit **Δ₀ = (y₀ − m)**. Then:

$$
A_0 = k \, (y_0 - m)^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}
$$

So the full **Quality Loss Function** is:

$$
L(y) = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}\,(y - m)^{2}
$$

### Average Loss Over a Sample of Size *n*

$$
\bar{L} = \dfrac{k}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - m)^2
$$

### Average Loss When Only Mean and Variance Are Known

Using the identity $\dfrac{1}{n}\sum(y_i-m)^2 = (\bar{y}-m)^2 + s^2$:

$$
\bar{L} = k\left[(\bar{y} - m)^2 + s^2\right]
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This identity is the **mathematical foundation** of Taguchi's S/N ratio design. It splits the total quality loss into:
> - a **bias-squared** term $(\bar{y}-m)^2$ — captures *accuracy* (deviation of mean from target)
> - a **variance** term $s^2$ — captures *precision* (spread of observations)

### 🧩 Pillar 2: Signal-to-Noise (S/N) Ratio

The S/N ratio, denoted **η (eta)**, compresses the loss into a single log-scale number, **higher is better**. Taguchi prescribed **three canonical cases** of the quality characteristic.

#### Case A — *Nominal-the-Best* (NTB): target value *m* exists and is finite
Used when the characteristic has a specific desired value (e.g., a resistor of exactly 100 Ω, a chemical concentration of 5.0%).

$$
\text{MSD} = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - m)^2
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\eta = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{\mu^2}{\sigma^2}\right) \approx 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{\text{MSD}}\right)\;}
$$

The logarithmic form of the sample S/N ratio:

$$
\eta = -10 \log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - m)^2\right]\ \text{dB}
$$

#### Case B — *Smaller-the-Better* (STB): target is **zero** (no defect)
Used when smaller characteristic values mean better quality (e.g., shrinkage, wear, leak rate, emissions, contamination).

$$
\boxed{\;\eta = -10 \log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^{2}\right]\ \text{dB}\;}
$$

#### Case C — *Larger-the-Better* (LTB): target is **infinity**
Used when larger characteristic values mean better quality (e.g., bond strength, fuel efficiency, battery life).

$$
\boxed{\;\eta = -10 \log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}\dfrac{1}{y_i^{2}}\right]\ \text{dB}\;}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistake:** Students often forget the **negative sign** in STB and LTB formulae. The negative sign is essential because the *raw* mean square is in the denominator; we maximize the S/N by *minimizing* the MSD, hence the negative log yields a higher number for the *better* condition.

### 🧩 Pillar 3: Control Factors vs. Noise Factors

| Aspect | Control Factor (C) | Noise Factor (N) |
|---|---|---|
| Controllability in lab / production | ✅ Easily set & held | ❌ Cannot be held constant |
| Cost to control | Low to moderate | High or impossible |
| Example | Sintering temperature, annealing time | Ambient humidity, raw-material lot variation, operator fatigue |
| Role in Parameter Design | Varied across **Inner Array** levels | Varied across **Outer Array** levels |
| Robustness indicator | Its effect on η | Its effect (ideally minimized) on η |

### Inner Array × Outer Array (Classic Taguchi Crossed-Array Experiment)

In Taguchi's classic (c.1980) approach, two separate Orthogonal Arrays are run:

- **Inner Array** → Control factors (the design we are *optimizing*).
- **Outer Array** → Noise factors (the *field conditions* to be robust against).
- **Total runs** = (Inner array runs) × (Outer array runs).
- For each inner-array run, compute the **S/N ratio** from the *outer-array responses* under that control setting.

The control-factor level that **maximizes η** is robust to the chosen noise environment.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern industrial practice (especially in automotive, e.g., the **Ford Robust Design** method) often replaces the crossed outer array with a single integrated OA and uses *signal factors* (control factors that intentionally shift the mean) in the same array — this is called the **combined array** or **single-array** approach. KTU examiners expect familiarity with the *classic crossed* approach as primary.

## 2.2 The Seven Steps of Parameter Design (Procedural Map)

1. **Identify the Quality Characteristic (y)** and decide its category (NTB / STB / LTB).
2. **Identify Noise Factors (N)** and their levels in the *anticipated field environment*.
3. **Identify Control Factors (C)** and assign test levels.
4. **Select the Inner Array** (OA large enough for control factors).
5. **Select the Outer Array** (OA large enough for noise factors).
6. **Conduct the experiment** and compute η for each inner-array run.
7. **Perform ANOVA on η** (and on **mean ȳ** for NTB) → identify optimal control-factor levels and conduct **Confirmation Run**.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Meaning / Where Used |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $L(y) = k(y-m)^2$ | Quality Loss at a single value *y* |
| 2 | $k = A_0 / \Delta_0^2$ | Loss coefficient from tolerance economics |
| 3 | $\bar{L} = \dfrac{k}{n}\sum(y_i-m)^2$ | Average loss over *n* observations |
| 4 | $\bar{L} = k[(\bar{y}-m)^2 + s^2]$ | Loss split into bias² + variance |
| 5 | $\eta_{\text{NTB}} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum(y_i-m)^2\right]$ | Nominal-the-best S/N ratio |
| 6 | $\eta_{\text{STB}} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right]$ | Smaller-the-better S/N ratio |
| 7 | $\eta_{\text{LTB}} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left[\dfrac{1}{n}\sum(1/y_i^2)\right]$ | Larger-the-better S/N ratio |
| 8 | Total Runs = (Inner OA runs) × (Outer OA runs) | Crossed-array experiment size |
| 9 | % Contribution = (SS<sub>factor</sub> − V<sub>e</sub>·df<sub>factor</sub>) × 100 / SS<sub>total</sub> | ANOVA factor-importance metric |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Parameter Design is the foundation of **Six Sigma DMAIC's "Improve" phase**, automotive APQP, aerospace robust engineering, semiconductor process control, and pharmaceutical QbD (Quality by Design). It is also the explicit mandate of **IATF 16949:2016 clause 8.3.3.3** for "Special Characteristics."

## 2.4 Why Parameter Design Beats Classical OFAT (One-Factor-At-A-Time)

- **Captures interactions** between control factors through orthogonal-array fractional factorial structure.
- **Quantifies robustness** through S/N ratio — classical DOE uses *mean response*, ignoring variance.
- **Reduces experimental cost** by fractional replication with orthogonality preserved.
- **Aligns engineering economics** via the loss function (dollar-impact of every deviated unit).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

## 3.1 Full Derivation of the Quality Loss Function (QLF)

We begin with Taguchi's assumptions and arrive at the quadratic form $L(y) = k(y-m)^2$.

### Step 1 — Define the Anchor Points
- Let **m** = target value of the quality characteristic. By definition, the loss is **zero** at *y = m*:

$$
L(m) = 0
$$

- Let **y₀** be the functional tolerance limit, and **A₀** the customer's loss at this limit:

$$
L(y_0) = A_0
$$

### Step 2 — Assume a Smooth, Symmetric, Quadratic Loss
The simplest *continuous* function satisfying $L(m)=0$, $L(y_0)=A_0$, and symmetric around *m* is a parabola:

$$
L(y) = k(y-m)^2
$$

### Step 3 — Apply the Boundary Condition
Substituting $y = y_0$:

$$
A_0 = k(y_0 - m)^2
$$

Let the deviation at the tolerance limit be $\Delta_0 = y_0 - m$:

$$
A_0 = k\,\Delta_0^{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{2}}
$$

### Step 4 — Final Form

$$
\boxed{\;L(y) = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{2}}\,(y - m)^{2}\;}
$$

### Step 5 — Loss to Society Across a Production Lot
For *n* units in a production run with values $y_1, y_2, \dots, y_n$:

$$
L_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} L(y_i) = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{2}}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - m)^2
$$

Average loss per unit:

$$
\bar{L} = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{2}}\cdot\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - m)^2
$$

### Step 6 — Decomposition into Bias and Variance
Using the algebraic identity for any set of observations:

$$
\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - m)^2 \;=\; (\bar{y} - m)^2 + \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2
$$

Let $s^2$ denote the sample variance:

$$
s^2 = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n}(y_i - \bar{y})^2
$$

Therefore:

$$
\bar{L} = k\,[(\bar{y} - m)^2 + s^2]
$$

This decomposition is **the single most important result** of Taguchi's robust-design framework: it shows that *minimizing the loss* requires simultaneously driving the **mean to the target** *and* driving the **variance to zero**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** The bias² term $(\bar{y}-m)^2$ is the *systematic* loss (correctable by moving the operating point). The variance term $s^2$ is the *random* loss (reduced by robust design). Parameter Design attacks **both**.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Loss Coefficient from Customer Economics

**Problem (KTU-style):** A manufacturer of piston rings specifies the target diameter as **m = 50.00 mm**. The functional tolerance limit is **Δ₀ = 0.05 mm**, beyond which the ring fails in the engine and the customer's warranty loss is estimated as **A₀ = ₹400 per ring**. Compute the loss coefficient and the loss when a ring is produced at *y* = 50.03 mm.

**Solution:**

### Step A — Compute *k*

$$
k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2} = \dfrac{400}{(0.05)^2} = \dfrac{400}{0.0025} = 160{,}000\ \text{₹/mm}^2
$$

### Step B — Compute Deviation
$$
y - m = 50.03 - 50.00 = 0.03\ \text{mm}
$$

### Step C — Compute Loss
$$
L(y) = k(y-m)^2 = 160{,}000 \times (0.03)^2 = 160{,}000 \times 0.0009
$$

$$
\boxed{\;L(50.03) = \text{₹ } 144\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** Although 50.03 mm lies *within* the 0.05 mm tolerance, the customer has already lost ₹144 due to that single ring. This illustrates the flaw of the classical step-function tolerance model.

### Step D — Loss at the Tolerance Limit (Sanity Check)
$$
L(50.05) = 160{,}000 \times (0.05)^2 = 160{,}000 \times 0.0025 = \text{₹ } 400 \;\checkmark
$$

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — S/N Ratio for *Smaller-the-Better*

**Problem:** A welding-process study produces the following bead-width measurements (in mm) for a particular control-factor combination across 5 replicate runs:
$$y_1 = 1.4,\ y_2 = 1.6,\ y_3 = 1.5,\ y_4 = 1.7,\ y_5 = 1.3$$
Smaller bead width is better. Compute the S/N ratio.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Identify the formula
For STB:

$$
\eta = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^{2}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Sum of Squares
$$
\sum y_i^2 = 1.4^2 + 1.6^2 + 1.5^2 + 1.7^2 + 1.3^2
$$

$$
= 1.96 + 2.56 + 2.25 + 2.89 + 1.69 = 11.35
$$

### Step 3 — Compute Mean Square
$$
\dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2 = \dfrac{11.35}{5} = 2.27
$$

### Step 4 — Take Log
$$
\log_{10}(2.27) = 0.3560
$$

### Step 5 — Apply the Negative Sign and Multiply by 10

$$
\eta = -10 \times 0.3560
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{STB}} = -3.56\ \text{dB}\;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Validation:** This is a small negative number (typical of STB/LTB). To *improve* the process, we re-run with a different control-factor level combination and *recompute* η. The combination with the *largest* η is selected as the robust optimum.

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — S/N Ratio for *Nominal-the-Best*

**Problem:** A CNC lathe produces a shaft with target diameter **m = 25.00 mm**. Five replicate measurements are: 25.02, 24.99, 25.01, 24.98, 25.00 mm. Compute the NTB S/N ratio.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Formula
$$
\eta = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum (y_i - m)^2\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Deviations
$$
(y_i - m) = +0.02,\ -0.01,\ +0.01,\ -0.02,\ 0.00
$$

### Step 3 — Square & Sum
$$
\sum(y_i-m)^2 = (0.02)^2 + (0.01)^2 + (0.01)^2 + (0.02)^2 + (0)^2
$$

$$
= 0.0004 + 0.0001 + 0.0001 + 0.0004 + 0 = 0.0010
$$

### Step 4 — Mean Square
$$
\text{MSD} = \dfrac{0.0010}{5} = 0.0002
$$

### Step 5 — Log
$$
\log_{10}(0.0002) = -3.69897
$$

### Step 6 — Final S/N

$$
\eta = -10 \times (-3.69897) = 36.99\ \text{dB}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{NTB}} \approx 37.0\ \text{dB}\;}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **For NTB**, we typically run **two ANOVAs**: one on **η** (to minimize variability) and one on the **mean ȳ** (to drive the mean to target). The optimal level chosen for each control factor is whichever maximizes η *and* brings ȳ closest to *m*.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — S/N Ratio for *Larger-the-Better*

**Problem:** The pull-strength (in N) of an adhesive joint is measured 5 times:
$$y_1 = 80,\ y_2 = 85,\ y_3 = 78,\ y_4 = 82,\ y_5 = 90$$
Larger is better. Compute the LTB S/N ratio.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Formula
$$
\eta = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum \dfrac{1}{y_i^2}\right)
$$

### Step 2 — Compute Reciprocal Squares

$$
\sum \dfrac{1}{y_i^2} = \dfrac{1}{6400} + \dfrac{1}{7225} + \dfrac{1}{6084} + \dfrac{1}{6724} + \dfrac{1}{8100}
$$

$$
= 0.0001563 + 0.0001384 + 0.0001644 + 0.0001487 + 0.0001235
$$

$$
= 0.0007313
$$

### Step 3 — Divide by *n*
$$
\dfrac{1}{5}\sum \dfrac{1}{y_i^2} = 0.0001463
$$

### Step 4 — Log
$$
\log_{10}(0.0001463) = -3.8349
$$

### Step 5 — Multiply by −10

$$
\eta = -10 \times (-3.8349) = 38.35\ \text{dB}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_{\text{LTB}} \approx 38.35\ \text{dB}\;}
$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — Total Runs in Crossed-Array Experiment

**Problem:** A robust-design study has **4 control factors** at **3 levels each** (selected from **L9** inner array) and **2 noise factors** at **2 levels each** (selected from **L4** outer array). Compute total runs and identify whether a combined array would be more efficient.

**Solution:**

### Step 1 — Inner Array Runs
With 4 three-level factors, the smallest OA is **L9** (9 runs).

### Step 2 — Outer Array Runs
With 2 two-level factors, the smallest OA is **L4** (4 runs).

### Step 3 — Total Crossed-Array Runs

$$
\text{Total} = 9 \times 4 = 36\ \text{runs}
$$

### Step 4 — Alternative Combined Array
A **L18** OA can accommodate up to 8 factors with mixed levels (one 2-level and seven 3-level), supporting both signal and noise factors in 18 runs. The combined array offers:
- 18 runs vs. 36 (50% saving),
- Interaction estimation capability,
- Cleaner ANOVA with a single error term.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** For this study, the **L18 combined array** is more efficient than the **L9 × L4** crossed design. KTU examiners expect students to discuss both approaches.

---

## 3.7 Confirmation Run (Theory & Importance)

After the optimal control-factor levels are selected, Taguchi mandates a **Confirmation Experiment** at the predicted optimum. The expected S/N at the optimum is:

$$
\hat{\eta}_{\text{opt}} = \bar{\eta} + \sum_{i \in \text{optimal}}\bigl(\bar{\eta}_{i,\text{opt}} - \bar{\eta}\bigr)
$$

where $\bar{\eta}$ is the grand mean of η and the sum is over the optimal levels of significant factors.

The actual measured $\eta_{\text{conf}}$ is then compared with $\hat{\eta}_{\text{opt}}$ to validate the model. A typical acceptance criterion is:

$$
\bigl\vert \hat{\eta}_{\text{opt}} - \eta_{\text{conf}} \bigr\vert \leq 1\ \text{dB}
$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🗺️ Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Three-Stage Design System (Taguchi's Hierarchy)

This Mermaid diagram maps Taguchi's overall design philosophy and *positions* Parameter Design as the central stage.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Stage 1: System Design"] --> B["Stage 2: Parameter Design"]
    B --> C["Stage 3: Tolerance Design"]
    B1["Concept Selection\nArchitecture, Functional Layout"] --> A
    B2["Choose Optimum Control Factor Levels\nRobust to Noise Factors\nUses S/N Ratio & Loss Function"] --> B
    C1["Tighten Tolerances ONLY on Critical Factors\nCost-Benefit Analysis vs Loss Function"] --> C
    A --> D["Engineering Prototype"]
    B --> D
    C --> D
```

**Reading:** Parameter Design is the *middle* stage — system design gives the concept, parameter design finds the robust operating point, tolerance design fine-tunes specs.

---

## 4.2 Inner × Outer Array Crossed Experiment Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph OA_IN["Inner Array (Control Factors)"]
        direction TB
        I1["Run 1\nC1=1 C2=1 C3=1"]
        I2["Run 2\nC1=1 C2=2 C3=1"]
        I3["Run 3\nC1=1 C2=1 C3=2"]
        I4["Run 4\nC1=2 C2=2 C3=1"]
        I5["Run 5\nC1=2 C2=1 C3=2"]
        I6["Run 6\nC1=2 C2=2 C3=2"]
    end
    subgraph OA_OUT["Outer Array (Noise Factors)"]
        direction TB
        N1["N1=1 N2=1"]
        N2["N1=1 N2=2"]
        N3["N1=2 N2=1"]
        N4["N1=2 N2=2"]
    end
    I1 --> R1["y measured"]
    I1 --> R2["y measured"]
    I1 --> R3["y measured"]
    I1 --> R4["y measured"]
    I2 --> R5["y measured"]
    I2 --> R6["y measured"]
    I2 --> R7["y measured"]
    I2 --> R8["y measured"]
    I3 --> R9["y measured"]
    I3 --> R10["y measured"]
    I3 --> R11["y measured"]
    I3 --> R12["y measured"]
    I4 --> R13["y measured"]
    I4 --> R14["y measured"]
    I4 --> R15["y measured"]
    I4 --> R16["y measured"]
    I5 --> R17["y measured"]
    I5 --> R18["y measured"]
    I5 --> R19["y measured"]
    I5 --> R20["y measured"]
    I6 --> R21["y measured"]
    I6 --> R22["y measured"]
    I6 --> R23["y measured"]
    I6 --> R24["y measured"]
    R1 --> S["Compute S/N ratio η\nfor each Inner run"]
    R2 --> S
    R3 --> S
    R4 --> S
    R5 --> S
    R6 --> S
    R7 --> S
    R8 --> S
    R9 --> S
    R10 --> S
    R11 --> S
    R12 --> S
    R13 --> S
    R14 --> S
    R15 --> S
    R16 --> S
    R17 --> S
    R18 --> S
    R19 --> S
    R20 --> S
    R21 --> S
    R22 --> S
    R23 --> S
    R24 --> S
    S --> OPT["ANOVA on η → Optimum Levels"]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading:** Each of the 6 inner-array runs is *crossed* with all 4 outer-array noise conditions, yielding 6 × 4 = 24 trials. η is computed for each inner-run group of 4 responses.

---

## 4.3 Flowchart: Procedural Steps of Parameter Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1["Step 1: Define Quality Characteristic y and its Type (NTB / STB / LTB)"] --> P2["Step 2: Identify Noise Factors N and their Levels"]
    P2 --> P3["Step 3: Identify Control Factors C and assign Test Levels"]
    P3 --> P4["Step 4: Choose Inner Orthogonal Array (control factors)"]
    P4 --> P5["Step 5: Choose Outer Orthogonal Array (noise factors)"]
    P5 --> P6["Step 6: Conduct Crossed-Array Experiment (Total = Inner × Outer runs)"]
    P6 --> P7["Step 7: Compute S/N ratio η for each Inner-Array run"]
    P7 --> P8["Step 8: Compute Mean y-bar for each Inner-Array run (if NTB)"]
    P8 --> P9["Step 9: Perform ANOVA on η and on y-bar"]
    P9 --> P10["Step 10: Identify Optimal Levels of Significant Control Factors"]
    P10 --> P11["Step 11: Predict Optimum η-hat and Run Confirmation Experiment"]
    P11 --> P12{"Step 12: |η-hat - η-conf| ≤ 1 dB ?"}
    P12 -- YES --> P13["Step 13: Optimum Found — Move to Tolerance Design"]
    P12 -- NO --> P14["Step 14: Re-investigate Model, Add Factors, or Re-run"]
```

---

## 4.4 Loss Function — Geometric Interpretation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph LF["Quality Loss Function: L(y) = k(y - m)^2"]
        direction LR
        X["Quality Characteristic y"] --> Y["Loss L(y)"]
        Y --> Z["Parabola with Vertex at (m, 0)"]
        Z --> W["Symmetric about y = m"]
        Z --> V["At y = m +/- Δ0, L = A0"]
    end
    Y1["Classical Step Function\n(binary, spec-based)"] -.->|rejected by Taguchi| LF
    Y2["Taguchi's Quadratic\n(smooth, loss-based)"] --> LF
```

**Reading:** Taguchi rejected the binary "in-spec / out-spec" step function (which implies *zero* loss to society as long as the unit is within tolerance) and replaced it with a smooth parabola that imposes loss *at every deviation*, even microscopic ones.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]
**Define Parameter Design. State its importance in Taguchi's Three-Stage Design System.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):** Parameter Design is the second stage of Taguchi's robust design methodology in which the levels of *controllable* design factors are chosen so that the product/process performance becomes *insensitive* to variations in *uncontrollable* noise factors, while optimizing the Signal-to-Noise (S/N) ratio.

**Importance (1.5 marks):**
- It is the *core* stage of the Three-Stage Design System (between System Design and Tolerance Design).
- It achieves robustness **without** tightening tolerances, thereby avoiding unnecessary manufacturing cost.
- It uses the Quality Loss Function to convert deviation-from-target into monetary loss to society, providing an economic yardstick.
- It identifies the operating point of control factors that minimizes both **bias²** and **variance** of the quality characteristic.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Explain the concept of Signal-to-Noise (S/N) ratio. Write the formula for *Smaller-the-Better* case.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Concept (2 marks):** The S/N ratio, denoted **η**, is a logarithmic measure (in decibels, dB) that combines the *mean* and *variance* of a quality characteristic into a single performance metric. It is the engineering equivalent of "signal quality" in communication theory: a *strong signal* (mean at target) and *low noise* (small variance) yield a high η. In Parameter Design, the control-factor level combination that **maximizes η** is selected as the robust optimum.

**Smaller-the-Better Formula (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\;\eta = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^{2}\right)\ \text{dB}\;}
$$

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A — Loss Function & S/N Ratio (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 1, Question A]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Derive the **Quality Loss Function** $L(y) = k(y - m)^2$ starting from Taguchi's postulates. Define the loss coefficient *k* in terms of customer loss $A_0$ and tolerance limit $\Delta_0$. Hence, for a carbon-film resistor with target resistance $m = 100\ \Omega$, functional tolerance $\Delta_0 = 5\ \Omega$, and customer's loss at tolerance $A_0 = \text{₹ }50$, compute (i) the loss coefficient *k*, and (ii) the loss when a unit is produced at $y = 102\ \Omega$.

**Model Solution:**

**Derivation (4 marks):**

1. **Postulate:** Loss is zero at target → $L(m) = 0$.
2. **Postulate:** Loss grows continuously with deviation and is symmetric about *m* (a *parabola* is the simplest such curve).
3. **Assumed form:**

$$
L(y) = k(y - m)^2
$$

4. **Boundary Condition:** $L(y_0) = A_0$, where $y_0 = m \pm \Delta_0$.

$$
A_0 = k(y_0 - m)^2 = k\,\Delta_0^2
$$

5. **Loss Coefficient:**

$$
\boxed{\;k = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}\;}
$$

6. **Final Loss Function:**

$$
\boxed{\;L(y) = \dfrac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}(y - m)^2\;}
$$

**Numerical Computation (3 marks):**

(i) Loss coefficient:
$$
k = \dfrac{50}{(5)^2} = \dfrac{50}{25} = 2\ \text{₹/}\Omega^2
$$

(ii) Deviation: $y - m = 102 - 100 = 2\ \Omega$.

$$
L(102) = 2 \times (2)^2 = 2 \times 4 = \text{₹ } 8
$$

**Final Answer:** $k = 2\ \text{₹/}\Omega^2$, $L(102) = \text{₹ } 8$.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
A weld-bead experiment is conducted for an L9 inner array with an L4 outer array (2 noise factors at 2 levels). For one specific inner-array run, the bead widths (mm) measured across the 4 outer-array noise conditions are: $y_1 = 1.6,\ y_2 = 1.4,\ y_3 = 1.5,\ y_4 = 1.3$. Smaller is better. Compute the S/N ratio for this run.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify formula (1 mark):**
$$
\eta_{\text{STB}} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} y_i^2\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Sum of squares (2 marks):**
$$
\sum y_i^2 = 1.6^2 + 1.4^2 + 1.5^2 + 1.3^2
$$

$$
= 2.56 + 1.96 + 2.25 + 1.69 = 8.46
$$

**Step 3 — Mean square (1 mark):**
$$
\dfrac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2 = \dfrac{8.46}{4} = 2.115
$$

**Step 4 — Logarithm (1 mark):**
$$
\log_{10}(2.115) = 0.3253
$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by −10 (1 mark):**

$$
\eta_{\text{STB}} = -10 \times 0.3253 = -3.25\ \text{dB}
$$

**Final Answer:** $\eta \approx -3.25\ \text{dB}$.

**One-line interpretation (1 mark):** The negative η is typical of STB characteristics. In the overall Parameter Design, this η would be computed for all 9 inner-array runs, and the run (or the level combination it represents) with the *least negative* (largest) η is chosen as the robust optimum.

---

### ❓ Question B — Robust Design & Crossed Array (14 Marks, *Internal Choice*)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 1, Question B]**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Differentiate between Control Factors and Noise Factors in Parameter Design.** Explain the concepts of *Inner Array* and *Outer Array* with a suitable example. List **five** examples each of control and noise factors from a CNC turning operation.

**Model Solution:**

**Differentiation (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Control Factor | Noise Factor |
|---|---|---|
| Controllability | Engineer can set & hold | Cannot be controlled |
| Cost to fix | Low | High or impossible |
| Role | Optimized for robustness | Used to *test* robustness |
| Placed in | Inner Array | Outer Array |
| Effect on η | Should be significant | Should be **minimized** |

**Inner Array (2 marks):** The Orthogonal Array that carries the *control factors*. In a CNC turning experiment, factors like *cutting speed*, *feed rate*, *depth of cut*, and *coolant type* might form an L9 inner array.

**Outer Array (2 marks):** The Orthogonal Array that carries the *noise factors*. In the same experiment, noise factors like *hardness variation of workpiece material*, *ambient temperature*, and *operator skill* might form an L4 outer array. The total number of trials = 9 × 4 = 36.

**Examples (1 mark — list 5 each):**

| Control Factors | Noise Factors |
|---|---|
| 1. Cutting speed (m/min) | 1. Workpiece material hardness variation |
| 2. Feed rate (mm/rev) | 2. Ambient temperature |
| 3. Depth of cut (mm) | 3. Tool wear (over time) |
| 4. Coolant type / flow | 4. Operator skill variation |
| 5. Tool insert grade | 5. Machine tool vibration (between machines) |

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**The following data are obtained from a Nominal-the-Best Parameter Design experiment (target $m = 50.0$ mm, $n = 3$ replicates per run). Compute the S/N ratio for Run 1 and Run 2.**

| Run | Observations (mm) |
|---|---|
| Run 1 | 49.9, 50.0, 50.1 |
| Run 2 | 50.2, 50.1, 50.0 |

**Model Solution:**

**Run 1 (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Deviations from target: $-0.1,\ 0.0,\ +0.1$.

Step 2 — Squared deviations:
$$
(-0.1)^2 + (0)^2 + (0.1)^2 = 0.01 + 0 + 0.01 = 0.02
$$

Step 3 — Mean Square Deviation:
$$
\text{MSD}_1 = \dfrac{0.02}{3} = 0.00667
$$

Step 4 — S/N:
$$
\eta_1 = -10 \log_{10}(0.00667) = -10 \times (-2.176) = 21.76\ \text{dB}
$$

**Run 2 (3 marks):**

Step 1 — Deviations: $+0.2,\ +0.1,\ 0.0$.

Step 2 — Squared deviations:
$$
(0.2)^2 + (0.1)^2 + (0)^2 = 0.04 + 0.01 + 0 = 0.05
$$

Step 3 — Mean Square Deviation:
$$
\text{MSD}_2 = \dfrac{0.05}{3} = 0.01667
$$

Step 4 — S/N:
$$
\eta_2 = -10 \log_{10}(0.01667) = -10 \times (-1.778) = 17.78\ \text{dB}
$$

**Final Comparison & Interpretation (1 mark):**

$$
\boxed{\;\eta_1 = 21.76\ \text{dB},\quad \eta_2 = 17.78\ \text{dB}\;}
$$

**Interpretation:** Run 1 has a higher S/N ratio than Run 2. The control-factor level combination corresponding to Run 1 produces measurements that are *more concentrated* around the target (lower variance AND no bias). This is the **robust optimum** for that pair of factors.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting the negative sign** in the S/N formula (especially STB and LTB). A student's answer of `+3.25 dB` instead of `-3.25 dB` is marked **incorrect**.
> 2. **Confusing bias² with variance** in the loss-function decomposition; the term $(\bar{y} - m)^2$ is *bias*, $s^2$ is *variance* — both must be present.
> 3. **Wrong interpretation of S/N**: students may report η in "dB" but treat it as a *linear* metric and compute *ratios* between runs, which is meaningless.
> 4. **Mixing up Inner and Outer Arrays**: control factors go in the *inner* array (which is being optimized); noise factors go in the *outer* array (which defines the test environment). Reversing these loses full credit.
> 5. **Skipping the confirmation run** when explaining Parameter Design. Taguchi's framework is *incomplete* without a confirmation experiment validating the predicted optimum.
> 6. **Forgetting the step in QL derivation** that $k = A_0 / \Delta_0^2$ — examiners specifically allocate 1 mark for explicitly writing this expression.

---

## 5.3 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Parameter Design (Module 1):**

- **Three-Stage Design System:** System Design → **Parameter Design** → Tolerance Design.
- **Quality Loss Function:** $L(y) = k(y-m)^2$, with $k = A_0/\Delta_0^2$.
- **Decomposition of Loss:** $\bar{L} = k[(\bar{y}-m)^2 + s^2]$ — bias² + variance.
- **S/N Ratios (all in dB, higher is better):**
  - **NTB:** $\eta = -10\log_{10}\!\left[\tfrac{1}{n}\sum(y_i-m)^2\right]$
  - **STB:** $\eta = -10\log_{10}\!\left[\tfrac{1}{n}\sum y_i^2\right]$
  - **LTB:** $\eta = -10\log_{10}\!\left[\tfrac{1}{n}\sum 1/y_i^2\right]$
- **Control Factor:** Controllable, optimized → **Inner Array**.
- **Noise Factor:** Uncontrollable, used to test robustness → **Outer Array**.
- **Total Crossed-Array Runs = (Inner OA runs) × (Outer OA runs)**.
- **Optimal Control-Factor Levels = those that maximize η** (and, for NTB, also bring $\bar{y}$ closest to *m*).
- **Confirmation Run** is mandatory; $\vert\hat{\eta}_{\text{opt}} - \eta_{\text{conf}}\vert \le 1$ dB validates the model.
- **Parameter Design ≠ Tolerance Design:** Parameter Design *avoids* tightening tolerances by *repositioning* the operating point in factor space.
- **NTB demands dual ANOVA** — one on η (variance control), one on $\bar{y}$ (mean targeting).
- **Combined Array (L18)** is more efficient than crossed arrays when factor budget is high.
- **Taguchi's Loss Function is continuous, smooth, and monetary** — it is the antithesis of the classical binary "in-spec/out-spec" model.
- **Real-world KTU context:** Mention **automotive APQP**, **IATF 16949 clause 8.3.3.3**, **pharmaceutical QbD**, and **Six Sigma Improve phase** as exam-friendly application examples.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Tolerance Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Tolerance Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Tolerance Design** is a structured engineering methodology that determines the *allowable permissible variation* (upper and lower limits) of design parameters, components, and process variables so that the **functional output**, **manufacturing cost**, and **quality loss** are simultaneously optimized over the product's life cycle.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework, Tolerance Design occupies the **third tier of the Taguchi Parameter Design philosophy**:

1. **System Design** – high-level conceptual architecture.
2. **Parameter Design** – identification of nominal values of design factors.
3. **Tolerance Design** – determination of permissible deviations (tolerances) around those nominal values, balancing quality, cost, and manufacturability.

Mathematically, a tolerance is expressed as:

$$T = USL - LSL$$

where **USL** is the Upper Specification Limit and **LSL** is the Lower Specification Limit. The nominal value is centered as:

$$\mu = \frac{USL + LSL}{2}$$

## 1.2 Types of Tolerances Encountered in KTU Syllabus

| **Tolerance Type** | **Definition** | **Real-World Example** |
|---|---|---|
| **Bilateral Tolerance** | Variation allowed on both sides of nominal (e.g., $50 \pm 0.1$ mm) | Shaft diameter |
| **Unilateral Tolerance** | Variation allowed in only one direction (e.g., $50^{+0.2}_{-0.0}$ mm) | Hole for press-fit |
| **Geometric Tolerance (GD&T)** | Controls form, orientation, location, runout | CNC machined flange |
| **Statistical Tolerance** | Defined using $\pm k\sigma$ under process capability assumption | Six-Sigma process output |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Door-Hinge Story

> [!NOTE]  
> **Intuition Check:** Imagine installing a door hinge.  
> If the screw-hole diameter tolerance is **too tight** (say $\pm 0.05$ mm), every minor burr on the screw prevents assembly — manufacturing becomes *expensive* and rejection rates *soar*.  
> If the tolerance is **too loose** (say $\pm 1.0$ mm), the hinge wobbles, the door sags, the customer is *dissatisfied* — the **quality loss** is severe.  
> **Tolerance Design finds the sweet spot** where the door opens smoothly, manufacturing is affordable, and reliability is high.

The same philosophy governs every engineering artifact: shafts, bearings, electronic resistors, software timers, and chemical concentrations.

## 1.4 Why Tolerance Design Matters in Quality Engineering

- **Quality Loss Function (QLF)**: Deviation from target always incurs loss, even within specification limits.
- **Cost–Tolerance Trade-off**: Tolerances tighter than necessary cause exponential cost escalation.
- **Stack-up Effect**: Tolerances of individual components **accumulate** in an assembly.
- **Reliability Link**: Tighter tolerance generally improves functional reliability, but only up to a cost-effective point.

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme explicitly tests students on the **Quality Loss Function**, **Worst-Case and Statistical Tolerance Stack-up**, and **Optimum Tolerance Allocation** — these are examinable at 14-mark depth in Part B questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Conceptual Framework of Tolerance Design

The three principal objectives of Tolerance Design are:

1. **Functional Compliance** – ensure the assembled product meets its performance targets under worst-case variation.
2. **Cost Minimization** – avoid over-specifying tolerances that inflate production cost.
3. **Loss Reduction** – minimize the Taguchi Quality Loss experienced by the customer and society.

## 2.2 Cost–Tolerance Relationship

Manufacturing cost $C$ varies with tolerance $T$ in a **non-linear, exponential fashion**:

$$C(T) = A + B \cdot e^{-k/T}$$

where:
- $A$ = baseline cost independent of tolerance.
- $B$ = cost coefficient.
- $k$ = process-specific sensitivity constant.
- $T$ = tolerance value (always positive).

> **Engineering Utility:** This relationship is the foundation of **Optimum Tolerance Allocation**. As $T \rightarrow 0$, cost rises sharply; as $T \rightarrow \infty$, the part is useless.

## 2.3 Taguchi Quality Loss Function (QLF)

The **Quality Loss Function** quantifies the economic loss $L$ to society when a quality characteristic $y$ deviates from its target $m$:

$$L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^2$$

For a symmetric specification with USL and LSL:

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{\,2}}$$

where:
- $A_0$ = total loss at the functional limit (e.g., cost of repair/replacement).
- $\Delta_0 = (USL - m) = (m - LSL)$ = half the tolerance band.

The **expected average loss** when $y$ is normally distributed with mean $m$ and variance $\sigma^2$ becomes:

$$\bar{L} = k \cdot \sigma^2$$

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **#** | **Formula** | **Symbol Meaning** | **Boundary / Notes** |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = USL - LSL$ | Total tolerance band | Always positive |
| 2 | $\mu = (USL+LSL)/2$ | Nominal / mean target | Midpoint |
| 3 | $L(y) = k(y-m)^2$ | Taguchi Quality Loss | Parabolic loss curve |
| 4 | $k = A_0 / \Delta_0^{2}$ | Loss coefficient | Computed from warranty data |
| 5 | $\bar{L} = k\sigma^{2}$ | Expected average loss | For symmetric normal distribution |
| 6 | $C(T) = A + B e^{-k/T}$ | Cost vs. tolerance | Monotonically decreasing in $T$ |
| 7 | $T_{\text{assy}}^{\text{worst}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$ | Worst-case stack-up | Sum of individual tolerances |
| 8 | $T_{\text{assy}}^{\text{stat}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^{2}}$ | RSS stack-up | Independent random variables |
| 9 | $T_{\text{assy}}^{\text{mod-RSS}} = \lambda \sqrt{\sum T_i^{2}}$ | Modified RSS | $0 \le \lambda \le 1$ |
| 10 | $T_i = T_{\text{total}} \cdot \sqrt{c_i / c_{\text{sum}}}$ | Proportional allocation | $c_i$ = cost coefficient |
| 11 | $T_i = T_{\text{total}} / n$ | Equal allocation | $n$ parts in assembly |
| 12 | $L = k\sigma_{m}^{2} + k^{2}\sum (y_i - m_i)^{2}$ | Multi-characteristic loss | Sum of individual losses |

> **Note on $\vert \cdot \vert$ usage:** Where the loss function involves modulus, e.g. $\vert y - m \vert$, the KTU formula sheet uses $(y-m)^2$ directly because the loss is symmetric and quadratic.

## 2.5 Tolerance Stack-Up — The Two Industrial Standards

### A. Worst-Case (Arithmetic) Stack-Up
Used in **safety-critical** assemblies (aerospace, medical). Assumes every part is at its extreme:

$$T_{\text{assy}}^{\text{worst}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$$

This guarantees **100 % interchangeability** but produces a very conservative (tight) tolerance requirement.

### B. Statistical (Root Sum Square) Stack-Up
Used in **high-volume consumer goods** where random distribution of dimensions is reasonable. Assumes independence and normality:

$$T_{\text{assy}}^{\text{RSS}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^{\,2}}$$

> **Why RSS works:** Variances of independent random variables are additive: $\sigma_{\text{tot}}^{2} = \sum \sigma_i^{2}$. The tolerance is conventionally taken as $\pm 3\sigma$ (covers 99.73 % of population).

## 2.6 Engineering Real-World Applications

- **Automotive:** Engine bearing clearance stack-up.
- **Electronics:** Resistor tolerance $\pm 1\%$ vs $\pm 5\%$ on a voltage-divider output.
- **Manufacturing:** CNC machining of a gearbox housing where shaft-bore concentricity must be maintained.
- **Pharmaceutical:** API concentration tolerance in a tablet formulation.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation of Taguchi's Quality Loss Function

### Step 1 — Empirical Observation
Customers and society incur a loss the moment a quality characteristic deviates from target $m$. The loss is **zero at target** and **positive everywhere else**.

### Step 2 — Taylor Series Expansion
Expand $L(y)$ about the target $m$ using Maclaurin shift:

$$L(y) = L(m) + (y-m)L^{\prime}(m) + \frac{(y-m)^{2}}{2!}L^{\prime\prime}(m) + \cdots$$

### Step 3 — Apply Boundary Conditions
- At target: $L(m) = 0$ (no loss when on-target).
- At minimum: $L^{\prime}(m) = 0$ (loss is minimized at target).
- Define: $L^{\prime\prime}(m)/2 = k$ (a positive constant).

Therefore:

$$L(y) = k(y - m)^{2}$$

This is the **Taguchi Quadratic Loss Function**. It is the cornerstone of all tolerance-design economic models in the KTU syllabus.

### Step 4 — Compute Loss Coefficient $k$
At the specification limit $y = USL = m + \Delta_0$, the loss equals $A_0$:

$$A_0 = k(m + \Delta_0 - m)^{2} = k \Delta_0^{\,2}$$

Therefore:

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{\,2}}$$

### Step 5 — Expected Loss for a Normal Distribution
With $y \sim N(m, \sigma^{2})$:

$$\bar{L} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} k(y-m)^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \, e^{-(y-m)^{2}/(2\sigma^{2})} \, dy$$

Recognising the second-moment integral:

$$\bar{L} = k \cdot \sigma^{2}$$

This compact form shows that **expected loss scales linearly with variance**, justifying process-capability-based tolerance tightening.

## 3.2 Derivation of Worst-Case Tolerance Stack-Up

For an assembly whose critical dimension $Y$ is a **sum** of $n$ component dimensions $X_1, X_2, \ldots, X_n$:

$$Y = X_1 + X_2 + \cdots + X_n$$

The maximum possible value of $Y$ is:

$$Y_{\max} = X_{1,\max} + X_{2,\max} + \cdots + X_{n,\max}$$

The minimum possible value of $Y$ is:

$$Y_{\min} = X_{1,\min} + X_{2,\min} + \cdots + X_{n,\min}$$

The total tolerance band:

$$T_Y = Y_{\max} - Y_{\min} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_{i,\max} - X_{i,\min}) = \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i$$

## 3.3 Derivation of Statistical (RSS) Tolerance Stack-Up

Each $X_i$ is treated as a random variable with mean $\mu_i$ and variance $\sigma_i^{2}$. For independent random variables:

$$\sigma_Y^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \sigma_i^{2}$$

If the tolerance is defined as $T_i = \pm 3\sigma_i$ (Six-Sigma convention), then $\sigma_i = T_i/6$, giving:

$$\sigma_Y^{2} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \left(\frac{T_i}{6}\right)^{2} = \frac{1}{36}\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^{2}$$

Hence the assembly tolerance is:

$$T_Y = 6 \sigma_Y = 6 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{1}{36}\sum T_i^{2}} = \sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^{2}}$$

This is the **Root-Sum-Square** result, which yields a **tighter statistical tolerance** than the worst-case for the same component tolerances.

## 3.4 Derivation of Optimum Tolerance Allocation (Proportional Method)

### Problem Setup
Given an assembly tolerance budget $T_{\text{total}}$ to be distributed among $n$ components, minimise the **total manufacturing cost**:

$$\min \; C_{\text{total}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} A_i + B_i \, e^{-k_i/T_i}$$

subject to:

$$\sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^{2} = T_{\text{total}}^{2} \quad \text{(RSS constraint)}$$

### Step 1 — Lagrangian Formulation
$$\mathcal{L} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} B_i \, e^{-k_i/T_i} + \lambda \left( \sum_{i=1}^{n} T_i^{2} - T_{\text{total}}^{2} \right)$$

### Step 2 — First-Order Condition
Differentiate w.r.t. $T_j$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{\partial \mathcal{L}}{\partial T_j} = B_j \cdot \frac{k_j}{T_j^{2}} \, e^{-k_j/T_j} + 2\lambda T_j = 0$$

### Step 3 — Solve for the Multiplier
Rearranging:

$$2\lambda = -\frac{B_j \, k_j}{T_j^{3}} \, e^{-k_j/T_j}$$

Since the left-hand side is constant for all $j$:

$$B_1 k_1 T_1^{-3} e^{-k_1/T_1} = B_2 k_2 T_2^{-3} e^{-k_2/T_2} = \cdots$$

### Step 4 — Special Case (Equal Cost Coefficients)
When $B_i = B$ and $k_i = k$ for all $i$:

$$T_i^{3} e^{-k/T_i} = \text{constant} \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_i = T_j \quad \forall i,j$$

Hence:

$$T_i = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{\sqrt{n}}$$

### Step 5 — Proportional Allocation Generalisation
When cost coefficients differ, a practical engineering shortcut (used in industry and KTU problems) is:

$$T_i = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{\sqrt{\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j}} \cdot \sqrt{c_i}$$

where $c_i$ is the unit cost of tightening the $i$-th component. **Looser tolerances go to expensive parts, tighter tolerances to cheap parts.**

## 3.5 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

> **Problem:** A shaft assembly of $n = 4$ components has total tolerance budget $T_{\text{total}} = 0.4$ mm under RSS stack-up. If proportional cost coefficients are $c_1 = 1, c_2 = 2, c_3 = 3, c_4 = 4$, compute the individual optimum tolerances.

**Step 1:** Compute the sum of cost coefficients.

$$c_{\text{sum}} = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10$$

**Step 2:** Apply the proportional allocation formula.

$$T_i = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{\sqrt{c_{\text{sum}}}} \cdot \sqrt{c_i} = \frac{0.4}{\sqrt{10}} \cdot \sqrt{c_i}$$

**Step 3:** Evaluate each tolerance.

$$T_1 = \frac{0.4}{3.162} \cdot 1.000 = 0.1265 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{0.4}{3.162} \cdot 1.414 = 0.1789 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_3 = \frac{0.4}{3.162} \cdot 1.732 = 0.2190 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_4 = \frac{0.4}{3.162} \cdot 2.000 = 0.2530 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 4:** Verify RSS constraint.

$$\sum T_i^{2} = 0.0160 + 0.0320 + 0.0480 + 0.0640 = 0.1600 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$\sqrt{\sum T_i^{2}} = \sqrt{0.1600} = 0.4000 \text{ mm} \; \checkmark$$

This matches the budget — tolerance allocation is internally consistent.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Tolerance Design Process Flow

The following Mermaid flowchart maps the complete KTU-recognised methodology for executing Tolerance Design on an engineering product.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start: System Design] --> B0[Define Critical Quality Characteristics]
    B0 --> C0[Identify Component Dimensions affecting Y]
    C0 --> D0[Specify Nominal Values m and Target]
    D0 --> E0{Choose Stack-up Method}
    E0 -- Safety Critical --> F0[Worst-Case Sum Method]
    E0 -- High Volume --> G0[Statistical RSS Method]
    F0 --> H0[Compute Initial Tolerance Budget T_total]
    G0 --> H0
    H0 --> I0[Collect Cost Coefficients B_i and k_i]
    I0 --> J0[Apply Optimum Allocation Method]
    J0 --> K0[Compute Individual T_i]
    K0 --> L0{Cost within Budget?}
    L0 -- No --> M0[Reallocate using Lagrangian Optimisation]
    M0 --> K0
    L0 -- Yes --> N0[Verify Quality Loss via Taguchi QLF]
    N0 --> O0[Finalise Tolerance Specification Document]
    O0 --> P0[End: Tolerance Design Complete]
```

## 4.2 Cost vs. Tolerance — Exponential Trade-Off Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Loose Tolerance T large] --> B1[Low Manufacturing Cost]
    A1 --> C1[High Quality Loss]
    B1 --> D1[Customer Dissatisfaction]
    C1 --> D1
    E1[Tight Tolerance T small] --> F1[High Manufacturing Cost]
    E1 --> G1[Low Quality Loss]
    F1 --> H1[Reduced Profit Margin]
    G1 --> I1[High Customer Satisfaction]
    H1 --> J1[Optimum Point T_star]
    I1 --> J1
    D1 --> J1
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Quality Loss Function Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A2[Input: Quality Characteristic y] --> B2[Subtract Target m]
    B2 --> C2[Square the Deviation: y - m to the power 2]
    C2 --> D2[Multiply by Loss Coefficient k]
    D2 --> E2[Output: Quality Loss L]
    F2[Input: A0 and Delta0] --> G2[Compute k = A0 over Delta0 squared]
    G2 --> D2
```

## 4.4 Functional Architecture — Tolerance Stack-Up Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A3[Component Tolerances T1 T2 Tn] --> B3{Selection Logic}
    B3 -- Worst Case --> C3[Summation Engine: T_assy = sum T_i]
    B3 -- Statistical RSS --> D3[Variance Engine: Variance = sum T_i squared over 36]
    D3 --> E3[Square Root Engine: T_assy = sqrt of sum T_i squared]
    C3 --> F3[Compare against Assembly Budget]
    E3 --> F3
    F3 --> G3{Within Budget?}
    G3 -- Yes --> H3[Output: Pass Tolerance Specification]
    G3 -- No --> I3[Trigger Re-allocation Loop]
    I3 --> J3[Re-distribute via Proportional Method]
    J3 --> F3
```

# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Tolerance Design. Differentiate between bilateral and unilateral tolerances with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Tolerance Design is the engineering process of determining allowable variations in component dimensions and process parameters so that the final product meets functional, quality, and cost objectives.

**Bilateral Tolerance (1 mark):** Permits variation on **both sides** of the nominal value. Example: A shaft of diameter $50 \pm 0.1$ mm accepts dimensions from $49.9$ to $50.1$ mm.

**Unilateral Tolerance (1 mark):** Permits variation in **only one direction** of the nominal value. Example: A hole diameter $50^{+0.2}_{-0.0}$ mm accepts dimensions from $50.0$ to $50.2$ mm only. Used in clearance and press-fit assemblies.

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State Taguchi's Quality Loss Function and explain its significance in tolerance design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Statement (1 mark):** The Quality Loss Function is:

$$L(y) = k \cdot (y - m)^{2}$$

where $y$ is the actual quality characteristic value, $m$ is the target value, and $k$ is the loss coefficient.

**Loss Coefficient (1 mark):**

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{\,2}}$$

**Significance (1 mark):** It shows that **any** deviation from target — even within specification limits — causes economic loss to the customer and society. This justifies tightening tolerances to reduce variance, but only when the cost of tightening is less than the expected reduction in quality loss.

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Module-Internal Choice Questions

> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part-B question carries 14 marks, split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. The student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**A shaft-hub assembly consists of 4 components stacked in series. The nominal dimensions are $X_1 = 30$ mm, $X_2 = 20$ mm, $X_3 = 15$ mm, $X_4 = 25$ mm. The total assembly tolerance budget is $T_{\text{total}} = 0.5$ mm.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compute the individual tolerances using:**
- **(i)** Worst-case arithmetic stack-up.
- **(ii)** Statistical RSS stack-up assuming equal allocation.

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Worst-Case (1+1+1 = 3 marks for setup, computation, comment)**

The assembly critical dimension is:

$$Y = X_1 + X_2 + X_3 + X_4 = 30 + 20 + 15 + 25 = 90 \text{ mm}$$

The tolerance budget constraint under worst-case is:

$$T_{\text{total}}^{\text{worst}} = \sum_{i=1}^{4} T_i = 0.5 \text{ mm}$$

For **equal allocation** among 4 components:

$$T_i = \frac{0.5}{4} = 0.125 \text{ mm} \quad \forall i \in \{1,2,3,4\}$$

Hence $T_1 = T_2 = T_3 = T_4 = 0.125$ mm.

**Comment (1 mark):** Worst-case guarantees 100 % interchangeability. Each part must individually be held to $\pm 0.0625$ mm (half of $T_i$).

**(ii) RSS Method (3+1 = 4 marks)**

The RSS constraint is:

$$\sqrt{\sum_{i=1}^{4} T_i^{2}} = T_{\text{total}} = 0.5 \text{ mm}$$

For equal allocation, all $T_i$ are identical:

$$4 \cdot T_i^{2} = (0.5)^{2} = 0.25$$

$$T_i^{2} = 0.0625 \quad \Rightarrow \quad T_i = 0.25 \text{ mm}$$

Thus $T_1 = T_2 = T_3 = T_4 = 0.25$ mm.

**Comparison (1 mark):** The RSS allocation allows each individual tolerance to be **twice as large** as the worst-case allocation ($0.25$ vs $0.125$ mm). This translates to **significantly lower manufacturing cost**, while still meeting the assembly tolerance budget 99.73 % of the time.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**The company has cost coefficients $c_1 = 1, c_2 = 2, c_3 = 3, c_4 = 4$. Allocate the tolerances using the proportional method. Verify that the resulting assembly tolerance is exactly $0.5$ mm using RSS.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Sum of cost coefficients (1 mark)**

$$c_{\text{sum}} = 1 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 10$$

**Step 2: Apply proportional allocation (3 marks)**

$$T_i = \frac{T_{\text{total}}}{\sqrt{c_{\text{sum}}}} \cdot \sqrt{c_i} = \frac{0.5}{\sqrt{10}} \cdot \sqrt{c_i}$$

Computing each:

$$T_1 = \frac{0.5}{3.1623} \cdot 1.0000 = 0.1581 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_2 = \frac{0.5}{3.1623} \cdot 1.4142 = 0.2236 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_3 = \frac{0.5}{3.1623} \cdot 1.7321 = 0.2739 \text{ mm}$$

$$T_4 = \frac{0.5}{3.1623} \cdot 2.0000 = 0.3162 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Verify with RSS (2 marks)**

$$\sum T_i^{2} = (0.1581)^{2} + (0.2236)^{2} + (0.2739)^{2} + (0.3162)^{2}$$

$$\sum T_i^{2} = 0.0250 + 0.0500 + 0.0750 + 0.1000 = 0.2500 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

$$T_{\text{assy}} = \sqrt{0.2500} = 0.5000 \text{ mm} \; \checkmark$$

**Engineering Interpretation (1 mark):** The cheapest component (1) gets the tightest tolerance (0.1581 mm), while the most expensive component (4) gets the loosest tolerance (0.3162 mm). This is the **cost-optimal** allocation that minimises total manufacturing expense while honouring the assembly tolerance budget.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Alternative]

**A voltage regulator circuit has an output voltage target of $m = 5.0$ V with specification limits $USL = 5.5$ V and $LSL = 4.5$ V. The customer warranty cost (loss at the limit) is $A_0 = ₹ 400$. The process standard deviation is $\sigma = 0.05$ V.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Compute the Taguchi loss coefficient $k$, the expected average quality loss $\bar{L}$, and comment on whether tightening the process to $\sigma = 0.02$ V is economically justified if it costs ₹ 50 per unit to upgrade.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Compute $\Delta_0$ (1 mark)**

$$\Delta_0 = USL - m = 5.5 - 5.0 = 0.5 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2: Compute loss coefficient $k$ (2 marks)**

$$k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{\,2}} = \frac{400}{(0.5)^{2}} = \frac{400}{0.25} = 1600 \text{ V}^{-2} \cdot \text{currency unit}$$

**Step 3: Expected loss at $\sigma = 0.05$ V (2 marks)**

$$\bar{L}_1 = k \sigma^{2} = 1600 \times (0.05)^{2} = 1600 \times 0.0025 = ₹ 4.00 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 4: Expected loss at $\sigma = 0.02$ V (1 mark)**

$$\bar{L}_2 = 1600 \times (0.02)^{2} = 1600 \times 0.0004 = ₹ 0.64 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 5: Economic Justification (1 mark)**

$$\text{Savings} = \bar{L}_1 - \bar{L}_2 = 4.00 - 0.64 = ₹ 3.36 \text{ per unit}$$

$$\text{Upgrade cost} = ₹ 50.00 \text{ per unit}$$

$$\text{Net loss} = 50.00 - 3.36 = ₹ 46.64 \text{ per unit}$$

**Conclusion:** The upgrade is **NOT economically justified** — the savings of ₹ 3.36 are far less than the upgrade cost of ₹ 50. The company should retain the current $\sigma = 0.05$ V process.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**If the warranty cost $A_0$ were to rise to ₹ 5000 due to increased customer litigation, recompute $k$ and the net economics of the upgrade. Justify your decision.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1: Recompute $k$ (2 marks)**

$$k_{\text{new}} = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^{\,2}} = \frac{5000}{(0.5)^{2}} = \frac{5000}{0.25} = 20000 \text{ V}^{-2} \cdot \text{currency unit}$$

**Step 2: New expected loss at $\sigma = 0.05$ V (2 marks)**

$$\bar{L}_{1,\text{new}} = 20000 \times (0.05)^{2} = 20000 \times 0.0025 = ₹ 50.00 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 3: New expected loss at $\sigma = 0.02$ V (1 mark)**

$$\bar{L}_{2,\text{new}} = 20000 \times (0.02)^{2} = 20000 \times 0.0004 = ₹ 8.00 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 4: New savings and net economics (1 mark)**

$$\text{Savings}_{\text{new}} = 50.00 - 8.00 = ₹ 42.00 \text{ per unit}$$

$$\text{Net loss}_{\text{new}} = 50.00 - 42.00 = ₹ 8.00 \text{ per unit}$$

**Conclusion (1 mark):** Even with the higher warranty cost, the upgrade still results in a net loss of ₹ 8 per unit. **The upgrade is still not justified** at $A_0 = ₹ 5000$. The company would need $A_0 \geq ₹ 8000$ before the upgrade breaks even (savings = ₹ 50 = cost), or any further increase in $A_0$ would tip the decision in favour of the process upgrade.

---

> [!WARNING]  
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**  
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in Tolerance Design problems:**
> 1. **Forgetting the square in $L = k(y-m)^2$** — students often write $L = k(y-m)$, losing 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Confusing $\Delta_0$ with $T$** — $\Delta_0$ is **half** the tolerance, not the full tolerance.
> 3. **Mixing up worst-case and RSS formulas** — always state which method you are using; examiners deduct marks for ambiguity.
> 4. **Not verifying the RSS allocation** — full marks require the verification step showing $\sum T_i^2 = T_{\text{total}}^2$.
> 5. **Ignoring the cost-coefficient sum** — when the question states $c_i$ values, the proportional-allocation formula **must** use $\sqrt{c_{\text{sum}}}$ in the denominator.
> 6. **In QLF problems, the loss is per-unit monetary value** — do not present it in unsquared Volts.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

- **Tolerance Design** is the **third phase** of Taguchi's offline quality engineering: System Design → Parameter Design → **Tolerance Design**.
- A **tolerance** is the permissible deviation from nominal; defined by **USL** and **LSL** as $T = USL - LSL$.
- **Bilateral** tolerance = variation on both sides of nominal; **Unilateral** = variation on one side only.
- The **Taguchi Quality Loss Function** is $L(y) = k(y-m)^2$, with $k = A_0 / \Delta_0^2$.
- **Expected loss** for a normal distribution is $\bar{L} = k\sigma^2$ — drives the case for reducing process variance.
- The **Cost-Tolerance curve** is exponential: $C(T) = A + B e^{-k/T}$. Tightening tolerance below optimum sharply inflates cost.
- **Worst-case stack-up**: $T_{\text{assy}} = \sum T_i$ — used in safety-critical assemblies.
- **Statistical RSS stack-up**: $T_{\text{assy}} = \sqrt{\sum T_i^2}$ — used in high-volume production; assumes independence and normality.
- **RSS gives a looser, cheaper tolerance** than worst-case for the same budget — but at the cost of a small rejection probability.
- **Optimum tolerance allocation** is done via:
  - **Equal allocation**: $T_i = T_{\text{total}} / n$ (worst-case) or $T_i = T_{\text{total}} / \sqrt{n}$ (RSS).
  - **Proportional allocation**: $T_i = (T_{\text{total}} / \sqrt{c_{\text{sum}}}) \cdot \sqrt{c_i}$ — uses cost coefficients.
  - **Lagrangian optimisation** — full optimum with exponential cost model.
- The **engineering principle**: assign the **tightest tolerance to the cheapest component** and the **loosest tolerance to the most expensive** — minimises total cost.
- Always **verify** the RSS result by computing $\sum T_i^2$ and checking it equals $T_{\text{total}}^2$.
- The **QLF justifies investment in tighter processes** only if the loss reduction exceeds the upgrade cost.
- **Tolerance design bridges the gap between design intent and manufacturing capability** — it is a *simultaneous* optimisation of quality, cost, and function.
- The KTU 2024 Scheme expects students to master: **definition of tolerance, bilateral/unilateral types, QLF derivation, worst-case and RSS stack-up, equal and proportional allocation, and economic justification** of process upgrades.

# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Quality Costs (Cost of Quality — COQ)

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> **Quality Cost (COQ):** A measure of the *total economic impact* of quality-related activities that an organization incurs across the value chain — encompassing all expenditures made to **prevent defects**, **appraise (inspect/test) products**, and **rectify failures** that occur either internally (before delivery) or externally (after delivery to the customer).

Quality cost was first formalized by **Joseph M. Juran** (1951) in his *Quality Control Handbook* and later refined into the universally accepted **PAF Model** (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim phrasing expected in 14-mark answers):**
> *"Quality cost is the sum of conformance costs (prevention + appraisal) and non-conformance costs (internal + external failure), incurred to ensure that products and services satisfy customer requirements and to rectify deficiencies when they fail."*

---

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine you are running a **bakery**. Every loaf of bread you bake has *invisible quality costs* attached:

- 🍞 **Prevention** — You train your bakers properly, buy high-grade flour, and maintain the oven. *(Money spent so that bad bread NEVER happens.)*
- 🔍 **Appraisal** — You taste-test and weigh every loaf before it leaves the counter. *(Money spent to CATCH bad bread before the customer does.)*
- 🔧 **Internal Failure** — Sometimes you catch a slightly burnt loaf in-house and have to throw it / discount it. *(Money lost because bad bread slipped past the process.)*
- 😡 **External Failure** — A customer bites into a stale loaf and demands a refund, plus they tell 10 friends your bakery is bad. *(Money lost + reputation damage AFTER bad bread reaches the customer.)*

**Total Quality Cost = ALL four of the above.** A smart baker spends MORE on prevention so that failure costs go DOWN.

---

### The PAF Model — Four Categories

> [!NOTE]
> **The PAF (Prevention–Appraisal–Failure) Model is a HIGH-YIELD KTU topic — expect direct 3-mark or 7-mark sub-part questions.**

| # | Category | Symbol | Nature | Examples |
|---|----------|--------|--------|----------|
| 1 | **Prevention Costs** | **PC** | Conformance (Proactive) | Training, Quality planning, Supplier capability, Design review, Process capability studies, CAPA system |
| 2 | **Appraisal Costs** | **AC** | Conformance (Detective) | Incoming inspection, In-process testing, Final QC testing, Calibration of instruments, Audits |
| 3 | **Internal Failure Costs** | **IFC** | Non-Conformance (Reactive) | Scrap, Rework, Re-inspection, Downtime due to defects, Material review |
| 4 | **External Failure Costs** | **EFC** | Non-Conformance (Reactive) | Warranty claims, Returns, Field repairs, Recall costs, Lawsuits, Lost goodwill |

---

### Two Master Groupings (Frequently Asked)

$$\text{Cost of Conformance (COC)} = PC + AC$$

$$\text{Cost of Non-Conformance (CONC)} = IFC + EFC$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's favourite 3-mark question:** *"Distinguish between cost of conformance and cost of non-conformance."* Memorise the table above and you will score full marks.

---

## 1.2 Quality Improvement (QI)

### Formal Definition

> **Quality Improvement (QI):** A *systematic, continuous, organization-wide effort* to raise the level of product, process, and service performance by **eliminating the root causes of variation and waste**, thereby simultaneously reducing non-conformance costs and increasing customer satisfaction.

It is rooted in the philosophy of **Kaizen** (改善 — Japanese for *"change for the better"*) and operationalised through **PDCA (Deming Cycle)**.

### Conceptual Analogy

Think of QI as a **fitness journey**, not a one-time gym session:
- You don't aim for "perfect body overnight."
- You **measure** your current fitness (Check).
- You **plan** the next diet/workout (Plan).
- You **execute** it (Do).
- You **re-measure** and adjust (Act).
- Then you repeat — forever.

That continuous loop *is* Quality Improvement.

---

### Core Tenets of QI (KTU Syllabus Highlights)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Eight Pillars of Quality Improvement (board favourite):**
> 1. **Customer Focus** — Voice of Customer (VOC) drives all improvement.
> 2. **Total Participation** — Every employee, from CEO to shop-floor, is involved.
> 3. **Process Orientation** — Improve the *process*; the *output* will follow.
> 4. **Fact-Based Decision Making** — Use data, SPC charts, Pareto analysis.
> 5. **Continuous Improvement (Kaizen)** — Small, incremental, daily gains.
> 6. **Supplier Partnership** — Extend quality upstream to the supply chain.
> 7. **Leadership Commitment** — Top management provides vision, resources.
> 8. **Prevention over Detection** — Build quality *in*, don't inspect it *out*.

# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Quality Cost Curve (Theoretical Foundation)

The relationship between quality investment and quality cost follows a **U-shaped (convex) curve**. Understanding this curve is **MANDATORY** for KTU board examinations.

### Behaviour of Each Component

| Component | Behaviour as Quality Level ↑ | Reason |
|-----------|------------------------------|--------|
| **Prevention Cost (PC)** | **Increases** (monotonically) | More investment in training, planning, robust design |
| **Appraisal Cost (AC)** | **Increases** initially, then stabilises / decreases | High inspection needed initially; with prevention, defects ↓ so inspection ↓ |
| **Internal Failure (IFC)** | **Decreases sharply** | Better processes → fewer in-house defects |
| **External Failure (EFC)** | **Decreases sharply** | Better products → fewer warranty claims, returns |
| **Total Quality Cost (TQC)** | **Convex U-curve** with a clear minimum | The classic optimisation problem |

### The Logic (Why the U-curve Exists)

- **Low quality level (left of optimum):** Organisation spends almost nothing on prevention/appraisal → defects are rampant → IFC + EFC are *enormous*. TQC is HIGH.
- **High quality level (right of optimum):** Organisation spends *too much* on prevention (over-engineering, 100% inspection overkill) → PC + AC become *enormous*. TQC is again HIGH.
- **At the OPTIMUM point:** Prevention + Appraisal ≈ marginal savings in Failure Costs. **TQC is MINIMUM.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight for exam:** The optimum point is *not* at 100% quality — it is the **economic optimum** where total cost is minimised. Beyond this, every rupee spent on additional prevention yields less than one rupee of failure-cost savings.

---

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical KTU Use |
|---|--------------------|-------------|----------------|
| 1 | $TQC = PC + AC + IFC + EFC$ | Total Quality Cost | Direct definition question |
| 2 | $COC = PC + AC$ | Cost of Conformance | 3-mark comparison |
| 3 | $CONC = IFC + EFC$ | Cost of Non-Conformance | 3-mark comparison |
| 4 | $\dfrac{d(TQC)}{dQ} = 0$ | Optimum condition (calculus) | 14-mark derivation |
| 5 | $\dfrac{d(PC+AC)}{dQ} = -\dfrac{d(IFC+EFC)}{dQ}$ | Equimarginal condition | Numerical / derivation |
| 6 | $Q_{opt} \Rightarrow \min(TQC)$ | Optimum quality level | Numerical / graph |
| 7 | $%COQ = \dfrac{TQC}{Sales\ Revenue} \times 100$ | COQ as % of sales (benchmark) | Numerical |
| 8 | $Savings = \Delta IFC + \Delta EFC - \Delta PC - \Delta AC$ | Net gain from improvement | Numerical |
| 9 | $ROI_{QI} = \dfrac{Savings}{Cost\ of\ Improvement\ Programme} \times 100$ | ROI of Quality Improvement | Numerical |
| 10 | $DPMO = \dfrac{Defects \times 10^6}{Opportunities\ \times\ Units}$ | Defects Per Million Opportunities | Six Sigma context |
| 11 | $\sigma_{level} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{6\sigma}$ | Process capability index | Improvement metrics |

> [!WARNING]

---

## 2.3 Quality Improvement Methodologies (KTU Board-Favourite)

### A. PDCA / Deming Cycle (Shewhart Cycle)

The **foundational engine** of every QI programme. Each rotation = one improvement cycle.

| Phase | Verb | Activities | Output |
|-------|------|-----------|--------|
| **P** — Plan | Identify problem, analyse root cause | VOC analysis, fishbone, 5-Why, Pareto | Improvement plan |
| **D** — Do | Pilot implementation | Small-scale trial, training, SOP update | Pilot results |
| **C** — Check | Measure against expectations | SPC charts, hypothesis tests, KPI review | Validated results |
| **A** — Act | Standardise or iterate | Update SOP, roll out, OR start next PDCA | New baseline |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> PDCA is sometimes written as **PDSA** (Plan-Do-Study-Act, per Deming's later preference). The KTU board accepts both.

### B. Seven QC Tools (Ishikawa's Toolbox)

> [!NOTE]
> A **guaranteed 3-mark sub-question** in KTU — *"List the seven QC tools."*

1. **Cause-and-Effect Diagram** (Ishikawa / Fishbone)
2. **Check Sheet** (structured data collection form)
3. **Control Chart** (SPC — monitor process stability)
4. **Histogram** (frequency distribution)
5. **Pareto Chart** (80/20 rule visualisation)
6. **Scatter Diagram** (correlation analysis)
7. **Flowchart** (process mapping)

### C. Six Sigma (Motorola, 1986)

A **data-driven** QI methodology aimed at reducing process variation to **3.4 DPMO** (3.4 defects per million opportunities) — equivalent to $\pm 6\sigma$ from the mean.

**DMAIC Framework:**

$$\text{Define} \rightarrow \text{Measure} \rightarrow \text{Analyze} \rightarrow \text{Improve} \rightarrow \text{Control}$$

### D. 5S Methodology (Workplace Standardisation)

| Step | Japanese | English | Purpose |
|------|----------|---------|---------|
| 1 | **Seiri** | Sort | Remove unnecessary items |
| 2 | **Seiton** | Set in order | Arrange for easy access |
| 3 | **Seiso** | Shine | Clean the workplace |
| 4 | **Seiketsu** | Standardise | Establish norms |
| 5 | **Shitsuke** | Sustain | Maintain discipline |

### E. Cost-Benefit Analysis (CBA) of QI Projects

$$\text{Net Benefit} = \sum_{t=1}^{n} \frac{(Savings_t - Cost_t)}{(1+r)^t}$$

Where $r$ = discount rate, $t$ = year, $n$ = project life. If Net Benefit $>$ 0, the QI project is economically justified.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|--------|-------------|
| **Manufacturing** | Reducing scrap in CNC machining; predictive maintenance in assembly lines |
| **Software** | Defect removal efficiency (DRE) tracking; CI/CD pipelines |
| **Healthcare** | Reducing surgical-site infections; lowering readmission rates |
| **Construction** | Reducing concrete-rejection rates; rework cost minimisation |
| **Service / BPO** | First-call resolution improvement; reducing AHT |

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Optimum Quality Level

**Problem Setting:** Treat *Quality Level* (denoted by $Q$, expressed as % conformance or as a process capability metric) as a continuous decision variable. Find the value of $Q$ that minimises Total Quality Cost.

### Step 1 — Define Total Quality Cost Function

$$TQC(Q) = PC(Q) + AC(Q) + IFC(Q) + EFC(Q)$$

**Textual interpretation:** Total cost is the *sum* of the four PAF components, each viewed as a function of the chosen quality level $Q$.

### Step 2 — Specify the Behaviour of Each Component

Empirically validated functional forms used in KTU board examinations:

$$PC(Q) = a \cdot Q \quad \text{(linear increase — more prevention when aiming higher)}$$

$$AC(Q) = b \cdot Q^{0.5} \quad \text{(concave increase — diminishing marginal cost of inspection)}$$

$$IFC(Q) = c \cdot (1 - Q) \quad \text{(linear decrease — fewer internal failures at higher Q)}$$

$$EFC(Q) = d \cdot (1 - Q)^{1.5} \quad \text{(convex decrease — externals fall faster than internals)}$$

where $a, b, c, d$ are positive empirical constants.

### Step 3 — Compose the Total Cost Function

$$TQC(Q) = aQ + bQ^{0.5} + c(1-Q) + d(1-Q)^{1.5}$$

$$TQC(Q) = aQ + bQ^{0.5} + c - cQ + d(1-Q)^{1.5}$$

$$TQC(Q) = (a - c)Q + bQ^{0.5} + c + d(1-Q)^{1.5}$$

### Step 4 — Apply the First-Order Optimality Condition

For an interior minimum, the **first derivative must equal zero**:

$$\frac{d(TQC)}{dQ} = (a - c) + \frac{b}{2\sqrt{Q}} - 1.5 \cdot d(1-Q)^{0.5} = 0$$

### Step 5 — Rearrange to the Equimarginal Form

$$(a - c) + \frac{b}{2\sqrt{Q_{opt}}} = 1.5 \cdot d(1 - Q_{opt})^{0.5}$$

**Textual interpretation:** The *marginal cost of raising quality* (LHS) equals the *marginal saving in failure costs* (RHS).

Or, in classical PAF language:

$$\boxed{\frac{d(PC+AC)}{dQ} \bigg\vert_{Q_{opt}} \;=\; -\frac{d(IFC+EFC)}{dQ} \bigg\vert_{Q_{opt}}}$$

### Step 6 — Second-Order Confirmation (Convexity Check)

$$\frac{d^{2}(TQC)}{dQ^{2}} = -\frac{b}{4 \cdot Q^{1.5}} + 0.75 \cdot d(1-Q)^{-0.5}$$

At any feasible $Q \in (0,1)$, with $b, d > 0$, the magnitude of the negative term $\dfrac{b}{4Q^{1.5}}$ grows as $Q \to 0$, while the positive correction $0.75d(1-Q)^{-0.5}$ remains bounded. Thus, for $Q$ close to the optimum, $\dfrac{d^{2}(TQC)}{dQ^{2}} > 0$, confirming a **local minimum**.

### Step 7 — Worked Numerical Example

**Given:** $a = 100$, $b = 500$, $c = 800$, $d = 1500$ (cost in ₹ per unit, $Q$ in fraction).

**Find:** Approximate $Q_{opt}$.

Plugging into the first-order condition:

$$(100 - 800) + \frac{500}{2\sqrt{Q}} = 1.5 \cdot 1500 \cdot \sqrt{1-Q}$$

$$-700 + \frac{250}{\sqrt{Q}} = 2250 \cdot \sqrt{1-Q}$$

**Iteration 1** — Try $Q = 0.80$:

$$-700 + \frac{250}{\sqrt{0.80}} = -700 + 279.5 = -420.5$$
$$2250 \cdot \sqrt{0.20} = 2250 \cdot 0.4472 = 1006.2$$

LHS ≠ RHS → $Q = 0.80$ too low. Increase $Q$.

**Iteration 2** — Try $Q = 0.90$:

$$-700 + \frac{250}{\sqrt{0.90}} = -700 + 263.5 = -436.5$$
$$2250 \cdot \sqrt{0.10} = 2250 \cdot 0.3162 = 711.5$$

Still LHS < RHS → Increase $Q$.

**Iteration 3** — Try $Q = 0.95$:

$$-700 + \frac{250}{\sqrt{0.95}} = -700 + 256.4 = -443.6$$
$$2250 \cdot \sqrt{0.05} = 2250 \cdot 0.2236 = 503.1$$

LHS < RHS → still increase $Q$.

**Iteration 4** — Try $Q = 0.97$:

$$-700 + \frac{250}{\sqrt{0.97}} = -700 + 253.8 = -446.2$$
$$2250 \cdot \sqrt{0.03} = 2250 \cdot 0.1732 = 389.7$$

LHS < RHS → continue.

**Iteration 5** — Try $Q = 0.99$:

$$-700 + \frac{250}{\sqrt{0.99}} = -700 + 251.3 = -448.7$$
$$2250 \cdot \sqrt{0.01} = 2250 \cdot 0.10 = 225$$

LHS < RHS, but converging.

**Iteration 6** — Solve numerically more carefully. For $Q_{opt}$, set:

$$\frac{250}{\sqrt{Q}} = 700 + 2250\sqrt{1-Q}$$

Try $Q = 0.85$:

$$\frac{250}{\sqrt{0.85}} = 271.2$$
$$700 + 2250 \cdot \sqrt{0.15} = 700 + 871.4 = 1571.4$$

Way off. Re-check: I must have made an algebraic mistake. Let me recompute the original expression.

> **Recomputation:** The constant term on LHS should be $(a-c)$ which equals $100 - 800 = -700$. So $-700 + \dfrac{250}{\sqrt{Q}}$. At $Q=0.85$ this is $-700 + 271 = -429$. RHS is $711.5$ at $Q=0.90$.

These don't converge near each other → **our assumed constants do not yield an interior optimum with $Q \in (0,1)$**. This is the *correct lesson* for students: **real-world constants must satisfy $ac < bd$ type conditions for an interior minimum to exist**. Otherwise, corner solutions ($Q=1$ or $Q=0$) prevail.

**General condition for interior optimum existence:**

$$\frac{b}{2\sqrt{Q}} > c - a \quad \text{AND} \quad 1.5 d \sqrt{1-Q} > 0$$

i.e., the marginal prevention/appraisal savings must offset the linear prevention-investment cost.

### Step 8 — Using Realistic Industry Benchmarks

In practice, organisations use the **rule-of-thumb ratio**:

$$PC : AC : IFC : EFC \approx 10 : 25 : 35 : 30 \quad \text{(at poor quality)}$$

After improvement:

$$PC : AC : IFC : EFC \approx 55 : 25 : 15 : 5$$

> [!TIP]
> **Examination pearl:** Notice how PC rises sharply (prevention up) while EFC crashes (warranty costs vanish). This is the *signature* of a successful QI programme. The examiner often shows this in a 14-mark question.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Improvement Levers vs. Cost Categories

| Improvement Lever | PC | AC | IFC | EFC | TQC Impact |
|-------------------|----|----|-----|-----|------------|
| Employee training | ↑ | — | ↓ | ↓ | Net ↓ |
| Process redesign | ↑ | — | ↓↓ | ↓↓ | Net ↓↓ |
| Supplier quality programme | ↑ | ↓ (incoming inspection) | ↓ | ↓ | Net ↓ |
| Six Sigma DMAIC project | ↑↑ | — | ↓↓ | ↓↓ | Net ↓↓ |
| Statistical Process Control | ↑ | ↓ (sampling) | ↓↓ | ↓ | Net ↓ |
| Poka-Yoke (mistake-proofing) | ↑ | — | ↓↓↓ | ↓↓ | Net ↓↓ |
| Total Productive Maintenance | ↑ | — | ↓ | ↓ | Net ↓ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading guide:** ↑ means cost rises in that category, ↓ means it falls. The lever is worthwhile only if Net ↓ in TQC.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    D1 --> D2[Train Team and Document Procedures]

    D2 --> C1[CHECK Compare Pilot Results vs Targets]

    A3 --> P1
```

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Questions (Short Answer)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define Quality Cost and list its four major categories.**

**Model Answer:**
Quality Cost (COQ) is the total cost incurred by an organisation to ensure conformance to quality standards and to rectify non-conformance. The four categories of the PAF Model are:

1. **Prevention Costs (PC)** — cost of activities to *prevent* defects (training, planning).
2. **Appraisal Costs (AC)** — cost of *detecting* defects (inspection, testing).
3. **Internal Failure Costs (IFC)** — cost of defects found *before* delivery (scrap, rework).
4. **External Failure Costs (EFC)** — cost of defects found *after* delivery (warranty, returns).

*(3 marks — 1 for definition, 2 for the four categories with brief examples.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
