```mermaid
    G -- Number of Defectives, Fixed n --> I[np-chart]
    G -- Number of Defects per Unit, Constant Area --> J[c-chart]
    G -- Defects per Unit, Variable Area --> K[u-chart]
    B -- Multivariate p>1 --> L[Hotelling T-squared Chart]
    D --> M{Shift Size Priority?}
    M -- Small shifts 0.5-1 sigma --> N[CUSUM Chart]
    M -- Small shifts + Smoothing --> O[EWMA Chart]
    M -- Large shifts >= 1.5 sigma --> P[Shewhart Chart]
    style A fill:#e1f5ff
    style D fill:#fff4e1
    style H fill:#e8f5e9
    style L fill:#fce4ec
    style N fill:#f3e5f5
    style O fill:#e0f7fa
```

### Diagram 2: CUSUM Tabular Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Input x_i, mu_0, K, H] --> B[C_i+ = max of 0, x_i minus mu_0+K + C_{i-1}+]
    A --> C[C_i- = max of 0, mu_0-K - x_i + C_{i-1}-]
    B --> D{C_i+ greater than H?}
    C --> E{C_i- greater than H?}
    D -- Yes --> F[Action: Upward Shift Detected]
    D -- No --> G[Continue Monitoring]
    E -- Yes --> H[Action: Downward Shift Detected]
    E -- No --> G
    G --> I[Move to Next Sample i+1]
    I --> A
    style F fill:#ffcdd2
    style H fill:#ffcdd2
    style G fill:#c8e6c9
```

### Diagram 3: Block-Level Functional Architecture of EWMA Filter

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT [Data Input]
        A1[Current Subgroup Mean X_i]
        A2[Previous EWMA Z_{i-1}]
        A3[Smoothing Constant lambda]
        A4[Process Target mu_0]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE [EWMA Engine]
        B1[Weighted Sum lambda X_i plus 1-lambda Z_{i-1}]
        B2[Time Varying Std Dev sigma times sqrt lambda by 2-lambda]
        B3[Steady State Limit mu_0 plus L sigma]
    end
    subgraph DECIDE [Decision Module]
        C1{Is Z_i within UCL and LCL?}
        C2 -- Yes --> D1[In Control: Continue]
        C2 -- No --> D2[Out of Control: Action Signal]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    A3 --> B1
    A1 --> B2
    A3 --> B2
    A4 --> B3
    B1 --> C1
    B3 --> C1
    style D2 fill:#ffab91
    style D1 fill:#a5d6a7
```

### Diagram 4: Attribute Chart Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Quality Characteristic Type] --> B{Count of Defectives or Defects?}
    B -- Defectives Item-Level Go or No Go --> C{Sample Size}
    B -- Defects Number of Flaws per Unit --> D{Area of Opportunity Constant?}
    C -- Fixed n --> E[np-chart: Central Line n bar p]
    C -- Variable n --> F[p-chart: Central Line bar p]
    D -- Yes --> G[c-chart: Central Line bar c]
    D -- No --> H[u-chart: Central Line bar u]

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 1]**
*Define the Cumulative Sum (CUSUM) control chart. Mention any two advantages it has over a Shewhart control chart.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The CUSUM control chart plots the cumulative sum of deviations of sample measurements from a target value. It is designed to detect small, sustained shifts in the process mean (typically $0.5\sigma$ to $1\sigma$) that Shewhart charts may miss.

**(2 marks for definition; 1 mark for advantages)**

**Advantages over Shewhart:**
1. **Faster detection of small shifts** — ARL₁ is significantly lower for shifts $\le 1.5\sigma$.
2. **Effective use of all data** — Every observation contributes to the cumulative statistic, whereas Shewhart uses only the most recent point.
3. Provides a clear visual plot of the cumulative drift direction.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between a c-chart and a u-chart. In which situation is each preferred?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | c-chart | u-chart |
|---|---|---|
| **Measures** | Number of defects per inspection unit | Number of defects per unit, with variable area |
| **Sample size** | Constant ($n$ fixed or one unit) | Variable ($n_i$ changes) |
| **Central line** | $\bar{c}$ | $\bar{u}$ |
| **Control limit** | $\bar{c} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$ | $\bar{u} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n_i}$ |
| **Preferred when** | Area of opportunity is constant (e.g., one printed circuit board) | Area varies (e.g., fabric rolls of different lengths) |

(2 marks for table; 1 mark for preferred situations)

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Paper 2]

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the construction and use of the **EWMA control chart**. Derive the expression for its steady-state control limits. State clearly the role of the smoothing constant $\lambda$.

**(b) (7 marks)** A process has target mean $\mu_0 = 200$ and $\sigma = 5$. Construct an EWMA chart using $\lambda = 0.2$ and $L = 3$. The subgroup means (n=4) are: 201, 199, 202, 201, 200, 202, 203, 201, 204, 205. Comment on the state of control.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — EWMA Construction (7 marks):**

The EWMA statistic is defined recursively as:
$$Z_i = \lambda \bar{X}_i + (1 - \lambda) Z_{i-1}, \quad Z_0 = \mu_0$$

**Derivation of steady-state variance:** Expanding the recursion:
$$Z_i = \lambda \bar{X}_i + \lambda(1-\lambda)\bar{X}_{i-1} + \lambda(1-\lambda)^2 \bar{X}_{i-2} + \dots$$

Assuming $\bar{X}_i$ are independent with variance $\sigma^2/n$ (or $\sigma^2$ for individuals), the variance of $Z_i$ is:
$$\text{Var}(Z_i) = \sigma^2 \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} \lambda^2 (1-\lambda)^{2j} = \sigma^2 \cdot \frac{\lambda[1-(1-\lambda)^{2i}]}{2-\lambda}$$

As $i \to \infty$:
$$\text{Var}(Z_i) \to \sigma^2 \cdot \frac{\lambda}{2-\lambda}$$

**Steady-state control limits:**
$$UCL = \mu_0 + L\sigma\sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{2-\lambda}}, \quad LCL = \mu_0 - L\sigma\sqrt{\frac{\lambda}{2-\lambda}}$$

**Role of $\lambda$:** $\lambda$ controls the **weight given to recent vs. historical data**.
- Small $\lambda$ (e.g., 0.1) → smoother, emphasizes older data → detects very small shifts slowly.
- Large $\lambda$ (e.g., 0.5) → more responsive, similar to Shewhart → detects larger shifts faster.
- Typical recommendation: $\lambda = 0.2$ for small shifts.

(3 marks: definition + recursion; 2 marks: variance derivation; 1 mark: limit expression; 1 mark: role of λ)

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Construction (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Steady-state standard deviation:
$$\sigma_Z = 5 \times \sqrt{\frac{0.2}{1.8}} = 5 \times \sqrt{0.1111} = 5 \times 0.3333 = 1.667$$

**Step 2:** Control limits:
$$UCL = 200 + 3(1.667) = 205.00, \quad LCL = 200 - 3(1.667) = 195.00$$

**Step 3:** Sequential $Z_i$ calculations:

| $i$ | $\bar{X}_i$ | $Z_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 0 | — | 200.000 |
| 1 | 201 | 0.2(201) + 0.8(200) = 200.20 |
| 2 | 199 | 0.2(199) + 0.8(200.2) = 200.00 (approx.) |

**Detailed computations:**

$$Z_1 = 0.2(201) + 0.8(200) = 40.2 + 160 = 200.20$$

$$Z_2 = 0.2(199) + 0.8(200.20) = 39.8 + 160.16 = 199.96$$

$$Z_3 = 0.2(202) + 0.8(199.96) = 40.4 + 159.968 = 200.37$$

$$Z_4 = 0.2(201) + 0.8(200.37) = 40.2 + 160.296 = 200.50$$

$$Z_5 = 0.2(200) + 0.8(200.50) = 40 + 160.40 = 200.40$$

$$Z_6 = 0.2(202) + 0.8(200.40) = 40.4 + 160.32 = 200.72$$

$$Z_7 = 0.2(203) + 0.8(200.72) = 40.6 + 160.576 = 201.18$$

$$Z_8 = 0.2(201) + 0.8(201.18) = 40.2 + 160.944 = 201.14$$

$$Z_9 = 0.2(204) + 0.8(201.14) = 40.8 + 160.912 = 201.71$$

$$Z_{10} = 0.2(205) + 0.8(201.71) = 41.0 + 161.368 = 202.37$$

**Step 4: Conclusion:**
All $Z_i$ values lie within [195, 205]. However, $Z_{10} = 202.37$ shows a **sustained upward trend** approaching the UCL. While no point crosses the limits, the trend signals a **probable process drift** — operator should investigate for assignable causes even though no formal out-of-control signal has triggered. (A CUSUM chart would have detected this earlier.)

(2 marks: limits; 3 marks: computations; 2 marks: interpretation)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - June 2023, Model Paper 1]

**(a) (7 marks)** Explain the **p-chart** with its construction procedure, control limits, and assumptions. When is it appropriate to use a p-chart instead of an np-chart?

**(b) (7 marks)** A manufacturer inspects 20 samples of 200 items each. The number of defectives in the samples are: 12, 15, 10, 18, 14, 16, 11, 13, 17, 19, 12, 15, 14, 10, 16, 13, 11, 18, 14, 15. Construct a p-chart and check for process control. If one sample lies outside the control limits, suggest corrective action.

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — p-Chart Theory (7 marks):**

The p-chart monitors the **fraction (proportion) of defective items** in successive samples. It is used when sample sizes may vary.

**Central line:** $\bar{p} = \dfrac{\sum d_i}{\sum n_i}$

**Control limits (variable n):**
$$UCL = \bar{p} + 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n_i}}, \quad LCL = \bar{p} - 3\sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n_i}}$$

If LCL < 0, set LCL = 0.

**Assumptions:**
1. Each item is independently classified as defective or non-defective.
2. The probability of defectiveness $p$ is constant for all items.
3. The number of defectives follows a **binomial distribution** $B(n, p)$.
4. $n\bar{p} \ge 5$ and $n(1-\bar{p}) \ge 5$ (for normal approximation to hold).

**p-chart vs np-chart:**
- Use **p-chart** when sample size $n$ is **variable** across subgroups.
- Use **np-chart** only when $n$ is **fixed/constant** (because np-chart plots raw count $np$, which is meaningful only for constant $n$).

(2 marks: definition + limits; 2 marks: assumptions; 2 marks: p vs np; 1 mark: construction procedure)

---

**Part (b) — p-Chart Numerical (7 marks):**

**Step 1:** Total defectives:
$$\sum d_i = 12+15+10+18+14+16+11+13+17+19+12+15+14+10+16+13+11+18+14+15 = 283$$

Total inspected: $n_{total} = 20 \times 200 = 4000$.

**Step 2:** Average proportion:
$$\bar{p} = \frac{283}{4000} = 0.07075$$

**Step 3:** Standard deviation of p:
$$\sigma_p = \sqrt{\frac{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})}{n}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.07075 \times 0.92925}{200}} = \sqrt{\frac{0.06575}{200}} = \sqrt{0.0003287} = 0.01813$$

**Step 4:** Control limits (n = 200):
$$UCL = 0.07075 + 3(0.01813) = 0.07075 + 0.05439 = 0.12514$$
$$LCL = 0.07075 - 3(0.01813) = 0.07075 - 0.05439 = 0.01636$$

**Step 5:** Compute $p_i = d_i/200$ for each sample:

| $i$ | $d_i$ | $p_i$ | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 12 | 0.060 | In control |
| 2 | 15 | 0.075 | In control |
| 3 | 10 | 0.050 | In control |
| 4 | 18 | 0.090 | In control |
| 5 | 14 | 0.070 | In control |
| 6 | 16 | 0.080 | In control |
| 7 | 11 | 0.055 | In control |
| 8 | 13 | 0.065 | In control |
| 9 | 17 | 0.085 | In control |
| 10 | 19 | 0.095 | In control |
| 11 | 12 | 0.060 | In control |
| 12 | 15 | 0.075 | In control |
| 13 | 14 | 0.070 | In control |
| 14 | 10 | 0.050 | In control |
| 15 | 16 | 0.080 | In control |
| 16 | 13 | 0.065 | In control |
| 17 | 11 | 0.055 | In control |
| 18 | 18 | 0.090 | In control |
| 19 | 14 | 0.070 | In control |
| 20 | 15 | 0.075 | In control |

**Step 6: Conclusion:**
All $p_i$ lie between 0.01636 and 0.12514 → **Process is in statistical control.** No corrective action required. However, an observation close to UCL ($p_{10} = 0.095$) should be monitored, and a CUSUM could be considered for tighter monitoring.

(1 mark: $\bar{p}$; 1 mark: limits; 3 marks: $p_i$ computation; 1 mark: interpretation; 1 mark: trend check)

---

### KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner Pattern):**
>
> 1. **Confusing p-chart and np-chart:** Students frequently use np-chart formulas when $n$ varies, which is mathematically wrong. Always verify if $n$ is fixed before choosing.
> 2. **Forgetting to set LCL = 0:** When LCL becomes negative for p-chart or c-chart, students often write the negative value, losing 1 mark. Always reset to 0.
> 3. **CUSUM direction error:** Mixing up $C_i^+$ and $C_i^-$. Memorize: $C_i^+$ uses $(\mu_0 + K)$ subtracted from $x_i$ (detects upward shifts in the LOWER direction); $C_i^-$ uses $(\mu_0 - K)$ subtracted from $x_i$ (detects upward shifts in $x_i$).
> 4. **EWMA initial value:** Forgetting $Z_0 = \mu_0$ leads to a cascading error across all $Z_i$ values.
> 5. **ARL formula confusion:** $ARL_0 = 1/\alpha$ (in-control, expected false alarm rate) vs. $ARL_1 = 1/(1-\beta)$ (out-of-control, average delay). Examiners specifically test this distinction.
> 6. **Attribute chart misclassification:** Using a c-chart when the variable is "defectives" (binary) instead of "defects" (count of flaws). This is the single most common error in attribute chart questions.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **CUSUM** uses cumulative sums $C_i^+$ and $C_i^-$ with reference value $K = \delta\sigma/2$ and decision interval $H$; excels at detecting shifts of $0.5\sigma$ to $1\sigma$.
- **EWMA** uses exponential weighting $Z_i = \lambda \bar{X}_i + (1-\lambda) Z_{i-1}$; steady-state limits are $\mu_0 \pm L\sigma\sqrt{\lambda/(2-\lambda)}$.
- **Smoothing constant $\lambda$:** Smaller $\lambda$ → more smoothing → better for small shifts; larger $\lambda$ → more responsive.
- **p-chart** monitors proportion of defectives, allows variable $n$, central line $\bar{p}$, limits $\bar{p} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{p}(1-\bar{p})/n}$.
- **np-chart** monitors number of defectives, requires fixed $n$, central line $n\bar{p}$.
- **c-chart** monitors number of defects per unit, constant opportunity, limits $\bar{c} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{c}}$.
- **u-chart** monitors defects per unit, variable opportunity, limits $\bar{u} \pm 3\sqrt{\bar{u}/n_i}$.
- **Short-run charts** use standardized Z-scores when batch sizes are too small for classical control limits.
- **Hotelling T²** is the multivariate extension of the X-bar chart, used for correlated multiple quality characteristics.
- **ARL₀ = 1/α** and **ARL₁ = 1/(1-β)** — the two key reliability metrics of any control chart.
- **Assumption check:** p, np use Binomial; c, u use Poisson (data must be counts of rare events).
- **Reset rule:** When LCL is negative, always set LCL = 0 (cannot have negative proportion or defects).
- **CUSUM vs EWMA:** CUSUM is optimal for a *specific* shift size; EWMA is good over a *range* of shifts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capability
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Process Capability # SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Process Capability** is a statistical measure of the inherent ability of a stable (in statistical control) manufacturing or service process to produce output that conforms to the customer's **Specification Limits** (also called Voice of the Customer — VOC). It quantitatively expresses the relationship between the **natural process spread** (driven by common-cause variation) and the **tolerance band** defined by the engineer or customer. Mathematically, process capability is the ratio of the *allowable design tolerance* (Specification Width) to the *actual process spread* (Voice of the Process — VOP), usually expressed in standard deviations of the process (σ). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 Scheme):** > Process capability is the *bridge between design (specification) and manufacturing (process reality)*. It answers the single most important question in quality engineering: **"Can my process meet the customer's requirements?"** ## 1.2 Standard Key Terms (KTU 2024 Terminology) | Abbreviation | Full Form | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **USL** | Upper Specification Limit | Maximum value the customer will accept | | **LSL** | Lower Specification Limit | Minimum value the customer will accept | | **μ (mu)** | Process Mean | The long-run average of the process | | **σ (sigma)** | Process Standard Deviation | Measure of common-cause variation | | **CL** | Center Line | The target / nominal value of the process | | **NPL** | Natural Process Limits | μ ± 3σ (the actual voice of the process) | ## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy — "The Bottling Plant" 🍶 Imagine a beverage bottling plant that fills 500 mL water bottles. - The **customer specification** says: "I want bottles containing **495 mL to 505 mL**." → This sets USL = 505 mL and LSL = 495 mL. - The **filling machine** has a natural *random* spread because of tiny fluctuations in nozzle pressure, temperature, and viscosity. Over thousands of bottles, the fill volumes form a bell-shaped curve (the **Normal Distribution**). - The typical machine might have mean μ = 500 mL and standard deviation σ = 1.2 mL. This means almost all bottles fall between 500 ± 3(1.2) = **496.4 mL to 503.6 mL** — easily inside the 495–505 specification. - Now, suppose the nozzle drifts (mean shifts to μ = 503 mL) — some bottles will exceed 505 mL and be **rejected**. The capability *worsens* even though σ didn't change. - **Process Capability Indices** are the numbers that summarize this entire story in one or two digits. > [!NOTE] > **The "Capable Process" Rule of Thumb:** > A process is considered *capable* when its natural spread (6σ) is **comfortably narrower** than the specification width (USL − LSL). The industry benchmark ratio is **6σ spread ≤ Specification width**, giving Cp ≥ 1.0. ## 1.4 Why Process Capability Matters in Real Engineering - **Automotive (IATF 16949):** Cpk ≥ 1.33 is the minimum for critical dimensions on engine parts. - **Aerospace (AS9100):** Cpk ≥ 1.67 is required for flight-critical components. - **Pharmaceutical (FDA / Six-Sigma):** Cpk ≥ 2.0 is the working standard; Six-Sigma demands 4.5σ on each tail. - **Electronics (SMT / PCBA):** Cpk monitoring on solder-paste volume prevents bridge defects. Without measuring capability, a manufacturer is *flying blind* — they do not know if their process is producing good parts by luck or by design. # SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Capability Family of Indices Capability indices fall into **two major families**: 1. **First-Generation Indices (Process Potential)** — Measure what the process *could* do if perfectly centered. They use **only the specification width and 6σ spread**. 2. **Second-Generation Indices (Process Performance)** — Measure what the process *actually does*, including the effect of **off-centering** of the mean. ### 2.1.1 First-Generation Indices **Cp — Process Capability Index (Potential Capability):** - Assumes the process mean is exactly on the target (centered). - Compares specification width to the 6σ natural process spread. **CPU (Cpk Upper)** and **CPL (Cpk Lower):** - One-sided capability indices, measuring distance from the process mean to *one* specification limit, in units of 3σ. ### 2.1.2 Second-Generation Indices **Cpk — Process Capability Index (Actual Performance):** - The minimum of CPU and CPL — penalizes the process for being off-center. - Always ≤ Cp. If Cpk ≈ Cp, the process is well-centered. **Cpm — Taguchi Capability Index:** - Penalizes deviation from the **target value (T)**, not just from the specification limits. Reflects Taguchi's loss-to-society philosophy. **Pp and Ppk — Process Performance Indices:** - Use the **overall standard deviation (σ_overall)** computed from the *entire dataset*, not the within-subgroup σ. - Used for **short-run, batch, or pre-control** analyses. - Pp includes both common-cause AND special-cause variation; Cp includes only common-cause. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Critical KTU Distinction:** Cp and Cpk are calculated using **σ_within** (estimated from R-bar / d2 or S-bar / c4 from control charts), while Pp and Ppk use **σ_overall** (the standard deviation of ALL individual data points). When the process is in statistical control, Cp ≈ Pp. ## 2.2 Master Formula Sheet (KTU Exam Cheat Sheet) | Index | Formula | What it Measures | When to Use | |---|---|---|---| | **σ̂ (within)** | $\sigma_{\text{within}} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ or $\dfrac{\bar{S}}{c_4}$ | Short-term common-cause variation | Control-chart-based estimates | | **σ̂ (overall)** | $\sigma_{\text{overall}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum (x_i - \bar{x})^2}$ | Total long-term variation | Long-run / batch analysis | | **Cp** | $C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$ | Potential capability (centered) | Symmetric tolerance, centered process | | **CPU** | $C_{PU} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | One-sided upper capability | When only USL exists | | **CPL** | $C_{PL} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$ | One-sided lower capability | When only LSL exists | | **Cpk** | $C_{pk} = \min(C_{PU},\ C_{PL})$ | Actual capability (off-center) | General industrial use | | **Cpm** | $C_{pm} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}$ | Taguchi index (target-penalized) | When target ≠ midpoint of spec | | **Pp** | $P_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma_{\text{overall}}}$ | Long-term potential | Pre-control / short runs | | **Ppk** | $P_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL-\mu}{3\sigma_{\text{overall}}},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma_{\text{overall}}}\right)$ | Long-term actual | Pre-control / short runs | | **% Defects (Normal)** | $\text{Defect \%} = 2\bigl[1 - \Phi(C_{pk})\bigr] \cdot 100$ | Expected defect rate | Yield estimation | | **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Defects}}{\text{Opportunities}} \times 10^6$ | Defects per million opportunities | Six-Sigma reporting | | **σ-level (Six-Sigma)** | $\sigma\text{-level} = C_{pk} + 1.5$ | Motorola's shift-adjusted sigma | Six-Sigma quality level | > [!NOTE] > **Units Convention:** All capability indices are **dimensionless ratios** (no units). The standard deviation σ must always be in the same units as USL and LSL. KTU examiners frequently test unit-consistency — never mix mm with inches! ## 2.3 Industry Interpretation Bands (KTU Frequently Tested) | Cpk / Cp Value | Process Interpretation | Typical Defect Rate | |---|---|---| | **< 1.00** | **Incapable** — produces defects at unacceptable rate | > 66,800 PPM | | **1.00 – 1.33** | **Marginal / Capable** — meets minimum automotive | 6,700 – 66,800 PPM | | **1.33 – 1.67** | **Good** — typical industrial target | 233 – 6,700 PPM | | **1.67 – 2.00** | **Excellent** — aerospace / safety-critical | 3.4 – 233 PPM | | **≥ 2.00** | **World-class / Six-Sigma** — pharmaceutical, semiconductor | < 3.4 PPM | ## 2.4 The Concept of Process "Off-Centering" (k-factor) When a process is *not* on target, the relevant penalty is the **k-factor** (also called the *centering index*): $$k = \dfrac{\vert \mu - T \vert}{(USL - LSL)/2} \times 100\%$$ where T is the target. A k = 0% means perfectly centered; k = 25% means the process mean lies 25% of the half-tolerance away from the target. KTU examiners often ask students to compute k before judging Cpk vs Cp. ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Predicting Yield** before committing to mass production. - **Selecting Suppliers** by auditing their Cpk certificates. - **Validating New Equipment** during Process Validation (PV) and PPAP (Production Part Approval Process). - **Continuous Improvement** (Kaizen) — a Cpk below target triggers a DMAIC project. - **Six-Sigma Black-Belt Projects** rely entirely on Cpk improvement to reach 4.5σ-levels. # SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Cp Formula (Geometric Origin) The process output, when in statistical control, follows a Normal distribution N(μ, σ²). For a Normal distribution, the well-known **Empirical Rule (68-95-99.7)** tells us that **99.73%** of all output lies within μ ± 3σ. This band is called the **Natural Process Spread** or **Natural Process Limits (NPL)**: $$\text{Natural Process Spread} = \text{Upper NPL} - \text{Lower NPL} = (\mu + 3\sigma) - (\mu - 3\sigma) = 6\sigma$$ The **Specification Width** is the tolerance band set by the customer: $$\text{Specification Width} = USL - LSL$$ **Cp** is *defined* as the ratio of Specification Width to the Natural Process Spread: $$C_p = \dfrac{\text{Specification Width}}{\text{Natural Process Spread}} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$ **Logic Explanation:** If the specification width is *larger* than the natural spread, the process is capable (Cp > 1). If smaller, it is not. The factor 6 in the denominator comes directly from the ±3σ range of the Normal distribution. > [!NOTE] > **KTU Exam Tip:** If a question gives the process spread as a number (e.g., "the process spread is 0.06 mm"), it means 6σ = 0.06 mm, so σ = 0.01 mm. Do **not** treat the spread as σ itself — this is one of the most common mistakes. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — The Cpk Formula (Asymmetric Penalty) Cp assumes the process is *perfectly centered*. If the mean drifts away from the midpoint of the specifications, defects appear on *one* side first. The fix is to compute the distance from the mean to *each* specification limit separately. **Step 1 — Distance to Upper Limit:** $$d_U = USL - \mu$$ **Step 2 — Distance to Lower Limit:** $$d_L = \mu - LSL$$ **Step 3 — Convert each distance to units of 3σ:** The "engineering tolerance" on the upper side, expressed in σ-units, is: $$C_{PU} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma}$$ Similarly, on the lower side: $$C_{PL} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}$$ **Step 4 — Take the *minimum* of the two:** $$C_{pk} = \min(C_{PU}, C_{PL}) = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$ **Algebraic Proof that Cpk ≤ Cp:** The arithmetic mean of CPU and CPL is: $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{C_{PU} + C_{PL}}{2} &= \dfrac{1}{2}\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} + \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right) \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{1}{2} \cdot \dfrac{USL - \mu + \mu - LSL}{3\sigma} \\[4pt] &= \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = C_p \end{aligned} $$ Since the **minimum of two numbers is always ≤ their arithmetic mean**, we conclude: $$C_{pk} = \min(C_{PU}, C_{PL}) \leq C_p$$ **Interpretation:** Equality Cpk = Cp holds *only* when the process mean is exactly on the midpoint of the specifications. Any off-centering strictly reduces Cpk. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Expected Defect Rate from Cpk If the process is approximately Normal and the *worst* side has Cpk = Cpk, the proportion of the area in the corresponding tail is: $$p = 1 - \Phi(3 C_{pk})$$ where Φ is the Standard Normal CDF. The **total expected defect rate** (summing both tails) is approximately: $$ \begin{aligned} P_{\text{defect}} &= P(X > USL) + P(X < LSL) \\[4pt] &= 2\bigl[1 - \Phi(3 C_{pk})\bigr] \end{aligned} $$ **Expressing in Parts Per Million (PPM):** $$\text{Defect PPM} = 2\bigl[1 - \Phi(3 C_{pk})\bigr] \times 10^6$$ For example, Cpk = 1.33 → 3 Cpk = 3.99 → 1 − Φ(3.99) ≈ 3.36 × 10⁻⁵ → ≈ 67 PPM per side → **≈ 67,000 DPMO** when summed on both sides (this matches the industry "6,700" lower-end of marginal band when one-sided defects are quoted — a common point of KTU confusion, see Pitfall below). > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** "Defect rate" tables in textbooks sometimes quote **one-sided** defects, sometimes **two-sided**. Always read the problem statement carefully. When the question says "yield", it means the non-defect fraction, and you must compute 1 − P_defect. ## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Exam Style) **Problem:** A shaft diameter has specification **24.00 ± 0.10 mm**. A sample of 50 shafts yields mean μ = 24.03 mm and standard deviation σ = 0.025 mm. Assume the process is in statistical control. Calculate: 1. Cp 2. CPU, CPL 3. Cpk 4. k-factor (centering index) 5. Estimated defect rate in PPM ### Solution: **Step 1 — Identify parameters:** - USL = 24.10 mm, LSL = 23.90 mm - μ = 24.03 mm, σ = 0.025 mm - Target T = 24.00 mm **Step 2 — Compute Cp:** $$ C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma} = \dfrac{24.10 - 23.90}{6 \times 0.025} = \dfrac{0.20}{0.15} = 1.333 $$ **Step 3 — Compute CPU:** $$ C_{PU} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{24.10 - 24.03}{3 \times 0.025} = \dfrac{0.07}{0.075} = 0.933 $$ **Step 4 — Compute CPL:** $$ C_{PL} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \dfrac{24.03 - 23.90}{3 \times 0.025} = \dfrac{0.13}{0.075} = 1.733 $$ **Step 5 — Cpk is the minimum:** $$ C_{pk} = \min(0.933,\ 1.733) = 0.933 $$ **Step 6 — Centering index k:** $$ k = \dfrac{\vert 24.03 - 24.00 \vert}{(24.10 - 23.90)/2} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.03}{0.10} \times 100\% = 30\% $$ **Step 7 — Defect rate from worst tail:** The worst side has Cpk = 0.933, so 3 × Cpk = 2.80. Looking up Φ(2.80) in the Standard Normal Table: Φ(2.80) ≈ 0.9974. $$ p_{\text{one-sided}} = 1 - 0.9974 = 0.0026 = 0.26\% = 2{,}600 \text{ PPM} $$ The *other* side is much further inside the spec (CPL = 1.733 → Φ(5.20) ≈ 1.0000), contributing negligible defects. Therefore, **total defect rate ≈ 2,600 PPM**. ### Engineering Interpretation: - Cp = 1.33 says: *"If centered, this process would just meet the automotive minimum."* - Cpk = 0.93 < 1.00 says: *"But because the mean has drifted up by 0.03 mm, the process is currently INCAPABLE."* - The corrective action is **re-center the machine** (not tighten the variability). This is a classic KTU insight: high Cp with low Cpk = centering problem, not variability problem. ## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix (When to Use What) | Situation | Recommended Index | Reason | |---|---|---| | Process is in **statistical control** AND **centered** | Cp | Both give same value; Cp is simpler | | Process is in control but **off-center** | Cpk | Cp overestimates; Cpk reflects reality | | **New / short-run** process with no control-chart history | Pp, Ppk | Uses overall σ from raw data | | Target ≠ Midpoint of Spec | Cpm | Penalizes deviation from target T | | Only **one-sided** spec (e.g., strength ≥ X) | CPU or CPL | LSL or USL does not exist | | **Six-Sigma reporting** | Cpk + 1.5 shift | Motorola's adjusted sigma-level | # SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Process Capability Ecosystem This diagram maps the *information flow* between customer requirements, process data, control charts, capability indices, and engineering decisions — the system view KTU examiners expect you to draw in Module 2. ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Customer Requirements
USL and LSL and Target T] --> C[Capability Analysis Engine] B[Process Data
Subgroup samples X1 X2] --> C D[Control Chart
Xbar R or Xbar S] --> E[Sigma Within Estimator] B --> E E --> C C --> F[Cp Computation Block] C --> G[Cpk Computation Block] C --> H[Cpm Computation Block] C --> I[Pp and Ppk Computation Block] F --> J[Decision Dashboard] G --> J H --> J I --> J J --> K{Index against benchmark} K -->|Cpk less than 1| L[INCAPABLE
Stop production
Re engineer process] K -->|Cpk 1 to 1.33| M[MARGINAL
100 percent inspection
Kaizen project] K -->|Cpk 1.33 to 1.67| N[CAPABLE
Continue with SPC] K -->|Cpk greater than 1.67| O[EXCELLENT
Reduce inspection
World class] L --> P[Feedback Loop to Process Re design] M --> P N --> P O --> P P --> B ``` ## 4.2 Comparative Distribution Schematic (Centered vs Off-Center) This diagram illustrates the *physical meaning* of Cp and Cpk by comparing two identical processes (same σ) but different mean positions. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Sub1["PROCESS A: Centered (mu = 50.0 mm)"] A1[LSL = 49.85] --> A2[Lower NPL = 49.94] A2 --> A3[mu = 50.0] A3 --> A4[Upper NPL = 50.06] A4 --> A5[USL = 50.15] end subgraph Sub2["PROCESS B: Off Center (mu = 50.05 mm)"] B1[LSL = 49.85] --> B2[Lower NPL = 49.99] B2 --> B3[mu = 50.05] B3 --> B4[Upper NPL = 50.11] B4 --> B5[USL = 50.15] end Sub1 --> R1["Result: Cp = Cpk = 1.67
EXCELLENT"] Sub2 --> R2["Result: Cp = 1.67 but Cpk = 1.0
MARGINAL only"] ``` ## 4.3 Cp vs Cpk Decision Tree A schematic flow to help students decide which index to compute and how to interpret it. ```mermaid flowchart TB S[Start: Given data] --> Q1{Process in statistical
control?} Q1 -->|No| Z1[First stabilize
Remove special causes
Then recompute] Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Subgroup data
available?} Q2 -->|Yes| Z2[Use sigma within
from R bar or S bar] Q2 -->|No| Z3[Use sigma overall
from raw data] Z2 --> Q3{Mean centered
on target?} Z3 --> Q3 Q3 -->|Yes| O1[Compute Cp] Q3 -->|No| O2[Compute Cpk] O1 --> E[Compare with industry
benchmark and decide] O2 --> E Z1 --> S ``` # SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A.1** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Define Process Capability Index Cpk. How is it different from Cp?** **Model Answer (3 marks — keyword-marking scheme):** **Cpk (Process Capability Index — actual):** It is a statistical measure of the *ability* of a stable process to produce output within specification limits, **accounting for off-centering** of the process mean. (1 mark) $$C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma}\right)$$ (0.5 mark for formula) **Difference from Cp:** (1.5 marks) | Aspect | Cp | Cpk | |---|---|---| | Definition | *Potential* capability (assumes centered) | *Actual* capability (includes off-centering) | | Formula | (USL − LSL) / 6σ | min((USL−μ)/3σ, (μ−LSL)/3σ) | | Penalizes off-centering? | **No** | **Yes** | | Relationship | Cpk ≤ Cp always | Cpk = Cp only if μ is on target | --- ### **Question A.2** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Explain the significance of the 1.5σ shift used in Six-Sigma quality level.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** In real production environments, the process mean **drifts over time** due to tool wear, ambient temperature changes, and operator-related factors. Motorola's research quantified this long-term mean shift as approximately **1.5σ**. (1.5 marks) Therefore, even if a process operates with Cpk = 2.0 in the short term, its **effective long-term sigma level** is reported as: $$\sigma\text{-level} = C_{pk} + 1.5 = 3.5 \text{ (not 4.0)}$$ (1 mark) This convention aligns short-term capability with realistic long-term defect rates (3.4 DPMO at the 4.5σ Six-Sigma level, accounting for the 1.5σ mean shift). (0.5 mark) --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice) ### **Question B-A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** For a CNC turning process producing a critical shaft, the specification is **Ø 30.000 ± 0.050 mm**. From a control chart, the average range is R̄ = 0.040 mm with subgroup size n = 5. From 25 subgroups, the grand average is X̿ = 30.012 mm. Compute Cp, Cpk, and the k-factor. (7 marks) **(b)** With reference to the above result, explain the engineering meaning of the values obtained. What corrective action would you recommend? (7 marks) --- ### **Model Solution B-A(a):** **Step 1 — List knowns:** - USL = 30.050 mm, LSL = 29.950 mm - n = 5, R̄ = 0.040 mm, X̿ = 30.012 mm - Target T = 30.000 mm - For n = 5, the d2 constant = 2.326 (from KTU-approved table) **Step 2 — Estimate σ_within:** $$ \hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \dfrac{0.040}{2.326} = 0.01720 \text{ mm} $$ (2 marks) **Step 3 — Compute Cp:** $$ C_p = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \dfrac{30.050 - 29.950}{6 \times 0.01720} = \dfrac{0.100}{0.1032} = 0.969 $$ (1.5 marks) **Step 4 — Compute CPU and CPL:** $$ C_{PU} = \dfrac{USL - \mu}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \dfrac{30.050 - 30.012}{3 \times 0.01720} = \dfrac{0.038}{0.0516} = 0.736 $$ $$ C_{PL} = \dfrac{\mu - LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \dfrac{30.012 - 29.950}{3 \times 0.01720} = \dfrac{0.062}{0.0516} = 1.202 $$ (2 marks) **Step 5 — Cpk and k-factor:** $$ C_{pk} = \min(0.736,\ 1.202) = 0.736 $$ $$ k = \dfrac{\vert 30.012 - 30.000 \vert}{(30.050 - 29.950)/2} \times 100\% = \dfrac{0.012}{0.050} \times 100\% = 24\% $$ (1.5 marks) --- ### **Model Solution B-A(b):** **Engineering Interpretation:** - **Cp = 0.969 < 1.00** — even if the process were perfectly centered, it would *not* meet the customer specification. The **inherent variability** is too large for the tolerance band. (2 marks) - **Cpk = 0.736** — much worse than Cp, because the mean is **drifted upward by 12 μm**. The Cpk is limited by the upper side (CPU = 0.736) since the process is producing oversized shafts. (2 marks) - **k = 24%** — the process mean is 24% of the half-tolerance away from target, indicating a serious centering problem. (1 mark) **Recommended Corrective Action (in priority order):** 1. **Immediate:** Re-center the CNC tool offset to bring the mean back to 30.000 mm. This single action should raise Cpk toward 0.969. (1 mark) 2. **Short-term:** Investigate the variability — check tool wear, coolant flow, and ambient temperature. Aim to reduce σ from 0.0172 to ≤ 0.0139 mm (which would give Cp ≥ 1.20 after re-centering). (0.5 mark) 3. **Long-term:** Conduct a **Design of Experiments (DOE)** to identify dominant sources of variation. If Cp cannot be raised to ≥ 1.33, negotiate a wider tolerance with the design engineer. (0.5 mark) --- ### **Question B-B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** What is the Taguchi Capability Index (Cpm)? Derive its expression and explain how it differs from Cpk. Under what conditions is Cpm equal to Cpk? (7 marks) **(b)** A pharmaceutical tablet weight process has specification 250 mg ± 10 mg. A pilot run of 100 tablets gives mean μ = 251.2 mg and standard deviation σ = 1.8 mg. Calculate Cp, Cpk, Cpm, and the sigma-level assuming a 1.5σ long-term shift. Comment on the suitability of this process for production release. (7 marks) --- ### **Model Solution B-B(a):** **Step 1 — Definition of Cpm:** Taguchi's Capability Index penalizes deviation of the process mean from a **specified target T**, not just from the specification limits. (1 mark) **Step 2 — Derivation:** Taguchi's *Loss Function* suggests that the true "spread" of a process should include both the variation σ² and the squared off-target distance (μ − T)². The total "effective variability" is: $$\sigma_{\text{eff}}^2 = \sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2$$ Cpm is then defined as the specification width divided by 6 times the effective spread: $$C_{pm} = \dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\sigma^2 + (\mu - T)^2}}$$ (3 marks) **Step 3 — Difference from Cpk:** (2 marks) | Aspect | Cpk | Cpm | |---|---|---| | Penalizes deviation from | Specification limits | **Target value T** | | Useful when | Limits matter (pass/fail) | Target quality matters (e.g., strength, weight) | | Sensitive to | Off-centering toward limits | Off-centering from target | **Step 4 — Equality Condition:** Cpm = Cpk when **T = midpoint of specifications** *and* **μ = T** (i.e., process is perfectly centered on target). In that case, (μ − T)² = 0, and Cpm = (USL − LSL) / (6σ) = Cp = Cpk. (1 mark) --- ### **Model Solution B-B(b):** **Step 1 — Identify parameters:** - USL = 260 mg, LSL = 240 mg, T = 250 mg - μ = 251.2 mg, σ = 1.8 mg **Step 2 — Compute Cp:** $$ C_p = \dfrac{260 - 240}{6 \times 1.8} = \dfrac{20}{10.8} = 1.852 $$ (1 mark) **Step 3 — Compute CPU, CPL, Cpk:** $$ C_{PU} = \dfrac{260 - 251.2}{3 \times 1.8} = \dfrac{8.8}{5.4} = 1.630 $$ $$ C_{PL} = \dfrac{251.2 - 240}{3 \times 1.8} = \dfrac{11.2}{5.4} = 2.074 $$ $$ C_{pk} = \min(1.630,\ 2.074) = 1.630 $$ (2 marks) **Step 4 — Compute Cpm:** $$ C_{pm} = \dfrac{260 - 240}{6\sqrt{(1.8)^2 + (251.2 - 250)^2}} = \dfrac{20}{6\sqrt{3.24 + 1.44}} = \dfrac{20}{6\sqrt{4.68}} = \dfrac{20}{6 \times 2.163} = \dfrac{20}{12.98} = 1.541 $$ (2 marks) **Step 5 — Sigma-level with 1.5σ shift:** $$ \sigma\text{-level} = C_{pk} + 1.5 = 1.630 + 1.5 = 3.13 $$ (1 mark) **Step 6 — Production Release Suitability Comment:** (1 mark) - Cpk = 1.630 (≥ 1.33) — meets automotive standard but **below the pharmaceutical benchmark of Cpk ≥ 2.0**. - The **sigma-level of 3.13** implies roughly 13,000 DPMO — far from the 3.4 DPMO Six-Sigma goal that pharma demands. - **Recommendation:** Process is **NOT suitable for production release** as-is. Re-center the equipment (μ should drop from 251.2 to 250.0), and reduce σ from 1.8 to ≤ 1.0 mg through tighter process control. After these improvements, recompute Cpk to confirm it exceeds 2.0. --- ## 5.3 Examiner's Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Treating the process "spread" as σ instead of 6σ.** If the question says "process spread = 0.06", it means 6σ = 0.06, so σ = 0.01. Doing the opposite loses 1–2 marks. > 2. **Forgetting to use d2 / c4 constants** when σ is given only through R̄ or S̄. Hard-coding σ without the control-chart conversion is a classic KTU trap. > 3. **Reporting only Cp, not Cpk** for an off-center process. Full marks require the *minimum of CPU and CPL*, plus an explicit k-factor calculation. > 4. **Quoting "defect rate" without specifying one-sided or two-sided.** Always state explicitly: "approximately 2,600 PPM on the upper tail only." > 5. **Mixing σ_within and σ_overall in the same formula.** Cp/Pp use different σ — students frequently transpose them. > 6. **Cpk < 1 → "Process is bad"** is too vague. The correct interpretation is **"Process produces more than 2,700 PPM defects"** (at Cpk = 1) — quote numbers, not adjectives. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > **📋 Rapid-Revision Checklist for Process Capability** > > - [x] **Process Capability** = ratio of specification width to natural process spread (6σ). > - [x] **Cp** = potential capability (assumes μ is on target). Formula: (USL − LSL) / 6σ. > - [x] **Cpk** = actual capability (penalizes off-centering). Formula: min(CPU, CPL). > - [x] **CPU = (USL − μ) / 3σ** and **CPL = (μ − LSL) / 3σ**. > - [x] **Cpk ≤ Cp** always; equality only when the process is *perfectly centered*. > - [x] **Cpm** (Taguchi) penalizes deviation from the *target* T, not the spec limits. > - [x] **Pp and Ppk** use σ_overall (raw data), not σ_within (control chart). > - [x] **k-factor** = percentage of half-tolerance the mean is off from target. > - [x] **σ within** estimated via R̄ / d₂ or S̄ / c₄ from control charts. > - [x] **Industry benchmarks:** Cpk ≥ 1.33 (auto), 1.67 (aero), 2.00 (pharma). > - [x] **Six-Sigma convention:** σ-level = Cpk + 1.5 to account for long-term mean shift. > - [x] **DPMO = Defects / Opportunities × 10⁶** — Six-Sigma target: 3.4 DPMO at 4.5σ-level. > - [x] **Defect rate from Cpk:** P = 2 × [1 − Φ(3·Cpk)] (two-sided). > - [x] **First stabilize, then compute capability** — never compute Cpk on a process with active special causes. > - [x] **High Cp + low Cpk** → centering problem (re-set the mean). > - [x] **Low Cp + low Cpk** → variability problem (reduce σ).

topics.title: Process Capability Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Capability Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Process Capability Analysis (PCA)** is a systematic statistical procedure used in quality engineering to evaluate the inherent ability of a stable, in-control manufacturing or service process to produce output that consistently conforms to engineering specifications (tolerance limits). It quantifies the relationship between the **natural variability of a process** (described by its standard deviation $\sigma$) and the **allowable variability defined by the customer or design engineer** (described by the Upper Specification Limit – USL and Lower Specification Limit – LSL).

Mathematically, it is expressed through dimensionless indices collectively called **Process Capability Indices (PCIs)**, the most fundamental being **$C_p$**, **$C_{pk}$**, **$P_p$**, and **$P_{pk}$**. A process is deemed "capable" only when its actual variation fits comfortably inside the specification window with a predictable, statistically measurable safety margin.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Process Capability Analysis sits at the intersection of **Statistical Process Control (SPC)** and **Acceptance Sampling**. It is *not* a measure of whether the process is in statistical control — that is determined first by control charts (X-bar, R-chart). PCA can only be legitimately applied **after** the process has been proven stable and free of special-cause variation.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The Dartboard Analogy

Imagine a skilled archer standing 10 meters away from a dartboard. The bullseye represents the **target value** of a quality characteristic (e.g., shaft diameter = 50.00 mm), and the dartboard's outer boundary represents the **specification limits** (e.g., 49.90 mm to 50.10 mm).

- **A capable process** is one where the archer's darts always land *inside* the outer ring. Even if the average arrow is slightly off-center, as long as the cluster of arrows is small and inside the ring, the process is capable.
- **A precise process** has a tight cluster (low $\sigma$).
- **An accurate (centered) process** has the cluster's mean right on the bullseye ($\mu$ = target).

If you swap the archer for a **machine tool** turning out piston rings, you get the essence of process capability:

1. **How wide is the natural spread?** → controlled by $\sigma$ (process standard deviation).
2. **Where is the mean located?** → controlled by $\mu$ (process mean).
3. **How wide is the customer-allowed window?** → controlled by USL − LSL.

Process Capability Analysis is the **scorecard** that answers: *Given the spread and centering, how comfortably does the process fit inside the customer's tolerances?*

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Standard Glossary)

> [!NOTE]
> These terms appear verbatim in KTU previous-year question papers and must be memorized precisely.

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Upper Specification Limit | **USL** | Maximum permissible value of the quality characteristic |
| Lower Specification Limit | **LSL** | Minimum permissible value |
| Target Value | **T** | The nominal / design intent value |
| Process Mean | **$\mu$** | Long-run average of the process output |
| Process Standard Deviation | **$\sigma$** | Measure of inherent short-term variability (from control charts) |
| Overall Standard Deviation | **$s$** | Measure of long-term / total variability (from individual observations) |
| Process Capability Index | $C_p$ | Measures **potential** capability (spread only) |
| Process Capability Index | $C_{pk}$ | Measures **actual** capability (spread + centering) |
| Process Performance Index | $P_p$ | Long-term capability (uses $s$ instead of $\sigma$) |
| Process Performance Index | $P_{pk}$ | Long-term actual capability (uses $s$ and $\mu$) |
| Process Capability Ratio | $C_{pm}$ | Taguchi's index penalizing deviation from target |

## 1.4 Why Process Capability Analysis is Critical in Engineering

- **Customer Confidence:** Demonstrates quantitatively that a supplier's process can meet drawing tolerances over time.
- **Six-Sigma Programs:** A $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ (industry) or $C_{pk} = 2.00$ (Six-Sigma, 3.4 DPMO) is the universally accepted threshold.
- **Equipment Selection:** Engineers use $C_p$ to decide whether a given machine tool is suitable for a part's tolerance.
- **Cost Reduction:** A high $C_{pk}$ means less rework, scrap, warranty claims, and inspection effort.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Automotive (IATF 16949), aerospace (AS9100), and medical (ISO 13485) standards mandate demonstrated process capability before serial production (PPAP, FAI).

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Pre-Requisite: Is the Process Stable?

Process capability analysis has **two stages**:

1. **Stage 1: Stability Check** — Apply control charts ($\bar{X}$–$R$ or $\bar{X}$–$s$, I–MR for individuals). If all points lie within control limits and there is no run/trend, the process is **in statistical control**.
2. **Stage 2: Capability Computation** — Only now is it valid to compute $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Computing $C_p$ on an unstable process is the **#1 mistake** in KTU board exams. Examiners explicitly check whether the student addressed stability first.

## 2.2 Estimation of Process Standard Deviation

There are two distinct estimates that produce **two different families** of indices:

### A. Within-Subgroup Standard Deviation ($\hat{\sigma}$)
- Estimated from the **average range** $\bar{R}$ or pooled standard deviation $s_p$ of rational subgroups taken from a stable process.
- Captures **short-term, common-cause variability** only.
- Used in $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$.

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$$

where $d_2$ is a constant depending on subgroup size $n$ (e.g., $d_2 = 2.326$ for $n=5$, $d_2 = 1.128$ for $n=2$).

### B. Overall / Total Standard Deviation ($s$)
- Estimated from the standard deviation of **all individual measurements** in the dataset.
- Captures **long-term variability**, including drift between subgroups.
- Used in $P_p$ and $P_{pk}$.

$$s = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N-1}\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_i - \bar{x})^2}$$

The relationship is consistently $s \geq \hat{\sigma}$, so **$P_{pk} \leq C_{pk}$** for the same dataset. A large gap between them signals the presence of special causes.

## 2.3 The Core Capability Indices

### 2.3.1 $C_p$ — Process Capability Index (Potential Capability)

Measures the **potential** of the process to meet specifications, assuming it is perfectly centered. It is purely a ratio of "specification width" to "process spread":

$$C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{\text{Tolerance Width}}{\text{Process Width}}$$

- $C_p = 1.00$ → Process spread exactly equals the specification width (zero margin — not acceptable).
- $C_p = 1.33$ → Process uses 75% of the tolerance band (industry minimum for new processes).
- $C_p = 1.50$ → Process uses 50% of the band (preferred for existing capable processes).
- $C_p = 2.00$ → Six-Sigma level (process uses only 25% of the band).

> [!WARNING]
> A high $C_p$ alone is **misleading**. A process can be wildly off-center and still report a great $C_p$. This is why $C_{pk}$ always accompanies it.

### 2.3.2 $C_{pk}$ — Process Capability Index (Actual Capability)

Accounts for the **location of the process mean** relative to the specifications:

$$C_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \bar{\bar{x}}}{3\hat{\sigma}},\ \frac{\bar{\bar{x}} - \text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}}\right)$$

The two terms inside the minimum are called the **One-Sided Capability Indices**:

- **CPU (or $C_{pU}$)** = $\dfrac{\text{USL} - \bar{\bar{x}}}{3\hat{\sigma}}$ — distance of mean from upper limit
- **CPL (or $C_{pL}$)** = $\dfrac{\bar{\bar{x}} - \text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}}$ — distance of mean from lower limit

Therefore:

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{PU}, C_{PL})$$

**Centering relationship:**

$$C_{pk} = C_p (1 - k)$$

where $k$ is the **centering index**:

$$k = \frac{\left| \dfrac{\text{USL} + \text{LSL}}{2} - \bar{\bar{x}} \right|}{\dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{2}}, \quad 0 \le k \le 1$$

When $k = 0$, the process is perfectly centered and $C_{pk} = C_p$.

### 2.3.3 $P_p$ and $P_{pk}$ — Process Performance Indices

Use the **overall standard deviation $s$** instead of $\hat{\sigma}$. They reflect the *actual* long-run performance the customer will see, including drift:

$$P_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6s}$$

$$P_{pk} = \min\left(\frac{\text{USL} - \bar{x}}{3s},\ \frac{\bar{x} - \text{LSL}}{3s}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** *P*erformance = Population-based (uses $s$); *C*apability = Common-cause (uses $\hat{\sigma}$).

### 2.3.4 $C_{pm}$ — Taguchi's Capability Index

Genichi Taguchi proposed penalizing deviation from the **target** $T$, not just from the nearest specification limit:

$$C_{pm} = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 + (\bar{\bar{x}} - T)^2}}$$

A process with the same $C_p$ but a mean farther from target gets a **lower** $C_{pm}$. This embodies Taguchi's **Quality Loss Function** philosophy.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Index | Formula | Numerator Meaning | Denominator Meaning | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_p$ | $\dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}}$ | Specification width | Natural process width (6$\sigma$) | Stable process, ignore centering |
| 2 | $C_{pk}$ | $\min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{\bar{x}}}{3\hat{\sigma}}, \dfrac{\bar{\bar{x}} - LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}}\right)$ | Smaller distance to nearest spec | One-half of natural spread | Stable process, include centering |
| 3 | $C_{pm}$ | $\dfrac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 + (\bar{\bar{x}}-T)^2}}$ | Specification width | Spread + mean-shift penalty | When target value is critical |
| 4 | $P_p$ | $\dfrac{USL - LSL}{6s}$ | Specification width | Total observed spread | Long-term (entire data) |
| 5 | $P_{pk}$ | $\min\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \bar{x}}{3s}, \dfrac{\bar{x} - LSL}{3s}\right)$ | Distance to nearest spec | One-half of total spread | Long-term, include centering |
| 6 | $k$ | $\dfrac{\vert M - \bar{\bar{x}} \vert}{\frac{1}{2}(USL - LSL)}$ | Mean shift from midpoint | Half-tolerance | Centering diagnostic |
| 7 | $\hat{\sigma}$ | $\dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2}$ | Average subgroup range | Tabulated $d_2$ for $n$ | From control chart data |
| 8 | Defect Rate | $P(X > USL) = 1 - \Phi\!\left(\dfrac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | Tail probability | Normal CDF | PPM/prediction problems |

**Industry Capability Benchmarks (must memorize for KTU):**

| $C_{pk}$ Value | Process Status | Expected Defect Rate (ppm outside spec) |
|---|---|---|
| $< 1.00$ | Incapable | $> 2700$ |
| $1.00 - 1.33$ | Marginally capable | $63 - 2700$ |
| $1.33 - 1.50$ | Capable | $0.3 - 63$ |
| $1.50 - 1.67$ | Highly capable | $0.003 - 0.3$ |
| $1.67 - 2.00$ | Excellent / Six-Sigma class | $< 0.003$ |
| $\geq 2.00$ | World-class | ~ 0 (3.4 DPMO) |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CNC Machining:** Used to decide whether a part can be produced on a given lathe/mill or needs a higher-precision machine.
- **Electronics Manufacturing:** SMT solder-paste deposition volume monitored via $C_{pk}$ for IATF 16949 compliance.
- **Pharmaceutical:** Tablet weight, hardness, dissolution time all monitored; FDA demands $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ at PPAP.
- **Service Industry:** Banking transaction times, hospital lab TAT — same statistical framework applies.

# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why Divide by $6\sigma$?

The normal distribution exhibits the well-known **empirical rule**:

$$P(\mu - 3\sigma \le X \le \mu + 3\sigma) \approx 99.73\%$$

Hence the natural width of a stable process is the **6-sigma spread** $\mu \pm 3\sigma$, which has total length $6\sigma$. A specification window of width $USL - LSL$ will contain this natural spread if and only if:

$$6\sigma \le USL - LSL$$

The ratio that quantifies "how many times the natural spread fits into the specification" is therefore:

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sigma}$$

This dimensionless ratio is **scale-free** (works for mm, ohms, kg, seconds alike) — a major practical advantage.

## 3.2 Derivation: $C_{pk}$ as the Minimum of Two One-Sided Indices

Consider the upper tail first. The number of standard deviations from the process mean to the USL is:

$$Z_{USL} = \frac{USL - \mu}{\sigma}$$

The CPU index is this value divided by 3 (so it represents "how many 3$\sigma$ widths fit between the mean and USL"):

$$C_{PU} = \frac{USL - \mu}{3\sigma} = \frac{Z_{USL}}{3}$$

Similarly, the lower side:

$$C_{PL} = \frac{\mu - LSL}{3\sigma} = \frac{Z_{LSL}}{3}$$

A process is only as capable as its **worst side**, hence the minimum operator:

$$C_{pk} = \min(C_{PU}, C_{PL})$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (Full Solution)

> **KTU 2024-style 14-mark problem pattern**

**Problem:** A CNC turning process produces shafts. Specifications are: $USL = 25.05$ mm, $LSL = 24.95$ mm, target $T = 25.00$ mm. Twenty-five subgroups of size $n = 5$ were collected.

**Given statistics:**

- Grand mean: $\bar{\bar{x}} = 25.012$ mm
- Average range: $\bar{R} = 0.018$ mm
- Sample standard deviation of all 125 observations: $s = 0.011$ mm

**Required:** Compute $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $C_{pm}$, $P_p$, $P_{pk}$, and interpret.

### Solution

**Step 1 — Estimate within-subgroup standard deviation**

For $n = 5$, $d_2 = 2.326$.

$$\hat{\sigma} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \frac{0.018}{2.326}$$

$$\hat{\sigma} = 0.007740 \text{ mm} \quad (\text{rounded to 6 decimals})$$

**Step 2 — Compute $C_p$**

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{25.05 - 24.95}{6 \times 0.007740}$$

$$C_p = \frac{0.10}{0.04644} = 2.1533$$

**Step 3 — Compute $C_{PU}$ and $C_{PL}$**

$$C_{PU} = \frac{USL - \bar{\bar{x}}}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{25.05 - 25.012}{3 \times 0.007740} = \frac{0.038}{0.02322} = 1.6365$$

$$C_{PL} = \frac{\bar{\bar{x}} - LSL}{3\hat{\sigma}} = \frac{25.012 - 24.95}{3 \times 0.007740} = \frac{0.062}{0.02322} = 2.6701$$

**Step 4 — Compute $C_{pk}$**

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.6365,\ 2.6701) = 1.6365$$

The upper side is the binding constraint because the mean has shifted slightly above the target.

**Step 5 — Centering index $k$**

The midpoint of specifications: $M = \dfrac{USL + LSL}{2} = \dfrac{25.05 + 24.95}{2} = 25.00$ mm

$$k = \frac{\vert M - \bar{\bar{x}} \vert}{\tfrac{1}{2}(USL - LSL)} = \frac{\vert 25.00 - 25.012 \vert}{0.05} = \frac{0.012}{0.05} = 0.24$$

**Verification using the $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$ shortcut:**

$$C_p (1-k) = 2.1533 \times (1 - 0.24) = 2.1533 \times 0.76 = 1.6365 \ \checkmark$$

**Step 6 — Compute $C_{pm}$ (Taguchi Index)**

$$C_{pm} = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\sqrt{\hat{\sigma}^2 + (\bar{\bar{x}} - T)^2}}$$

$$\bar{\bar{x}} - T = 25.012 - 25.000 = 0.012 \text{ mm}$$

$$(\bar{\bar{x}} - T)^2 = (0.012)^2 = 0.000144$$

$$\hat{\sigma}^2 = (0.007740)^2 = 0.00005991$$

$$\sqrt{0.00005991 + 0.000144} = \sqrt{0.00020391} = 0.014280$$

$$C_{pm} = \frac{0.10}{6 \times 0.014280} = \frac{0.10}{0.08568} = 1.1671$$

> The drop from $C_p = 2.15$ to $C_{pm} = 1.17$ reveals that while the process spread is excellent, the mean shift from target heavily penalizes the Taguchi index.

**Step 7 — Compute $P_p$**

$$P_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6s} = \frac{0.10}{6 \times 0.011} = \frac{0.10}{0.066} = 1.5152$$

**Step 8 — Compute $P_{pk}$**

$$P_{PU} = \frac{USL - \bar{\bar{x}}}{3s} = \frac{0.038}{0.033} = 1.1515$$

$$P_{PL} = \frac{\bar{\bar{x}} - LSL}{3s} = \frac{0.062}{0.033} = 1.8788$$

$$P_{pk} = \min(1.1515,\ 1.8788) = 1.1515$$

**Step 9 — Interpretation and Comparison Table**

| Index | Value | Benchmark Status |
|---|---|---|
| $C_p$ | 2.1533 | Excellent (potential capability) |
| $C_{pk}$ | 1.6365 | Excellent, but lower than $C_p$ → off-center |
| $C_{pm}$ | 1.1671 | Acceptable (target-sensitive) |
| $P_p$ | 1.5152 | Good |
| $P_{pk}$ | 1.1515 | Marginal — long-term real-world capability is lower |

**Engineering Conclusion:**

- The process is **statistically excellent in potential** ($C_p > 1.67$).
- A noticeable **mean shift of +0.012 mm above target** has reduced $C_{pk}$ below $C_p$.
- The significant gap $C_{pk} - P_{pk} \approx 0.485$ indicates there is some **special-cause / between-subgroup variation** present — recheck control charts.
- **Recommendation:** Re-center the process (adjust CNC tool offset) to bring $\bar{\bar{x}}$ back to 25.00 mm; this alone could raise $C_{pk}$ to ~2.15.

## 3.4 Predicting Defect Rates (ppm outside spec)

Given a centered process with $C_p = 1.33$ (so $C_{pk} = 1.33$), the distance from mean to each spec is $4\sigma$. Tail probability:

$$P(Z > 4) = 0.0000317 \approx 31.7 \text{ ppm per side}$$

For a two-sided specification: $\approx 63.4$ ppm. This matches the automotive industry rule that $C_{pk} = 1.33$ ≈ 63 ppm defects.

For a Six-Sigma process ($C_{pk} = 2.00$ with 1.5$\sigma$ shift allowed), the defect rate is **3.4 ppm** — the famous Motorola benchmark.

# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Capability Decision Tree (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Have Process Data] --> B{Is the Process in Statistical Control?}
    B -- No --> C[Apply Control Charts: X-bar R]
    C --> D[Identify and Remove Special Causes]
    D --> B
    B -- Yes --> E[Compute Within-Subgroup Sigma-hat]
    E --> E1["hat-sigma = R-bar / d2"]
    E --> F[Compute Overall Std Dev s]
    F --> F1["s from individual values"]
    E --> G[Compute Cp and Cpk]
    F --> H[Compute Pp and Ppk]
    G --> G1["Cp = USL-LSL / 6 hat-sigma"]
    G --> G2["Cpk = min CPU, CPL"]
    H --> H1["Pp = USL-LSL / 6s"]
    H --> H2["Ppk = min PPU, PPL"]
    G1 --> I[Compare Cpk with Benchmarks]
    G2 --> I
    H1 --> I
    H2 --> I
    I --> I1{Cpk >= 1.33?}
    I1 -- Yes --> J[Process Capable: Approve for Production]
    I1 -- No --> K[Process Not Capable: Reduce Sigma-hat or Re-center]
    K --> L[Action: Machine Upgradation, Tool Change, Process Redesign]
    L --> B
```
