# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: Define Process Capability Index $C_p$. What does a $C_p$ value of 1.33 signify?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (2 marks):** $C_p$ is the **Process Capability Index** that compares the width of the specification tolerance (USL − LSL) to the six-sigma spread ($6\sigma$) of a stable process:

$$C_p = \frac{USL - LSL}{6\hat{\sigma}}$$

**Significance (1 mark):** A $C_p = 1.33$ indicates that the natural process width occupies only **75%** of the tolerance band, leaving a **25% safety margin** on either side. This is the **industry-accepted minimum** for a new process — the process spread is 1.33 times narrower than the specification width, ensuring that even without perfect centering, very few parts will fall outside specifications.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$. Why is $C_{pk}$ always less than or equal to $C_p$?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | $C_p$ | $C_{pk}$ |
|---|---|---|
| Considers centering? | **No** | **Yes** |
| Formula | $(USL - LSL)/6\hat{\sigma}$ | $\min(C_{PU}, C_{PL})$ |
| Interpretation | Potential capability | Actual capability |

**Inequality (1 mark):** $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$ where $k$ is the centering index ($0 \le k \le 1$). Since $(1-k) \le 1$, it follows that **$C_{pk} \le C_p$ always**, with equality holding **only when the process is perfectly centered** ($k=0$).

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** Explain the concept of Process Capability Analysis. Define the indices $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and $C_{pm}$ with formulas. Discuss the conditions under which each index is applicable.

**(b) [7 marks]** A grinding process produces bearings with specification $USL = 30.025$ mm, $LSL = 29.975$ mm. Twenty-five samples of size 5 are taken. The grand mean is $\bar{\bar{x}} = 30.005$ mm, average range $\bar{R} = 0.012$ mm, and overall standard deviation $s = 0.008$ mm. Calculate $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $P_p$, $P_{pk}$, and $C_{pm}$. Comment on the process capability.

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):** (See Sections 1, 2 and 2.3 of these notes for full conceptual write-up. Award 1 mark each for definitions of $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, $C_{pm}$ with formulas, 1 mark for applicability conditions, and remaining marks for discussion of stability pre-requisite and Six-Sigma context.)

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1:** $\hat{\sigma} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_2} = \dfrac{0.012}{2.326} = 0.00516$ mm (for $n=5$)

**Step 2: $C_p$**

$$C_p = \frac{30.025 - 29.975}{6 \times 0.00516} = \frac{0.050}{0.03096} = 1.6152$$

**Step 3: $C_{PU}$ and $C_{PL}$**

$$C_{PU} = \frac{30.025 - 30.005}{3 \times 0.00516} = \frac{0.020}{0.01548} = 1.2919$$

$$C_{PL} = \frac{30.005 - 29.975}{3 \times 0.00516} = \frac{0.030}{0.01548} = 1.9379$$

**Step 4: $C_{pk}$**

$$C_{pk} = \min(1.2919,\ 1.9379) = 1.2919$$

**Step 5: $P_p$**

$$P_p = \frac{0.050}{6 \times 0.008} = \frac{0.050}{0.048} = 1.0417$$

**Step 6: $P_{pk}$**

$$P_{PU} = \frac{0.020}{3 \times 0.008} = 0.8333$$

$$P_{PL} = \frac{0.030}{3 \times 0.008} = 1.2500$$

$$P_{pk} = \min(0.8333,\ 1.2500) = 0.8333$$

**Step 7: $C_{pm}$**

$$\sqrt{(0.00516)^2 + (0.005)^2} = \sqrt{0.0000266 + 0.0000250} = \sqrt{0.0000516} = 0.007181$$

$$C_{pm} = \frac{0.050}{6 \times 0.007181} = \frac{0.050}{0.04309} = 1.1604$$

**Summary Table:**

| Index | Value | Status |
|---|---|---|
| $C_p$ | 1.6152 | Capable |
| $C_{pk}$ | 1.2919 | Marginally capable (off-center) |
| $P_p$ | 1.0417 | Marginal |
| $P_{pk}$ | 0.8333 | **Not capable** long-term |
| $C_{pm}$ | 1.1604 | Marginal |

**Comments (2 marks):**
- The process has **good potential** ($C_p = 1.62$) but suffers from a **mean shift of +0.005 mm** above target.
- The large gap $C_{pk} - P_{pk} = 0.4586$ indicates **special-cause variation** exists between subgroups — investigate with control charts.
- Long-term, the process is **not capable** ($P_{pk} < 1.0$). Immediate corrective action: **re-center** the machine and **reduce** between-subgroup variation.

---

### Question B `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper Module 2]`

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the difference between a *centered* and an *off-centered* process. How does this difference affect $C_p$, $C_{pk}$, and the expected defect rate?

**(b) [7 marks]** A Six-Sigma project aims to achieve $C_{pk} = 2.00$ for a critical dimension with $USL = 12.020$ mm, $LSL = 11.980$ mm. If the current process has $C_p = 1.50$ and $C_{pk} = 1.20$, by what factor must the process standard deviation be reduced to meet the Six-Sigma target? What is the current defect rate (ppm)?

#### Model Solution

**Part (a):** (Award 1 mark for sketch description, 1.5 marks each for $C_p$/$C_{pk}$ relationship, 2 marks for defect-rate interpretation. Provide ASCII or descriptive sketch of a normal curve centered on target vs. shifted curve; explain that $C_p$ is unchanged but $C_{pk}$ drops by factor $(1-k)$.)

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution:**

**Step 1: Current state**

Tolerance: $USL - LSL = 0.040$ mm

Given $C_p = 1.50$:

$$1.50 = \frac{0.040}{6\sigma_{current}} \Rightarrow \sigma_{current} = \frac{0.040}{9.0} = 0.004444 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2: Target state with $C_{pk} = 2.00$**

For Six-Sigma, process must be centered (or within 1.5$\sigma$ of target). For full centering:

$$2.00 = \frac{0.040}{6\sigma_{target}} \Rightarrow \sigma_{target} = \frac{0.040}{12.0} = 0.003333 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 3: Reduction factor**

$$\text{Factor} = \frac{\sigma_{current}}{\sigma_{target}} = \frac{0.004444}{0.003333} = 1.3333$$

The standard deviation must be reduced by a factor of **1.333** (a 25% reduction).

**Step 4: Verify using $C_{pk} = 1.20$**

$C_{pk} = C_p(1-k) \Rightarrow 1.20 = 1.50(1-k) \Rightarrow k = 0.20$

The mean is shifted by 20% of the half-tolerance. Process mean is **0.004 mm off-center** (since half-tolerance = 0.020).

**Step 5: Current defect rate (ppm)**

The smaller of the two distances from mean to spec is $USL - \mu$:

$$\text{USL} - \mu = 20 \times (1 - 0.80) = 4\sigma \text{ on the tight side}$$

Wait — recompute: With $k = 0.20$, the distance from mean to the nearer spec is $(1-k) \times (USL - LSL)/2 = 0.80 \times 0.020 = 0.016$ mm.

$$Z = \frac{0.016}{0.004444} = 3.60$$

Tail probability: $P(Z > 3.60) \approx 0.000159 = 159$ ppm per side.

For two-sided specification: $\approx 318$ ppm (but actual ppm depends on the upper-side too). Upper side has $Z = 5.6$ (negligible, 0 ppm). So total defect rate ≈ **318 ppm**.

**Conclusion:** Reducing $\sigma$ by 25% combined with re-centering the process will dramatically cut defects from ~318 ppm to 3.4 ppm — the essence of Six-Sigma transformation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Skipping the stability check** — always state that the process must be in statistical control before computing $C_p$/$C_{pk}$ (loses 1–2 marks).
> 2. **Using $s$ instead of $\hat{\sigma}$ for $C_p$** — the $C$-family indices use within-subgroup variation; using $s$ gives $P_p$ instead. Many students confuse these in the exam hall.
> 3. **Forgetting the "min" in $C_{pk}$** — writing only one side (CPU or CPL) loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Wrong $d_2$ value** — $d_2 = 2.326$ for $n=5$ must be committed to memory; examiners do not provide it.
> 5. **No interpretation / no engineering recommendation** — at least 1–2 marks are reserved for "comment on the result" in 14-mark problems. Always end with a paragraph stating whether the process is capable and what corrective action to take.
> 6. **Confusing $C_{pm}$ with $C_{pk}$** — remember $C_{pm}$ uses the **target** $T$ (not the midpoint) and the square-root form.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap — Rapid Revision Checklist

- **Process Capability Analysis =** quantitative comparison of process variability to specification variability. **Stability first, then capability**.
- **$C_p$** = $(USL - LSL)/6\hat{\sigma}$ → potential, spread-only, ignores centering.
- **$C_{pk}$** = $\min(C_{PU}, C_{PL})$ → actual capability, accounts for centering.
- **$P_p$ and $P_{pk}$** use the **overall** standard deviation $s$ → long-term real-world performance.
- **$C_{pm}$** (Taguchi) penalizes deviation from the **target** value $T$.
- **Centering index $k$** connects $C_p$ and $C_{pk}$ via $C_{pk} = C_p(1-k)$.
- **Benchmark:** $C_{pk} \ge 1.33$ (industry min), $1.50$ (capable), $2.00$ (Six-Sigma, 3.4 ppm).
- **Within $\hat{\sigma} = \bar{R}/d_2$**: remember $d_2$ for $n = 2$ is 1.128, $n = 3$ is 1.693, $n = 4$ is 2.059, $n = 5$ is 2.326.
- **Gap $C_{pk} - P_{pk}$** signals special-cause variation still present.
- **High $C_p$ + low $C_{pk}$** = capable but **off-center** → fix the mean, not the machine.
- **Low $C_p$ + low $C_{pk}$** = incapable process → reduce $\sigma$ via machine upgrade, tooling, or process redesign.
- **Applications:** CNC machining, SMT electronics, pharma tablets, service industry TAT, IATF 16949 PPAP submissions.
- **Formula priority for the exam:** $C_p$ → $C_{pk}$ (with both CPU and CPL) → $C_{pm}$ → $P_p$ → $P_{pk}$ → interpretation.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process Capability Index
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Process Capability Index

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 📘 Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is Process Capability?

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition (2024 Scheme):**
> **Process Capability** is the measured, inherent reproducibility of a process operating under stable (in statistical control) conditions. It quantifies the ability of a process to produce output that conforms to the design **engineering specifications** (USL, LSL) by comparing the natural process spread (**6σ**) against the specification width (**USL − LSL**).

In simpler words — once your process is stable (no special causes of variation, i.e., the control chart shows only random variation), you ask: *"How wide is the natural spread of my process compared to the tolerance band the customer/engineer allows?"* That ratio is the **Process Capability Index**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy 🍰

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Cake Tin Analogy:**
> Imagine a baker pours cake batter into rectangular tins. The tin's internal length is the **specification width (USL − LSL)**. The actual variation in cake length (after baking, due to oven heat, mixing, etc.) is the **process spread (6σ)**.
> - If the cake is always smaller than the tin → the process is **capable** (low Cp risk).
> - If the cake sometimes overflows the tin → the process is **not capable** (defectives appear).
> - The **Process Capability Index (Cp)** is mathematically how many "tin-widths" of variation fit inside the cake — more than 1.33 is considered excellent for a well-centered process.

The same logic applies to machining a shaft: the lathe's natural diameter variation must be much smaller than the engineering tolerance (e.g., 30 mm ± 0.05 mm) for the part to be accepted consistently.

---

## 1.3 Why is Process Capability Important?

- It bridges the gap between **statistical process control (SPC)** and **product acceptance sampling**.
- It answers the customer's key question: *"Will this supplier's process consistently make parts within my drawing tolerances?"*
- It forms the foundation of the **Six-Sigma** methodology (target Cp = 2.0, i.e., 12σ within the spec).
- It is a direct input to **PPAP, APQP, IATF 16949, and AS9100** audits in the automotive and aerospace industries.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pre-Requisite Check:** Process Capability must ONLY be calculated when the process is proven **statistically in-control** (i.e., the X̄-R or I-MR chart shows no Western Electric / Nelson rule violations). Otherwise, the index is meaningless because special-cause variation inflates the standard deviation falsely.

---

## 1.4 Key Symbols & Notations

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **USL** | Upper Specification Limit | mm, °C, ohms, etc. |
| **LSL** | Lower Specification Limit | mm, °C, ohms, etc. |
| **μ** | Process mean (long-term average) | Same as characteristic |
| **σ** | Process standard deviation | Same as characteristic |
| **T = USL − LSL** | Total tolerance width | Same as characteristic |
| **6σ** | Natural process spread (99.73% under normality) | Same as characteristic |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 📗 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Family of Capability Indices

The KTU 2024 syllabus (Module 2) emphasizes four primary indices. They differ in **how they penalize off-centering** and **how they handle the target value (T₀)**.

### 2.1.1 Cp — The Basic Process Capability Index (Two-Sided Spec)

$$ C_p \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \;=\; \frac{T}{6\sigma} $$

**Logic Steps:**
- **Why 6σ?** Under a Normal distribution, 99.73% of all output lies within μ ± 3σ, so the total natural spread = 6σ.
- **Why USL − LSL?** That is the full "engineering window" the customer permits.
- **Interpretation:** Cp = 1 means the process spread *exactly* fills the specification. Cp = 1.33 leaves a safety margin of 4σ on each side from the spec limit. Cp < 1 ⇒ process is **incapable** (will produce defects).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Limitation of Cp:** Cp is **insensitive to centering**. A process drifting towards USL with Cp = 1.33 can still produce defects! Therefore, Cp is almost always reported **alongside Cpk**, never alone.

---

### 2.1.2 Cpk — The Centering-Adjusted Capability Index

$$ C_{pk} \;=\; \min\!\left( C_{pu},\; C_{pl} \right) \;=\; \min\!\left( \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \right) $$

**Logic Steps:**
- Computes the **one-sided capability** in each direction: how many 3σ's fit between the mean and the nearest spec limit.
- The smaller of the two is **Cpk** — it represents the worst-case side.
- **If Cpk = Cp**, the process is perfectly centered.
- **If Cpk < Cp**, the process is **off-center** (mean has drifted toward one spec limit).

---

### 2.1.3 Cpm — Taguchi's Capability Index (Target-Oriented)

$$ C_{pm} \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sqrt{\sigma^{2} + (\mu - T_{0})^{2}}} $$

**Logic Steps:**
- **T₀** is the **target value** (often the midpoint: T₀ = (USL+LSL)/2).
- The denominator penalizes deviation of μ from T₀, expressing **Taguchi's Quality Loss Philosophy** — any drift from target causes loss to society.
- Cpm ≤ Cp always; Cpm = Cp only if μ = T₀.

---

### 2.1.4 Cr — Process Capability Ratio (Inverse Form, less common)

$$ C_{r} \;=\; \frac{6\sigma}{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}} \;=\; \frac{1}{C_p} $$

Used in older texts and ISO standards; the **reciprocal** of Cp. KTU 2024 prefers Cp, but recognize Cr.

---

## 2.2 One-Sided Specification Indices

When only **one** spec limit exists (e.g., strength ≥ 40 MPa, or pH ≤ 7.0):

$$ C_{pu} \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} \quad\quad C_{pl} \;=\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} $$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Use Case:** Surface roughness (Ra) is often a *one-sided* spec — "Ra must be ≤ 1.6 μm." There is no LSL, so Cp and Cpk degenerate to Cpu only.

---

## 2.3 Capability vs. Performance Indices (Short-Run vs. Long-Run)

| Index | Uses which σ? | When to use? | Symbol Notation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cp, Cpk, Cpm** | **Within-subgroup** (σ̂w) or pooled σ̂ | Short-term / initial process qualification | Subscript: *p* (performance) sometimes used |
| **Pp, Ppk, Ppm** | **Overall / long-term** σ̂ₒₗₜ = √[(Σ(xᵢ−x̄)²)/(n−1)] | Long-term, includes between-subgroup drift | Symbol: capital P |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Always check whether your textbook problem specifies *"short-term"* (use σ̂w from R̄/d₂) or *"long-term"* (use sample s.d. of all data). The KTU examiner may explicitly state this in the problem statement.

**Formula for σ̂w (from R-bar method):**

$$ \hat{\sigma}_{w} \;=\; \frac{\bar{R}}{d_{2}} $$

where **d₂** is a tabulated constant depending on subgroup size *n* (e.g., n=5 → d₂ = 2.326).

**Formula for σ̂ₒₗₜ (from sample data of size N):**

$$ \hat{\sigma}_{olt} \;=\; \sqrt{\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N}(x_{i}-\bar{x})^{2}}{N-1}} $$

---

## 2.4 Six-Sigma Concept & Relationship to Cp

In **Six-Sigma methodology**, the goal is:

$$ C_{pk,\text{target}} \;=\; 2.0 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{12σ within spec limits} $$

This yields a defect rate of **3.4 DPMO** (defects per million opportunities) when allowing for a 1.5σ long-term mean shift. Cp = 1.5 ⇒ Cpk = 1.5 ⇒ 3.4 ppm ≈ the Six-Sigma benchmark.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!NOTE]
> The following is the **authoritative, exam-ready** formula sheet for Process Capability. Memorize column 2 verbatim.

| # | Formula | What it measures | Boundary condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Two-sided spread capability | $\sigma > 0$; assumes centering |
| 2 | $C_{pu} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma}$ | Upper one-sided | If no LSL |
| 3 | $C_{pl} = \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}$ | Lower one-sided | If no USL |
| 4 | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\;\dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Two-sided with centering | $C_{pk} \le C_p$ |
| 5 | $C_{pm} = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sqrt{\sigma^{2} + (\mu - T_{0})^{2}}}$ | Taguchi target loss | $T_0$ is nominal target |
| 6 | $P_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\hat{\sigma}_{olt}}$ | Long-term performance | Uses overall σ̂ |
| 7 | $P_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\hat{\sigma}_{olt}},\;\dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\hat{\sigma}_{olt}}\right)$ | Long-term performance, centered | $P_{pk} \le P_p$ |
| 8 | $\hat{\sigma}_{w} = \dfrac{\bar{R}}{d_{2}}$ | Short-term within σ̂ | d₂ table value |

**Capability Interpretation Standard (Automotive IATF 16949 / KTU accepted):**

| Cpk Value | Process Rating | Defect Rate (ppm, normal) |
|---|---|---|
| **< 1.00** | Incapable | > 66,807 |
| 1.00 – 1.33 | Marginally capable | 2,700 – 66,807 |
| 1.33 – 1.67 | Capable | 63 – 2,700 |
| **≥ 1.67** | Highly capable (Six-Sigma territory) | ≤ 63 |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application | Index used |
|---|---|---|
| CNC Machining | Shaft diameter control | Cpk, Cpm |
| Semiconductor | Wafer thickness | Cpk, Ppk |
| Chemical | pH control | Cpu (one-sided) |
| Pharma | Tablet weight | Cpk |
| Automotive (IATF 16949) | PPAP submission requires Cpk ≥ 1.67 for safety-critical | Cpk |
| Six-Sigma Green Belt | Process improvement DMAIC projects | Ppk → Cpk reduction in variation |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 📙 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Solved Problems & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Cp (Conceptual)

**Given:** A normally distributed process with mean μ and standard deviation σ.
**Engineering tolerance:** [LSL, USL].

The fraction of the process falling **outside** the spec limits is the **tail area**:

$$ P(\text{Defect}) \;=\; P(x < \text{LSL}) + P(x > \text{USL}) $$

For the process to be "capable" with zero defects, we need:

$$ \mu + 3\sigma \;\le\; \text{USL} \quad\text{AND}\quad \mu - 3\sigma \;\ge\; \text{LSL} $$

This is equivalent to:

$$ \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \;\ge\; 1 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; C_p \;\ge\; 1 $$

Hence Cp is mathematically defined as the ratio of the spec window to the natural process spread. The factor **6** comes from ±3σ covering 99.73% under the normal curve. ∎

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Cpk as a Centering Refinement

Start from the one-sided Z-equivalents:

$$ Z_{\text{USL}} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma},\qquad Z_{\text{LSL}} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{\sigma} $$

These Z values, divided by 3, represent **how many 3σ's of safety** exist on each side:

$$ \frac{Z_{\text{USL}}}{3} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = C_{pu}, \qquad \frac{Z_{\text{LSL}}}{3} = \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} = C_{pl} $$

The process can only be as good as its **worst side**, hence:

$$ C_{pk} = \min(C_{pu},\, C_{pl}) $$

If the mean is exactly at center, $C_{pu} = C_{pl} = C_p$, and thus $C_{pk} = C_p$. ∎

---

## 3.3 Solved Problem 1 — Basic Cp & Cpk (KTU Pattern)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Pattern]**
> A CNC turning process produces shafts with specification **30.00 ± 0.05 mm**. A preliminary capability study yields:
> - Process mean μ̂ = 30.012 mm
> - Standard deviation (within) σ̂w = 0.012 mm
> **Calculate (a) Cp, (b) Cpk, and (c) comment on process capability and centering.**

**Solution:**

**Given:**
- USL = 30.05 mm
- LSL = 29.95 mm
- μ̂ = 30.012 mm
- σ̂w = 0.012 mm

### Part (a): Cp

$$ C_p \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} \;=\; \frac{30.05 - 29.95}{6 \times 0.012} $$

$$ C_p \;=\; \frac{0.10}{0.072} \;=\; 1.389 $$

### Part (b): Cpk

First, calculate $C_{pu}$:

$$ C_{pu} \;=\; \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} \;=\; \frac{30.05 - 30.012}{3 \times 0.012} \;=\; \frac{0.038}{0.036} \;=\; 1.056 $$

Then, calculate $C_{pl}$:

$$ C_{pl} \;=\; \frac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma} \;=\; \frac{30.012 - 29.95}{3 \times 0.012} \;=\; \frac{0.062}{0.036} \;=\; 1.722 $$

Now, take the minimum:

$$ C_{pk} \;=\; \min(1.056,\; 1.722) \;=\; 1.056 $$

### Part (c): Interpretation

- $C_p = 1.389$: The process spread fits inside the spec window with ~28% safety margin. **Capable.**
- $C_{pk} = 1.056$: Significantly lower than Cp → the process is **off-center** (shifted toward USL).
- **Action:** Re-center the process. A 0.012 mm downward adjustment would raise Cpk to ≈ Cp = 1.389.

---

## 3.4 Solved Problem 2 — Six-Sigma Defect Rate Calculation

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question]**
> A process has μ = 100.0, σ = 2.0, with specification **95 ≤ X ≤ 105**. Find Cp, Cpk, and the expected defect rate in ppm.

**Solution:**

$$ C_p = \frac{105 - 95}{6 \times 2} = \frac{10}{12} = 0.833 $$

$$ C_{pu} = \frac{105 - 100}{3 \times 2} = \frac{5}{6} = 0.833 $$

$$ C_{pl} = \frac{100 - 95}{3 \times 2} = \frac{5}{6} = 0.833 $$

$$ C_{pk} = 0.833 \quad (\text{perfectly centered}) $$

**Defect Rate (normal distribution):**
Z for USL: $Z = \frac{105 - 100}{2} = 2.5$
Z for LSL: $Z = \frac{100 - 95}{2} = 2.5$

From standard normal table: P(Z > 2.5) = 0.00621

Total defect probability = 2 × 0.00621 = 0.01242 = **12,420 ppm**

> **Conclusion:** Cp < 1 → process is **incapable**; action required to halve σ.

---

## 3.5 Solved Problem 3 — Cpm (Taguchi) Calculation

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]**
> Specification: 50 ± 10 (LSL = 40, USL = 60). Target T₀ = 50. Process: μ = 51.5, σ = 3.0. Calculate Cp, Cpk, and Cpm.

**Solution:**

$$ C_p = \frac{60 - 40}{6 \times 3.0} = \frac{20}{18} = 1.111 $$

$$ C_{pu} = \frac{60 - 51.5}{3 \times 3.0} = \frac{8.5}{9} = 0.944 $$

$$ C_{pl} = \frac{51.5 - 40}{3 \times 3.0} = \frac{11.5}{9} = 1.278 $$

$$ C_{pk} = \min(0.944,\, 1.278) = 0.944 $$

$$ C_{pm} = \frac{60 - 40}{6\sqrt{\sigma^{2} + (\mu - T_{0})^{2}}} = \frac{20}{6\sqrt{9 + (1.5)^{2}}} $$

$$ C_{pm} = \frac{20}{6\sqrt{9 + 2.25}} = \frac{20}{6\sqrt{11.25}} = \frac{20}{6 \times 3.354} = \frac{20}{20.125} = 0.994 $$

**Observation:** $C_{pm} \le C_{pk} \le C_p$ — this ordering is always true when the mean drifts from the target. The Taguchi index correctly penalizes the 1.5 mm drift.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flowchart Logic

> [!TIP]
> **Decision Sequence to Choose the Correct Index (KTU 2024 High-Yield Skill):**

| If the problem says... | Use this index | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| "Two-sided spec, assume centered" | Cp | Simplest spread check |
| "Two-sided spec, mean ≠ midpoint" | Cpk | Adjusts for off-centering |
| "Target value given / quality loss" | Cpm | Penalizes drift from target |
| "One-sided spec (only USL)" | Cpu | No LSL exists |
| "One-sided spec (only LSL)" | Cpl | No USL exists |
| "Long-term / overall data" | Pp, Ppk | Uses overall σ, not within |
| "Subgroup R-bar given" | σ̂w = R̄/d₂, then Cp, Cpk | Within-subgroup estimate |

---

## 3.7 Quick-Reference: σ̂w from R̄/d₂ Table

| Subgroup size n | d₂ | 1/d₂ |
|---|---|---|
| 2 | 1.128 | 0.886 |
| 3 | 1.693 | 0.591 |
| 4 | 2.059 | 0.486 |
| **5** | **2.326** | **0.430** |
| 6 | 2.534 | 0.395 |
| 7 | 2.704 | 0.370 |
| 10 | 3.078 | 0.325 |

(For KTU, n = 5 and d₂ = 2.326 is the most frequently tested.)

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 📕 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The Capability Concept Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Process in Statistical Control] --> B{Is Spec One-Sided or Two-Sided?}
    B -- One-Sided USL Only --> C[Compute Cpu = USL minus mu divided by 3 sigma]
    B -- One-Sided LSL Only --> D[Compute Cpl = mu minus LSL divided by 3 sigma]
    B -- Two-Sided Spec --> E{Is Target Value T-zero defined?}
    E -- No --> F[Compute Cp AND Cpk]
    E -- Yes --> G[Compute Cp, Cpk, and Cpm]
    C --> H[Interpret against IATF thresholds]
    D --> H
    F --> H
    G --> H
    H --> I{Cpk Value?}
    I -- Less than 1.00 --> J[Process Incapable: Reduce Sigma and Re-center]
    I -- 1.00 to 1.33 --> K[Marginally Capable: Continuous Improvement]
    I -- 1.33 to 1.67 --> L[Capable: Maintain Control]
    I -- Greater or Equal 1.67 --> M[Highly Capable: Six Sigma Territory]
```

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Process Spread vs. Specification Window

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SPEC["Specification Window: T = USL minus LSL"]
        L1[LSL] --- L2[USL]
    end
    subgraph PROC["Process Spread: 6 sigma = mu minus 3 sigma to mu plus 3 sigma"]
        P1[mu minus 3 sigma] --- P2[mu plus 3 sigma]
    end
    L1 -. Cp Less than 1: Process WIDER than Spec .-> P1
    L2 -. Cp Equal 1: Process EQUALS Spec .-> P2
    L1 -. Cp Greater than 1: Process NARROWER than Spec .-> P2
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Cp vs. Cpk Decision Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Compute Cp] --> S2{Is Cp greater than 1.33?}
    S2 -- No --> X1[Process NOT capable: STOP or fix]
    S2 -- Yes --> S3[Compute Cpu and Cpl]
    S3 --> S4{Is Cpk Approx Equal Cp?}
    S4 -- Yes --> Y1[Process Centered AND Capable: ACCEPT]
    S4 -- No --> Y2[Process Capable but OFF-CENTER: Re-center mean, then re-check]
    Y2 --> S3
```

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Short-Term vs. Long-Term Capability

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Collect Process Data] --> B{Subgroups or Individual Values?}
    B -- Subgroups Available --> C[Compute R-bar per subgroup]
    C --> D[sigma-hat WITHIN equals R-bar divided by d2]
    D --> E[Use Cp, Cpk, Cpm with sigma-within]
    B -- All Data Treated as One Set --> F[sigma-hat OVERALL equals sample std deviation]
    F --> G[Use Pp, Ppk with sigma-overall]
    E --> H[Report Short-Term Capability]
    G --> I[Report Long-Term Performance]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 📒 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Process Capability Index (Cp). Why is Cp alone insufficient to judge a process's true capability?*

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** The Process Capability Index Cp is the ratio of the engineering specification width to the natural process spread (6σ) of a stable process:

$$ C_p = \frac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma} $$

**Why Cp is insufficient:**
1. Cp assumes the process is **perfectly centered** between USL and LSL.
2. It does **not detect mean shift** — a process with mean near the USL can still have Cp = 1.33 but produce defectives.
3. Cp **ignores the target value** and Taguchi's quality loss concept.
4. Therefore, Cp is **always reported alongside Cpk** for a true assessment.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between Cp, Cpk, and Cpm. State the relationship among them when the process mean is off-center.*

**Model Answer:**

| Index | Formula | Focus |
|---|---|---|
| Cp | (USL−LSL)/6σ | Spread only |
| Cpk | min((USL−μ)/3σ, (μ−LSL)/3σ) | Spread + centering |
| Cpm | (USL−LSL)/6√(σ² + (μ−T₀)²) | Spread + target deviation |

**Relationship when μ ≠ T₀ (off-center):**

$$ C_{pm} \;\le\; C_{pk} \;\le\; C_{p} $$

Cpm penalizes the off-centering most strongly because of the (μ−T₀)² term in its denominator.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### 🅰️ Question A (14 Marks) — Full-Length with Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**
*In an automotive component manufacturing line, a piston diameter is specified as **50.00 ± 0.04 mm**. A capability study yields 25 subgroups of size 5. The mean of subgroup means is x̄̄ = 50.012 mm and the average range is R̄ = 0.020 mm.*

**(a) Compute the Process Capability Indices Cp, Cpu, Cpl, and Cpk. Comment on the process capability and centering. (7 marks)**

**(b) If the management targets a Cpk of 1.67 (Six-Sigma level), what should be the new process standard deviation σ? Comment on the practical implications. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given Data:**
- USL = 50.04 mm, LSL = 49.96 mm
- x̄̄ = 50.012 mm
- R̄ = 0.020 mm
- Subgroup size n = 5, so **d₂ = 2.326**

**Step 1 — Estimate σ̂w (within-subgroup):**

$$ \hat{\sigma}_{w} = \frac{\bar{R}}{d_{2}} = \frac{0.020}{2.326} = 0.00860 \;\text{mm} $$

### Part (a):

**Cp calculation:**

$$ C_{p} = \frac{50.04 - 49.96}{6 \times 0.00860} = \frac{0.08}{0.0516} = 1.550 $$

**Cpu calculation:**

$$ C_{pu} = \frac{50.04 - 50.012}{3 \times 0.00860} = \frac{0.028}{0.0258} = 1.085 $$

**Cpl calculation:**

$$ C_{pl} = \frac{50.012 - 49.96}{3 \times 0.00860} = \frac{0.052}{0.0258} = 2.016 $$

**Cpk calculation:**

$$ C_{pk} = \min(1.085,\, 2.016) = 1.085 $$

**Comments:**
- $C_p = 1.550 > 1.33$: Process **spread is acceptable**.
- $C_{pk} = 1.085 < C_p$: Process is **off-center**, shifted toward USL.
- **Re-centering is mandatory** to raise Cpk to ~1.55.

### Part (b):

**Target Cpk = 1.67**, with Cpu being the limiting side (process is shifted up).

Setting $C_{pu} = 1.67$:

$$ C_{pu} = \frac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma} = 1.67 $$

Re-arranging for σ (with the current shift of 0.028 mm preserved):

$$ \sigma = \frac{0.028}{3 \times 1.67} = \frac{0.028}{5.01} = 0.00559 \;\text{mm} $$

**Practical implications:**
1. The required σ is **0.00559 mm vs. current 0.00860 mm** — a **35% reduction in variation**.
2. Achieving this typically requires:
   - New CNC machine with higher precision (e.g., 5-axis vs. 3-axis).
   - Improved tool material (e.g., diamond-coated inserts).
   - Tighter temperature control in the shop floor (thermal expansion of steel ≈ 12 µm/m·°C).
   - Statistical process control (SPC) charts to maintain the new σ.
3. The cost of quality (COQ) increases significantly — management must perform a **cost–benefit analysis** versus tightening the specification instead.

---

### 🅱️ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Internal Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question as per 2024 Scheme]**
*A pharmaceutical company manufactures tablets with target weight T₀ = 500 mg. The specification is **495 ≤ W ≤ 510 mg**. A sample of 30 tablets gave: x̄ = 502.5 mg, s = 1.8 mg.*

**(a) Calculate Cp, Cpk, and Cpm. Compare and comment. (7 marks)**

**(b) Estimate the expected defects per million (DPMO) using the standard normal table. (7 marks)**

#### Model Solution:

**Given:**
- USL = 510 mg, LSL = 495 mg, T₀ = 500 mg
- x̄ = 502.5 mg, s = 1.8 mg
- Sample size n = 30 (use s as σ̂ for long-term estimate)

### Part (a):

**Cp:**

$$ C_p = \frac{510 - 495}{6 \times 1.8} = \frac{15}{10.8} = 1.389 $$

**Cpk:**

$$ C_{pu} = \frac{510 - 502.5}{3 \times 1.8} = \frac{7.5}{5.4} = 1.389 $$

$$ C_{pl} = \frac{502.5 - 495}{3 \times 1.8} = \frac{7.5}{5.4} = 1.389 $$

$$ C_{pk} = \min(1.389,\, 1.389) = 1.389 $$

**Cpm:**

$$ C_{pm} = \frac{510 - 495}{6\sqrt{1.8^{2} + (502.5 - 500)^{2}}} = \frac{15}{6\sqrt{3.24 + 6.25}} $$

$$ C_{pm} = \frac{15}{6\sqrt{9.49}} = \frac{15}{6 \times 3.081} = \frac{15}{18.484} = 0.812 $$

**Comparison & Comments:**
- $C_p = C_{pk} = 1.389$: Process is **centered** and **capable**.
- $C_{pm} = 0.812$: The mean is **0.5 mg above target**, and Cpm penalizes this — it indicates **Taguchi quality loss** even though the spec is met.
- **Implication:** The process should be re-centered to T₀ = 500 mg to satisfy the **target value** requirement of the customer.

### Part (b): DPMO Calculation

**Z-values for the two spec limits:**

$$ Z_{\text{USL}} = \frac{510 - 502.5}{1.8} = 4.167 $$

$$ Z_{\text{LSL}} = \frac{502.5 - 495}{1.8} = 4.167 $$

**From standard normal table:**
P(Z > 4.167) ≈ 0.0000155 (linear interpolation between 4.16 and 4.17)
Standard tables: P(Z > 4.0) = 0.0000317; P(Z > 4.5) = 0.0000034
Approximation at 4.167: ≈ 1.55 × 10⁻⁵

**Total defect probability (one-tail) = 1.55 × 10⁻⁵**

**DPMO = 1.55 × 10⁻⁵ × 10⁶ = 15.5 ppm per side**

**Total DPMO = 15.5 + 15.5 = 31 ppm**

**Comment:** 31 ppm falls within the **"highly capable" Six-Sigma range (≤ 63 ppm)** for safety margin evaluation.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where KTU Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Using wrong σ:** KTU problems sometimes give both R̄ and s. Decide whether to use σ̂w (R̄/d₂) or σ̂ₒₗₜ (s) based on the question wording: "short-term" vs. "long-term" / "performance."
> 2. **Forgetting the min() in Cpk:** Many students compute Cpu AND Cpl but report the larger one. **Cpk is always the MINIMUM.**
> 3. **Using |μ − T₀| in Cpm:** Some students forget the **squared** term (μ − T₀)² in the denominator.
> 4. **Not stating "process must be in-control first":** In theory/proof questions, the 2-mark answer includes the prerequisite of statistical control.
> 5. **Misreading units:** σ̂ and USL/LSL must be in the **same units** (e.g., both in mm, not mm and cm).
> 6. **Confusing Cp with Pp:** Capital P = long-term; lowercase p (or no subscript) = short-term.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✍️

- ⭐ **Pre-requisite:** Process MUST be in statistical control (X̄-R or I-MR chart clean) before computing any capability index.
- ⭐ **Cp = (USL − LSL) / 6σ** — measures spread only, assumes centering.
- ⭐ **Cpk = min((USL−μ)/3σ, (μ−LSL)/3σ)** — adds centering awareness; **always ≤ Cp**.
- ⭐ **Cpm** adds Taguchi's quality loss via the (μ − T₀)² penalty; always ≤ Cpk when off-target.
- ⭐ **Industry benchmarks:** Cpk ≥ 1.33 = capable; ≥ 1.67 = highly capable (Six-Sigma).
- ⭐ **σ̂w = R̄ / d₂** (n=5 → d₂ = 2.326) for short-term; **σ̂ₒₗₜ = s** for long-term (Pp, Ppk).
- ⭐ **Six-Sigma target:** Cpk = 2.0 (12σ within spec) → 3.4 DPMO with 1.5σ shift.
- ⭐ **Ordering when off-center:** $C_{pm} \le C_{pk} \le C_{p}$.
- ⭐ **One-sided spec:** If only USL → use Cpu; if only LSL → use Cpl.
- ⭐ **Units must be consistent** throughout the calculation.
- ⭐ **Interpretation step** is mandatory in KTU 14-mark answers — *never* end with just a number.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Acceptance Sampling by Variables and Attributes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Acceptance Sampling by Variables and Attributes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 2 — ACCEPTANCE SAMPLING BY VARIABLES AND ATTRIBUTES

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

### Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Sampling** is a statistical quality control procedure in which a **random sample** is drawn from a production lot (or batch), inspected, and based on the number of defectives (or measured quality characteristics) found, a decision is made to either **Accept** the lot or **Reject** it. It is a *decision-making tool* applied to **incoming raw materials, in-process components, and finished goods** when 100% inspection is impossible, uneconomical, or destructive in nature.

Two principal branches exist in the KTU 2024 framework:

| Branch | What is Measured? | Decision Rule |
|---|---|---|
| **By Attributes** | Each item is classified as *defective* or *non-defective* (binary go / no-go). | Compare number of defectives `d` against acceptance number `c`. |
| **By Variables** | A measurable quality characteristic (length, weight, strength) is read on a continuous scale. | Compare sample mean / standard deviation against specification limits. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Go/No-Go Inspection** — A binary classification inspection where each unit is judged only as *pass* or *fail*, with no intermediate categories. This forms the foundation of attribute sampling.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **Kerala Spices Export Quality Officer** inspecting a consignment of 5,000 kg of cardamom before shipping to the European Union. Tasting every single gram is impossible (and destroys the product!). Instead, you:

1. Randomly pick a small handful (say 200 grams) — this is your **sample**.
2. Count the seeds that are discoloured, broken, or below size — this is **inspection by attributes**.
3. OR you weigh, measure moisture, and test oil content — this is **inspection by variables**.
4. If fewer than a threshold (say 3) of the 200 g are defective → **Accept** the whole lot; otherwise **Reject**.

The acceptance sampling plan tells you *how many* to pick (`n`) and *how many defects to tolerate* (`c`) so that bad lots are rejected most of the time, and good lots are accepted most of the time.

### Key Metrics and Constants

- **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level)** — the worst tolerable process average that is still considered acceptable. Producer's benchmark.
- **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective)** — the worst quality level the consumer is willing to tolerate. Consumer's benchmark.
- **AOQL (Average Outgoing Quality Limit)** — the *worst possible* average quality of lots leaving the inspection station, after replacing rejected lots with perfect ones.
- **α (Producer's Risk)** — probability of rejecting a *good* lot (Type-I error). Usually set at **0.05**.
- **β (Consumer's Risk)** — probability of accepting a *bad* lot (Type-II error). Usually set at **0.10**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Why Do We Need Acceptance Sampling? (The "Why")

- **100% inspection is infeasible** for destructive tests (bulb life, bullet impact, fuse testing).
- **Cost of 100% inspection** often exceeds the cost of the defect itself.
- **Fatigue in human inspectors** leads to higher error rates on long runs.
- Acceptance sampling provides a **statistically defensible compromise** between *no inspection* and *full inspection*.

## 2.2 The Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve — The Heart of Acceptance Sampling

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **OC Curve** is a plot of the **probability of acceptance $P_a$** of a lot (Y-axis) versus the **incoming lot quality $p$** (X-axis, expressed as fraction or percent defective). Every acceptance sampling plan has a unique OC curve.

### Interpretation Points on the OC Curve

- When lot quality is **excellent** ($p$ is very low) → $P_a \approx 1$ (lot almost always accepted).
- When lot quality is **terrible** ($p$ is very high) → $P_a \approx 0$ (lot almost always rejected).
- The curve is **S-shaped (sigmoidal)** for attribute plans.

### The Two Risk Points

| Symbol | Name | Coordinates on OC Curve | KTU Convention |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| $p_1$ | Acceptable Quality Level (AQL) | Point where $P_a = 1 - \alpha$ | $\alpha = 0.05$ |
| $p_2$ | Lot Tolerance Percent Defective (LTPD) | Point where $P_a = \beta$ | $\beta = 0.10$ |
| $IQL$ | Indifference Quality Level | Point where $P_a = 0.50$ | Producer & Consumer equally willing |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Favourite:** "Sketch and explain the OC curve. Mark AQL, LTPD, Producer's and Consumer's risks." This question appears almost every academic year.

## 2.3 Sampling by Attributes — Plan Types

### A. Single Sampling Plan (n, c)

- **Procedure:** Take `n` units. Count defectives `d`. If $d \le c$ → Accept; else Reject.
- **Probability of acceptance** under binomial (or Poisson approximation):
- **Hypergeometric** (small lot, finite correction): 

### B. Double Sampling Plan $(n_1, n_2, c_1, c_2)$

- First sample of $n_1$: accept if $d_1 \le c_1$, reject if $d_1 \ge c_2$, otherwise draw second sample $n_2$.
- Combined acceptance on $d_1 + d_2 \le c_2$.

### C. Multiple / Sequential Sampling Plan

- Several stages, smaller samples per stage, quicker decisions on very good or very bad lots.

## 2.4 Sampling by Variables — Two Approaches

### A. Standard Deviation $\sigma$ KNOWN (k-method / known-sigma plan)

- Acceptance criterion involves the **k-factor** (multiplier of standard deviation).
- Used when the process is in statistical control and historical $\sigma$ is reliable.

### B. Standard Deviation $\sigma$ UNKNOWN (t-method / unknown-sigma plan)

- Uses Student's t-distribution; sample standard deviation $s$ replaces $\sigma$.
- More realistic for small samples.

## 2.5 Famous Standard Plans (KTU Must-Know)

| Standard | Type | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **MIL-STD-105E** (now ANSI/ASQ Z1.4) | Attribute | Most widely used; tabulated by AQL and lot size; replaced by **ANSI Z1.4-2003 / ISO 2859-1**. |
| **MIL-STD-414** (now ANSI/ASQ Z1.9) | Variable | Companion to 105E for variables; tabulated by AQL and lot size. |
| **Dodge–Romig Tables** | Attribute | Designed for LTPD-based or AOQL-based protection; classic pre-MIL-STD system. |
| **ANSI/ASQ Z1.4** | Attribute | Current US industry standard (successor to MIL-STD-105E). |

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL TABLE — Memorise this for KTU 2024 Exams**

| # | Concept | Formula | Description |
|:---:|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hypergeometric $P_a$ | $P_a = \dfrac{\binom{D}{d}\binom{N-D}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}$ | $N$=lot size, $D$=defectives in lot, $n$=sample, $d$=defectives in sample. Used when $n/N > 0.10$. |
| 2 | Binomial $P_a$ | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c}\binom{n}{d}p^{d}(1-p)^{n-d}$ | Used when lot is large ($n/N \le 0.10$) and sampling with replacement. |
| 3 | Poisson $P_a$ (approx.) | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$ | Used when $n \ge 16$ and $p \le 0.10$; **fastest computation**. |
| 4 | Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) | $AOQ = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | $P_a \cdot p$ is incoming defect proportion that passes; assumes rejected lots are 100% screened. |
| 5 | Average Outgoing Quality Limit | $AOQL = \max_p (AOQ)$ | The maximum value of AOQ as $p$ varies from 0 to 1. |
| 6 | Average Total Inspection (ATI) | $ATI = n + (1 - P_a)(N - n)$ | For 100% screening of rejected lots. |
| 7 | Producer's Risk | $\alpha = 1 - P_a(p_1)$ | At AQL, probability of *rejecting* a good lot. |
| 8 | Consumer's Risk | $\beta = P_a(p_2)$ | At LTPD, probability of *accepting* a bad lot. |
| 9 | Sample size (k-method, $\sigma$ known, one-sided) | $n = \left(\dfrac{z_{\alpha}+z_{\beta}}{z_p}\right)^{2}$ (function of $p_1, p_2$) | Used for designing variable plans with known $\sigma$. |
| 10 | Defect rate for $c=0$ (Poisson) | $P_a = e^{-np}$ | Simplest possible plan. |

> [!NOTE]
> Always substitute $\vert x \vert$ with `\vert` or `\mid` in your LaTeX — the pipe character `$\vert x \vert$` will not break markdown table rendering.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electronics manufacturing**: Sampling ICs for solder-joint defects (MIL-STD-105E).
- **Automotive (Toyota, Tata Motors)**: Incoming fastener inspection (AQL-based attribute plans).
- **Pharmaceuticals**: Sterility testing (variable plans with tight LSL/USL).
- **Civil Engineering**: Concrete cube compressive strength testing (variable plan with known-sigma).
- **Aerospace**: Turbine blade lot acceptance (variable plan with destructive fatigue tests).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Probability of Acceptance under Binomial Distribution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Frequently Asked Derivation** (worth 4–5 marks)

### Setup

In a large lot (or with replacement) of size $N$ where $N \to \infty$, the sampling can be modelled as **independent Bernoulli trials** with constant defective probability $p$. Let $d$ be the number of defectives observed in a sample of size $n$.

The probability of exactly $d$ defectives in $n$ trials is given by the binomial PMF:

$$P(d) = \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}$$

### Acceptance Rule for a Single Sampling Plan $(n, c)$

The lot is **accepted** if and only if $d \le c$. By the **additivity axiom of probability** (mutually exclusive outcomes $d = 0, 1, 2, \ldots, c$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_a(p) &= P(d \le c) \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{d=0}^{c} P(d) \\[4pt]
&= \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Operating Characteristic function** for the single sampling plan $(n, c)$.

### Why Poisson Approximation Works

For large $n$ (say $n \ge 16$) and small $p$ (say $p \le 0.10$), with $\lambda = np$ fixed:

$$P(d) = \binom{n}{d} p^{d} (1-p)^{n-d} \;\longrightarrow\; \frac{\lambda^{d} e^{-\lambda}}{d!}$$

Hence the OC function becomes:

$$P_a(p) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \frac{e^{-np}(np)^{d}}{d!}$$

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — Single Sampling by Attributes (Poisson)

**Problem.** A lot of 2000 items contains 5% defectives. A single sampling plan $(n = 50, c = 1)$ is used. Using the Poisson approximation, find $P_a$.

**Solution.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= np = 50 \times 0.05 = 2.5 \\[4pt]
P_a &= P(d = 0) + P(d = 1) \\[4pt]
P(d = 0) &= e^{-2.5} \cdot \frac{(2.5)^0}{0!} = e^{-2.5} = 0.0821 \\[4pt]
P(d = 1) &= e^{-2.5} \cdot \frac{(2.5)^1}{1!} = 0.0821 \times 2.5 = 0.2052 \\[4pt]
P_a &= 0.0821 + 0.2052 = \mathbf{0.2873}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Interpretation:** There is only a 28.73% chance that this 5% defective lot is accepted by plan (50, 1). Good — the plan is rejecting it 71.27% of the time.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — Computing Producer's & Consumer's Risk

**Problem.** A plan $(n = 80, c = 2)$ is used. Compute $\alpha$ at AQL = 1% and $\beta$ at LTPD = 6%. Use Poisson.

**Solution.**

**Step 1:** At AQL ($p_1 = 0.01$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_1 &= np_1 = 80 \times 0.01 = 0.8 \\[4pt]
P_a(p_1) &= e^{-0.8}\left[\frac{(0.8)^0}{0!} + \frac{(0.8)^1}{1!} + \frac{(0.8)^2}{2!}\right] \\[4pt]
&= e^{-0.8}\left[1 + 0.8 + 0.32\right] \\[4pt]
&= 0.4493 \times 2.12 = 0.9525 \\[4pt]
\alpha &= 1 - P_a(p_1) = 1 - 0.9525 = \mathbf{0.0475 \;\;(\approx 5\%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2:** At LTPD ($p_2 = 0.06$):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda_2 &= np_2 = 80 \times 0.06 = 4.8 \\[4pt]
P_a(p_2) &= e^{-4.8}\left[1 + 4.8 + \frac{(4.8)^2}{2}\right] \\[4pt]
&= e^{-4.8}\left[1 + 4.8 + 11.52\right] \\[4pt]
&= 0.00823 \times 17.32 = 0.1425 \\[4pt]
\beta &= P_a(p_2) = \mathbf{0.1425 \;\;(\approx 14.25\%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Plan

| Inspection Cost | Lot Size | Defect Criticality | Recommended Plan Type |
|---|---|---|---|
| Low | Small | Low (cosmetic) | Single attribute, larger $c$ |
| Moderate | Medium | Moderate (functional) | Double attribute, MIL-STD-105E |
| High (destructive) | Large | Critical (safety) | Variable, small $n$, tight $c$ |
| Very High | Any | Very Critical | Skip-lot (skipping accepted suppliers) |

## 3.5 Sample Size Determination for Variables (Known $\sigma$, Two-Sided Limits)

Given $L$ = lower specification limit, $U$ = upper specification limit, $\mu$ = process mean, $\sigma$ = known standard deviation, the **k-factor method** says:

- Compute $k_L = \dfrac{\mu - L}{\sigma}$ and $k_U = \dfrac{U - \mu}{\sigma}$.
- The lot is accepted if both sample mean $\bar{x}$ lies within $(L + k_L \cdot \sigma, U - k_U \cdot \sigma)$.
- For symmetric tolerances and $\mu$ at midpoint, $k_L = k_U = k$.
- Sample size $n$ comes from a standard normal table such that $P_a(p_1) = 1 - \alpha$ and $P_a(p_2) = \beta$.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Acceptance Sampling Decision Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lot Arrives at Inspection Station] --> B[Lot Size N Identified]
    B --> C[Choose Sampling Plan: n c from MIL-STD or Dodge-Romig]
    C --> D[Draw Random Sample of n Units]
    D --> E{Inspection Type}
    E -->|Attribute| F[Count Defectives d]
    E -->|Variable| G[Measure Quality Characteristic x-bar and s]
    F --> H{d less than equal to c}
    G --> I{x-bar within k-sigma Limits}
    H -->|Yes| J[ACCEPT Lot]
    H -->|No| K[REJECT Lot]
    I -->|Yes| J
    I -->|No| K
    J --> L[Move Lot to Next Process]
    K --> M[Send Lot for 100% Screening or Return to Supplier]
    M --> N[Record AOQ and ATI for SPC Database]
    L --> N
```

## 4.2 OC Curve Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Incoming Lot Quality p] -->|low p| B[Pa approx 1.0]
    A -->|p = AQL p1| C[Pa = 1 minus alpha approx 0.95]
    A -->|p = p2 LTPD| D[Pa = beta approx 0.10]
    A -->|high p| E[Pa approx 0.0]
    subgraph S_SHAPED[OC Curve S Shape]
    B
    C
    D
    E
    end
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Variables Sampling Procedure

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Material Lot] --> B[Define USL and LSL Specifications]
    B --> C{Known Sigma}
    C -->|Yes| D[Use k Method with z Tables]
    C -->|No| E[Use t Method with Student Distribution]
    D --> F[Draw Sample of n]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Compute x-bar and s]
    G --> H{Is x-bar within L plus k times sigma to U minus k times sigma}
    H -->|Yes| I[ACCEPT]
    H -->|No| J[REJECT]
```

## 4.4 Sampling Plan Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Acceptance Sampling] --> B[By Attributes]
    A --> C[By Variables]
    B --> B1[Single Plan n c]
    B --> B2[Double Plan n1 n2 c1 c2]
    B --> B3[Multiple Plan]
    B --> B4[Sequential Plan]
    C --> C1[Sigma Known k Method]
    C --> C2[Sigma Unknown t Method]
    C --> C3[Single or Double Limit]
    B1 --> D[MIL-STD-105E]
    B2 --> D
    B3 --> D
    C1 --> E[MIL-STD-414]
    C2 --> E
    D --> F[Current: ANSI Z1.4]
    E --> G[Current: ANSI Z1.9]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam — Model Paper Pattern (Module 2)**

### Q1. Define Acceptance Sampling. List any four advantages of acceptance sampling over 100% inspection.
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**
Acceptance Sampling is a statistical procedure of inspecting a randomly drawn sample from a lot and using the inspection result to decide whether to accept or reject the entire lot.

**Advantages:**
1. **Economical** — far less inspection effort than 100%, drastically reducing cost.
2. **Practical for destructive tests** — e.g., fuse testing, bulb life tests where every unit cannot be destroyed.
3. **Reduces inspector fatigue** — smaller samples mean better attention and fewer errors.
4. **Provides a quantitative measure** of lot quality (via OC curve) and is **statistically defensible** in supplier-customer disputes.
5. *(Bonus)* Applicable to a wide range of lot sizes and quality characteristics.

---

### Q2. Distinguish between Acceptance Sampling by Attributes and Acceptance Sampling by Variables.
**[3 Marks]**

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | By Attributes | By Variables |
|---|---|---|
| What is measured | Each unit is classified as defective or non-defective | A measured quality characteristic (length, weight, etc.) |
| Data type | Discrete (counts of defectives) | Continuous (measurements) |
| Sample size | Larger (since info per unit is less) | Smaller (more info per unit) |
| Plans | MIL-STD-105E, Dodge-Romig, ANSI Z1.4 | MIL-STD-414, ANSI Z1.9 |
| Information | Less informative (pass/fail) | More informative (magnitude of deviation) |
| Difficulty | Easier to apply | Requires knowledge of $\sigma$ or use of t-tables |

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve**. With a neat sketch, mark the **AQL, LTPD, Producer's Risk ($\alpha$), and Consumer's Risk ($\beta$)** points. Discuss the **ideal OC curve** versus the **actual OC curve**.
**[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A single sampling plan $(n = 60, c = 2)$ is to be applied to lots of size 1000. Using the Poisson approximation, compute:
(i) The probability of acceptance when $p = 0.02$.
(ii) The Producer's risk at AQL = 2% ($\alpha = 0.05$ check).
(iii) The Consumer's risk at LTPD = 8%.
**[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question A**

**Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

The **Operating Characteristic (OC) curve** is a graphical representation of a sampling plan's discriminating power. It plots $P_a$ (probability of lot acceptance) on the Y-axis against the incoming lot fraction defective $p$ on the X-axis.

**Ideal OC curve:** A vertical step at $p_0$ (the true quality) — accept everything below, reject everything above. This is unattainable in practice due to sampling variability.

**Actual OC curve:** A smooth S-shaped curve. Key reference points:

- **AQL ($p_1$):** Quality at which $P_a = 1 - \alpha = 0.95$. Almost all good lots accepted.
- **LTPD ($p_2$):** Quality at which $P_a = \beta = 0.10$. Most bad lots rejected.
- **Indifference Quality ($p = 0.50$):** Equal chance of acceptance or rejection.
- **Producer's Risk ($\alpha$):** $1 - P_a(p_1)$ — probability of rejecting a *good* lot. Standard value = 0.05.
- **Consumer's Risk ($\beta$):** $P_a(p_2)$ — probability of accepting a *bad* lot. Standard value = 0.10.

The OC curve is steeper for larger $n$ and smaller $c$, giving better discrimination. It is flatter for small $n$ and large $c$, giving poor discrimination (lots of any quality are accepted often).

*(Sketch: S-shaped curve; X-axis = $p$ (fraction defective), Y-axis = $P_a$; mark AQL with horizontal dashed line at $P_a = 0.95$; mark LTPD at $P_a = 0.10$.)*

---

**Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

Given: $n = 60$, $c = 2$, Poisson approximation: $\lambda = np$.

**(i) Probability of acceptance at $p = 0.02$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= 60 \times 0.02 = 1.2 \\[4pt]
P_a &= e^{-1.2}\left[\frac{(1.2)^0}{0!} + \frac{(1.2)^1}{1!} + \frac{(1.2)^2}{2!}\right] \\[4pt]
&= e^{-1.2}\left[1 + 1.2 + 0.72\right] \\[4pt]
&= 0.3012 \times 2.92 \\[4pt]
&= \mathbf{0.8795}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Producer's risk at AQL = 2%:** From (i), $P_a(0.02) = 0.8795$.

$$\alpha = 1 - P_a(0.02) = 1 - 0.8795 = \mathbf{0.1205 \;(12.05\%)}$$

> This exceeds 5% — the plan is **not satisfactory** for the specified AQL.

**(iii) Consumer's risk at LTPD = 8%:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\lambda &= 60 \times 0.08 = 4.8 \\[4pt]
P_a &= e^{-4.8}\left[1 + 4.8 + \frac{4.8^2}{2}\right] \\[4pt]
&= e^{-4.8}\left[1 + 4.8 + 11.52\right] \\[4pt]
&= 0.00823 \times 17.32 = \mathbf{0.1425} \\[4pt]
\beta &= P_a(0.08) = \mathbf{0.1425 \;(14.25\%)}
\end{aligned}
$$

> This exceeds 10% — the plan is also **not satisfactory** for LTPD protection.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [ALTERNATIVE]**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Explain the **MIL-STD-105E** (Attribute) acceptance sampling system. Discuss the procedure for selecting a sampling plan using the AQL approach.
**[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain **Dodge–Romig** sampling plans. Distinguish clearly between **LTPD-based** and **AOQL-based** plans with their design philosophy.
**[7 Marks]**

---

### **Model Solution to Question B**

**Solution to (a) — 7 Marks**

**MIL-STD-105E** is a **military standard** for acceptance sampling by attributes. It was widely adopted globally for industrial quality assurance and has now been superseded by **ANSI/ASQ Z1.4-2003** and **ISO 2859-1**.

**Key Features:**
1. **AQL-based** — plans indexed by AQL values (e.g., 0.065%, 0.10%, 0.15%, ..., 6.5%, 10%).
2. **Lot-size-based** — three General Inspection Levels (I, II, III) and four Special Inspection Levels (S-1 to S-4). Level II is the default.
3. **Three plan types:** Single, Double, and Multiple sampling (with equivalent protection).
4. **Three switching rules:** Normal → Reduced → Tightened inspection, ensuring **long-term quality discipline**.

**Procedure for Plan Selection (AQL Approach):**
1. Specify the **AQL** (e.g., 1%).
2. Determine the **lot size** $N$.
3. Choose the **inspection level** (default = Level II).
4. Look up the **sample size code letter** (A, B, C, ..., R) in Table I.
5. Using the code letter and AQL, enter Table II-A (single), II-B (double), or II-C (multiple) to read the plan $(n, c)$ or $(n_1, n_2, c_1, c_2)$.
6. Draw the sample, count defectives, apply the acceptance/rejection rule.
7. Apply switching rules based on cumulative lot quality.

**Advantages:** Standardised, removes subjective judgement, simple to implement, supported by comprehensive tables.

---

**Solution to (b) — 7 Marks**

**Dodge–Romig Sampling Plans** are a classical set of attribute sampling plans, designed by H.F. Dodge and H.G. Romig in the 1920s–30s, primarily for **large-lot industrial inspection**. They are tabulated for both **LTPD-based** and **AOQL-based** philosophies.

**Design Philosophy:**
- Assumes lots are submitted in **series** (continuous production).
- **Rectifying inspection** is performed: rejected lots are 100% screened and defectives replaced with good ones.
- Aims to provide a **guaranteed level of protection** to either producer or consumer.

**LTPD-based Plans (Consumer's Risk Protection):**
- Tabulated by **LTPD** (e.g., 1%, 1.5%, 2%, 3%, 5%, 10%) and **lot size**.
- The plan is chosen so that $P_a(\text{LTPD}) = 0.10$ — i.e., the **consumer's risk is fixed at 10%**.
- The AQL is *minimised* (best possible for the consumer).

**AOQL-based Plans (Average Quality Protection):**
- Tabulated by **AOQL** (e.g., 0.5%, 1%, 2%, 3%, 5%) and **process average**.
- The plan is chosen so that the **maximum AOQ** (after rectifying inspection) equals the AOQL.
- Provides protection on the **long-term average outgoing quality** to the consumer.

| Feature | LTPD Plan | AOQL Plan |
|---|---|---|
| Guaranteed by | Consumer's risk $\beta = 0.10$ | Maximum average outgoing quality |
| Best for | Single isolated lots | Continuous production with rectifying inspection |
| Tables | LTPD vs. lot size | AOQL vs. process average |

**Note:** Dodge–Romig plans preceded MIL-STD-105E and are still referenced in some KTU exam questions.

---

## ⚠️ KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING / PITFALL CALLOUT

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing $\alpha$ and $\beta$ positions on the OC curve.** $\alpha$ is the *height drop* from 1.0 to $P_a$ at AQL — i.e., the probability of *rejecting* a good lot. $\beta$ is the *height* of $P_a$ above 0 at LTPD — i.e., the probability of *accepting* a bad lot. Many students write them backwards.
>
> 2. **Using binomial when Poisson is required** (and vice versa). The KTU question stem almost always says "use Poisson" or "use binomial" — *follow the instruction*. Default for $n \ge 16$ and $p \le 0.10$ is Poisson.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the $e^{-\lambda}$ factor** in Poisson $P_a$ calculations. The exponential is *not* equal to 1.
>
> 4. **Mixing up AOQ with $P_a$.** $P_a$ is a probability; $AOQ$ is a fraction defective *after* rectifying inspection, weighted by $P_a$ and $p$.
>
> 5. **In variable plans, omitting that the $k$-method requires KNOWN $\sigma$.** If $\sigma$ is unknown, the $t$-method with degrees of freedom $n - 1$ is mandatory.
>
> 6. **Not showing the substitution step** $\lambda = np$ explicitly — the KTU examiner awards 1 mark for this very step.
>
> 7. **Units:** Always express $p$ as a fraction (0.02) and AQL/LTPD in % (2%) — convert correctly, do not mix.

---

## 📋 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> **High-density revision checklist for the KTU 2024 Module 2 exam**

- **Acceptance Sampling** = inspect a sample → accept/reject the whole lot. Used when 100% inspection is impossible, uneconomical, or destructive.
- **Attribute plan** = binary (defective / non-defective). **Variable plan** = measured against specification limits.
- **OC curve** is the *fingerprint* of a sampling plan. It is an **S-shaped** curve.
- **AQL** = producer's benchmark; **LTPD** = consumer's benchmark.
- **Producer's Risk $\alpha = 0.05$**, **Consumer's Risk $\beta = 0.10$** are KTU-conventional default values.
- **Single Plan (n, c):** one decision based on one sample.
- **Double Plan (n₁, n₂, c₁, c₂):** up to two samples; saves ASN on extreme-quality lots.
- **Multiple / Sequential Plans:** even more efficient, more complex.
- **Hypergeometric** for finite small lots; **Binomial** for large lots with replacement; **Poisson** for $n \ge 16$, $p \le 0.10$.
- **MIL-STD-105E** → Attribute, AQL-based, replaced by **ANSI Z1.4** / **ISO 2859-1**.
- **MIL-STD-414** → Variable, AQL-based, replaced by **ANSI Z1.9** / **ISO 3951**.
- **Dodge–Romig** → Two flavours: LTPD-based (single lots) and AOQL-based (continuous production with rectifying inspection).
- **AOQL** = maximum possible average outgoing quality after rectifying inspection.
- **ATI** = average inspection effort per lot, including 100% screening of rejects.
- **Variable k-method** (sigma known) needs $z$-tables; **t-method** (sigma unknown) needs t-tables with df = $n - 1$.
- For **double-sided** variable specifications, the lot is accepted only if *both* $\bar{x} \le U - k\sigma$ AND $\bar{x} \ge L + k\sigma$.
- A **steeper OC curve** = better discrimination; a **flatter OC curve** = poor discrimination (c is too large or n is too small).
- Always **mark AQL, LTPD, $\alpha$, $\beta$** on the OC curve sketch in the exam.
- In problems: convert all percentages to fractions, calculate $\lambda = np$, and use Poisson tables (or compute $e^{-\lambda}$ directly).
- **Remember the $e^{-\lambda}$ factor** — it is the most commonly forgotten term.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Design of Sampling Plans
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Design of Sampling Plans

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Acceptance Sampling Plan**: A statistical quality control procedure in which a **random sample** is drawn from a lot (batch) of finished products, and on the basis of the inspection result (number of defectives found), a decision is made to **either accept** or **reject** the entire lot.

A **Sampling Plan** is a documented rule (or set of rules) that specifies:
1. The **sample size** $n$ to be drawn from a lot of size $N$.
2. The **acceptance number** $c$ (the maximum number of defectives allowed in the sample for lot acceptance).
3. The **decision rule** (accept the lot if defectives $\leq c$, reject otherwise).

The notation commonly used in KTU standard texts is **$(\,N, n, c\,)$** — a *triple* defining a single sampling plan.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Tea Tasting Test**
> Imagine a tea factory producing 10,000 cups of tea from a single brew. Testing every cup is impossible (destructive/costly). Instead, the Quality Manager randomly tastes **5 cups**. If **0 cups** taste bad, the batch is served. If even **1 cup** is bad, the entire batch is discarded.
> - The **5 cups** = sample size $n$
> - **0 bad cups allowed** = acceptance number $c$
> - The rule "taste 5, accept if all good" = the **sampling plan**

In engineering, this translates to inspecting a small subset of components (e.g., resistors, IC chips, fasteners) to decide on an entire shipment — making it the **bridge between production and the customer**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology (Bold-Defined Metrics)

- **AQL (Acceptable Quality Level)**: The worst tolerable process average at which lots are considered acceptable; the **Producer's Risk point** ($p_1$).
- **LTPD (Lot Tolerance Percent Defective) / RQL**: The defect level the consumer is willing to tolerate only rarely; the **Consumer's Risk point** ($p_2$).
- **Producer's Risk ($\alpha$)**: Probability of **rejecting a good lot** (with quality = AQL). KTU standard: $\alpha = 0.05$.
- **Consumer's Risk ($\beta$)**: Probability of **accepting a bad lot** (with quality = LTPD). KTU standard: $\beta = 0.10$.
- **OC Curve (Operating Characteristic Curve)**: The plot of $P_a$ (probability of acceptance) vs. incoming lot fraction defective $p$.
- **AOQ (Average Outgoing Quality)**: Expected fraction defective in the **remaining product** after rectifying inspection.
- **AOQL (Average Outgoing Quality Limit)**: The **maximum** value of AOQ over all possible $p$.
- **ASN (Average Sample Number)**: Expected number of items inspected per lot under the plan.

### 1.4 Why Sampling Plans? (Syllabus Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **When to use Sampling Inspection (vs. 100% inspection):**
> 1. Inspection is **destructive** (fuse testing, light bulb life).
> 2. Cost of 100% inspection is **prohibitively high**.
> 3. The lot consists of a **large number of items** ($N$ is very large).
> 4. **Fatigue / monotony** makes 100% inspection unreliable.
> 5. The process is **stable** and incoming lots are homogeneous.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Types of Sampling Plans (Operational Classification)

Sampling plans are classified by the **number of samples** drawn and the **inspection stages**:

#### A. Single Sampling Plan
A single random sample of size $n$ is drawn. The lot is **accepted** if the number of defectives $d \leq c$; otherwise, **rejected**.

**Decision Rule:**
$$
\text{Lot} = \begin{cases} \text{ACCEPT} & \text{if } d \leq c \\ \text{REJECT} & \text{if } d > c \end{cases}
$$

#### B. Double Sampling Plan
Two stages. First sample of size $n_1$ yields a decision: accept, reject, or **take a second sample** of size $n_2$. Final decision is then made on combined defectives.

**Decision Rule (with $c_1, c_2, c_3$):**
- If $d_1 \leq c_1$ → **Accept**
- If $d_1 > c_2$ → **Reject**
- If $c_1 < d_1 \leq c_2$ → Draw second sample, accept if $d_1 + d_2 \leq c_3$

#### C. Multiple Sampling Plan
Extension of double sampling with up to **7 stages**. Each stage has its own accept/reject thresholds.

#### D. Sequential Sampling Plan
Items are inspected **one by one**. After each item, a decision is made: accept, reject, or continue. The **smallest average ASN** among all plans.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip**: Single and Double sampling plans are the most frequently asked designs. Sequential plans appear in theory/numerical questions involving Wald's SPRT.

### 2.2 Operating Characteristic (OC) Curve — Conceptual Steps

1. For a given plan $(N, n, c)$, compute the probability of acceptance $P_a(p)$ for various values of incoming fraction defective $p$.
2. Plot $P_a(p)$ on the **y-axis** vs. $p$ on the **x-axis**.
3. The resulting curve is the **OC curve**.
4. A **good** sampling plan has an **ideal (vertical)** OC curve — a step function. Real curves are **S-shaped** due to sample size limitations.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula / Metric** | **Mathematical Expression** | **Description** |
|---|---|---|
| Probability of Acceptance (Hypergeometric, exact) | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{\binom{D}{d}\binom{N-D}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}$ | $D = Np$ defectives in lot |
| Probability of Acceptance (Binomial, when $n/N \leq 0.1$) | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}$ | Most used in KTU problems |
| Probability of Acceptance (Poisson Approximation) | $P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np}(np)^d}{d!}$ | Used when $p \leq 0.10$ and $n \geq 20$ |
| Producer's Risk | $P_a(p_1) = 1 - \alpha$ | $p_1 = \text{AQL}$ |
| Consumer's Risk | $P_a(p_2) = \beta$ | $p_2 = \text{LTPD}$ |
| Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ) | $\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}$ | Assumes rejected lots are 100% inspected and replaced with good units |
| AOQL (Limit) | $\text{AOQL} = \max_{p} \text{AOQ}(p)$ | Worst-case outgoing quality |
| Average Sample Number (Single) | $\text{ASN} = n$ | Constant for single plan |
| Poisson Parameter | $\lambda = np$ | Used as a lookup argument in KTU tables |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Electronics manufacturing**: Incoming inspection of resistors, capacitors, ICs.
- **Automotive industry**: MIL-STD-105E / ANSI/ASQ Z1.4 plans for parts acceptance.
- **Pharmaceuticals**: Drug batch release decisions.
- **Aerospace**: Composite material defect analysis (often destructive).
- **Textiles & Garments**: Fabric defect inspection per lot.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design Philosophy**: A sampling plan is **designed** such that the OC curve passes through **two points**:
> - Point $(p_1, 1-\alpha)$ — the Producer's protection point
> - Point $(p_2, \beta)$ — the Consumer's protection point
> This is the **classic two-point design** taught in KTU.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: OC Curve for Single Sampling Plan (Binomial Distribution)

Consider a lot of $N$ items containing $D = Np$ defectives. A random sample of $n$ items is drawn. Let $d$ be the number of defectives in the sample.

The number of ways of drawing $n$ items from $N$ is $\binom{N}{n}$. The number of ways of drawing exactly $d$ defectives (and $n-d$ non-defectives) is $\binom{D}{d} \cdot \binom{N-D}{n-d}$.

Hence, the probability of getting exactly $d$ defectives in the sample is given by the **hypergeometric distribution**:

$$
P(d) = \dfrac{\binom{D}{d}\binom{N-D}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}
$$

**Step 1**: Acceptance occurs if the number of defectives $d$ in the sample is $\leq c$.

$$
P_a = P(d \leq c) = \sum_{d=0}^{c} P(d)
$$

**Step 2**: Substituting the hypergeometric expression:

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{\binom{Np}{d}\binom{N(1-p)}{n-d}}{\binom{N}{n}}
$$

**Step 3 (Binomial Approximation)**: When the sampling fraction $n/N \leq 0.1$ (often satisfied in industry), the binomial approximation is valid:

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \binom{n}{d} p^d (1-p)^{n-d}
$$

**Step 4 (Poisson Approximation)**: For $p$ small ($p \leq 0.10$) and $n$ large ($n \geq 20$), the binomial converges to Poisson with parameter $\lambda = np$:

$$
P_a = \sum_{d=0}^{c} \dfrac{e^{-np} (np)^d}{d!}
$$

This is the most **frequently used** form in KTU numerical problems because it requires only the use of **Poisson tables** (read directly using $\lambda = np$ and $c$).

### 3.2 Worked Numerical: Designing a Single Sampling Plan (Two-Point Method)

**Problem Statement (KTU-style)**:
> Design a single sampling plan for the following requirements:
> - AQL ($p_1$) = 0.01 (1% defective)
> - LTPD ($p_2$) = 0.05 (5% defective)
> - Producer's Risk $\alpha = 0.05$
> - Consumer's Risk $\beta = 0.10$

**Step 1 — Translate constraints into OC curve points:**

$$
P_a(p_1 = 0.01) = 1 - \alpha = 0.95
$$

$$
P_a(p_2 = 0.05) = \beta = 0.10
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Poisson parameter ratios:**

$$
\dfrac{np_2}{np_1} = \dfrac{p_2}{p_1} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.01} = 5
$$

**Step 3 — Use the KTU Poisson Table / OC curve table to find $c$ and $np$:**

The operating ratio is:
$$
R = \dfrac{p_2}{p_1} = \dfrac{0.05}{0.01} = 5.0
$$

From the standard Poisson OC tables, for the pair $(P_a(p_1) = 0.95, P_a(p_2) = 0.10)$, we need to find a $c$ value such that the ratio of corresponding $np$ values is approximately 5.

| $c$ | $np_1$ (for $P_a = 0.95$) | $np_2$ (for $P_a = 0.10$) | Ratio $np_2/np_1$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.051 | 2.303 | 45.16 |
| 1 | 0.355 | 3.890 | 10.96 |
| 2 | 0.818 | 5.322 | 6.51 |
| **3** | **1.366** | **6.681** | **4.89** ✓ |
| 4 | 1.970 | 7.994 | 4.06 |
| 5 | 2.613 | 9.275 | 3.55 |

**Closest match** is $c = 3$ with ratio $\approx 4.89 \approx 5.0$. (The slight deviation is accepted in KTU — we pick the value yielding the *nearest* ratio.)

**Step 4 — Compute the sample size $n$:**

From $np_1 = 1.366$:
$$
n = \dfrac{np_1}{p_1} = \dfrac{1.366}{0.01} = 136.6 \approx 137
$$

From $np_2 = 6.681$ (verification):
$$
n = \dfrac{6.681}{0.05} = 133.62 \approx 134
$$

We take the **more conservative (larger) value** to ensure both constraints are met. Thus $n \approx 137$ (often rounded to 135 in KTU answer keys; we use **$n = 135, c = 3$** as a robust working plan).

**Final Plan:** $\boxed{(N, n, c) = (N, 135, 3)}$

### 3.3 Derivation: Average Outgoing Quality (AOQ)

Assume that **rejected lots are 100% inspected** and all defectives are replaced with good ones (rectifying inspection). Then the outgoing lot quality is:

**Step 1**: Number of defectives in the outgoing lot:
- If accepted: $D = Np$ defectives pass through.
- If rejected: $0$ defectives (all replaced).

**Step 2**: Probability the lot is accepted = $P_a$. Average number of defectives that escape:

$$
\text{Expected defectives} = P_a \cdot Np
$$

**Step 3**: The fraction defective in the **outgoing** product (defectives / total shipped) is:

$$
\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot Np}{N} = P_a \cdot p
$$

**Step 4**: When the rejected lots are **replaced by good items**, only the accepted portion contributes to outgoing fraction. The accepted items count is $P_a \cdot N$ (effective). The remaining product count is $N - n \cdot \text{(rejected inspection overhead)}$. The standard rectifying-inspection formula is:

$$
\text{AOQ} = \dfrac{P_a \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N}
$$

**Step 5**: AOQL is the maximum of AOQ over all $p$:

$$
\text{AOQL} = \max_{0 \leq p \leq 1} \left\{ \dfrac{P_a(p) \cdot p \cdot (N-n)}{N} \right\}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trap**: Students often forget the $(N-n)/N$ factor. With destructive testing, $n$ items are *consumed* and replaced with good ones, but the effective outgoing lot is still size $N$. Hence the correction is needed.

### 3.4 Derivation: Average Sample Number (ASN) for Double Sampling Plan

For a double sampling plan $(n_1, n_2, c_1, c_2, c_3)$:

**Step 1**: A second sample is drawn only when the first sample gives a "go to second sample" decision: $c_1 < d_1 \leq c_2$.

$$
P(\text{2nd sample needed}) = \sum_{d_1 = c_1+1}^{c_2} \binom{n_1}{d_1} p^{d_1}(1-p)^{n_1-d_1}
$$

**Step 2**: The ASN is:

$$
\text{ASN} = n_1 + n_2 \cdot P(\text{2nd sample needed})
$$

**Step 3 (Special case)**: For symmetric plans, ASN is minimized at the **AQL** — the design optimum.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Architecture: Single Sampling Plan Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Lot of N items arrives]:::startNode
    B{Draw random sample of size n}:::procNode
    C{Count defectives d in sample}:::procNode
    D{Is d less than or equal to c}:::decisionNode
    E[ACCEPT the lot]:::acceptNode
    F[REJECT the lot]:::rejectNode
    G[If rectifying: 100 percent inspect and replace defectives]:::repairNode

    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D -- YES --> E
    D -- NO --> F
    F --> G

    classDef startNode fill:#e0f2fe,stroke:#0284c7,stroke-width:2px,color:#0c4a6e
    classDef procNode fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b,stroke-width:2px,color:#78350f
    classDef decisionNode fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#db2777,stroke-width:2px,color:#831843
    classDef acceptNode fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#16a34a,stroke-width:2px,color:#14532d
    classDef rejectNode fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626,stroke-width:2px,color:#7f1d1d
    classDef repairNode fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#7c3aed,stroke-width:2px,color:#3b0764
```

### 4.2 Architecture: Double Sampling Plan Two-Stage Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Stage 1: Draw n1 items]:::stageNode
    B1{Count d1 defectives}:::procNode
    C1{Decision check}:::decisionNode
    D1[ACCEPT]:::acceptNode
    E1[REJECT]:::rejectNode
    F1[Stage 2: Draw n2 more items]:::stageNode
    G1{Count d2 from second sample}:::procNode
    H1{Is d1 plus d2 less than or equal to c3}:::decisionNode
    I1[ACCEPT]:::acceptNode
    J1[REJECT]:::rejectNode

    A1 --> B1
    B1 --> C1
    C1 -- d1 less than or equal to c1 --> D1
    C1 -- d1 greater than c2 --> E1
    C1 -- c1 less than d1 less than or equal to c2 --> F1
    F1 --> G1
    G1 --> H1
    H1 -- YES --> I1
    H1 -- NO --> J1
```
