> **(b)** For a new **ergonomic office chair** design, the following data is collected. Compute the **Absolute and Relative Weights** of each customer requirement. Using △ = 1, ○ = 5, ● = 9, also calculate the **Technical Importance** and identify which engineering parameter deserves maximum R&D focus. **(7 marks)**

**Data Table for Question B Part (b):**

| Customer Requirement | Iᵢ | Lumbar Support (°) | Seat Height (mm) | Cushion Density (kg/m³) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CR1 — Reduces back pain | 5 | ● | ○ | ● |
| CR2 — Fits tall users | 3 | △ | ● | △ |
| CR3 — Comfortable for long sitting | 4 | ● | △ | ● |
| CR4 — Affordable price | 2 | △ | ○ | △ |

#### Model Solution for Question B

**Part (a) — Six Components of the House of Quality (7 marks):**

| # | Component | Location | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Customer Requirements (WHATS) | Left Wall | Captures the customer needs in their own language. |
| 2 | Importance Ratings | Far Left (Planning) | Quantifies how much each customer requirement matters (1–5). |
| 3 | Engineering Requirements (HOWS) | Ceiling | Technical/measurable parameters that respond to the WHATS. |
| 4 | Relationship Matrix | Central Room | Shows strength of linkage between each WHAT and HOW. |
| 5 | Roof / Correlation Matrix | Triangular Top | Shows how the HOWS interact with each other (+, –, blank). |
| 6 | Technical Targets & Weights | Right Wall / Foundation | Engineering targets, competitive benchmarks, and final priorities. |

*(1 mark for the diagram + 1 mark per component explained = 7 marks.)*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Convert symbols to numbers:**

| CR | Iᵢ | w(Lumbar) | w(Height) | w(Density) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CR1 | 5 | 9 | 5 | 9 |
| CR2 | 3 | 1 | 9 | 1 |
| CR3 | 4 | 9 | 1 | 9 |
| CR4 | 2 | 1 | 5 | 1 |

**Step 2 — Row Sums (Rᵢ):**

$$R_1 = 9 + 5 + 9 = 23$$

$$R_2 = 1 + 9 + 1 = 11$$

$$R_3 = 9 + 1 + 9 = 19$$

$$R_4 = 1 + 5 + 1 = 7$$

**Step 3 — Absolute Weights (AWᵢ):**

$$AW_1 = 5 \times 23 = 115$$

$$AW_2 = 3 \times 11 = 33$$

$$AW_3 = 4 \times 19 = 76$$

$$AW_4 = 2 \times 7 = 14$$

**Step 4 — Total:**

$$\sum AW = 115 + 33 + 76 + 14 = 238$$

**Step 5 — Relative Weights (RWᵢ %):**

$$RW_1 = \frac{115}{238} \times 100\% \approx 48.32\%$$

$$RW_2 = \frac{33}{238} \times 100\% \approx 13.87\%$$

$$RW_3 = \frac{76}{238} \times 100\% \approx 31.93\%$$

$$RW_4 = \frac{14}{238} \times 100\% \approx 5.88\%$$

**Step 6 — Technical Importance (TJⱼ):**

$$TJ_{\text{Lumbar}} = (5)(9) + (3)(1) + (4)(9) + (2)(1) = 45 + 3 + 36 + 2 = 86$$

$$TJ_{\text{Height}} = (5)(5) + (3)(9) + (4)(1) + (2)(5) = 25 + 27 + 4 + 10 = 66$$

$$TJ_{\text{Density}} = (5)(9) + (3)(1) + (4)(9) + (2)(1) = 45 + 3 + 36 + 2 = 86$$

**Step 7 — Conclusion:**

| Customer Requirement | Iᵢ | AWᵢ | RWᵢ (%) | Rank |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| CR1 — Reduces back pain | 5 | 115 | 48.32 | **1** |
| CR2 — Fits tall users | 3 | 33 | 13.87 | 3 |
| CR3 — Comfortable for long sitting | 4 | 76 | 31.93 | 2 |
| CR4 — Affordable price | 2 | 14 | 5.88 | 4 |

| Technical Requirement | TJⱼ | Rank |
|---|---|---|
| Lumbar Support (°) | 86 | **1 (tied for top)** |
| Seat Height (mm) | 66 | 3 |
| Cushion Density (kg/m³) | 86 | **1 (tied for top)** |

> **Conclusion:** **Lumbar Support and Cushion Density** are tied for the **top-ranked engineering parameters (TJ = 86 each)**, and must receive maximum R&D focus. They both support the most critical customer requirement — **CR1 — Reduces back pain (RW = 48.32 %)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing △, ○, ● values.** The standard KTU scale is **1, 5, 9** — not 1, 3, 9. Using the wrong scale gives numerically different answers. Always state the scale at the top of your solution.
> 2. **Forgetting to compute BOTH AW and RW.** Many students stop at Absolute Weight. RW is required to produce a percentage ranking.
> 3. **Not identifying the top-ranked HOW.** A complete 7-mark problem must end with an explicit engineering decision (which parameter gets max focus).
> 4. **Missing the total of AW.** The denominator $\sum AW$ is mandatory for RW. A common arithmetic error is summing only 3 of the 4 rows.
> 5. **Mixing up rows and columns.** Customer requirements are *rows* (WHATS); engineering parameters are *columns* (HOWS). The relationship matrix is read *row-wise* for AW and *column-wise* for TJ.
> 6. **Omitting units in the final answer.** Always state the engineering parameter with its unit (e.g., "Insulation drop = 25 °C").

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 4: QFD – House of Quality):**

- **QFD** is a **customer-driven** product and process development methodology introduced in Japan by **Yoji Akao** in **1972**.
- **House of Quality (HOQ)** is the central matrix of QFD (Phase I — Product Planning).
- The matrix has **six components**: Whats (left wall), Planning/Importance, Hows (ceiling), Relationship Matrix (centre), Roof/Correlation (top), and Targets/Weights (right wall + foundation).
- Standard relationship scale used in KTU: **△ = 1, ○ = 5, ● = 9**.
- Customer importance scale: **1 to 5** (5 = highest).
- **Absolute Weight** formula: $AW_i = I_i \times R_i$ where $R_i = \sum w_{ij}$.
- **Relative Weight** formula: $RW_i = \dfrac{AW_i}{\sum AW_i} \times 100\%$ — used to **rank customer requirements**.
- **Technical Importance** formula: $TJ_j = \sum_{i} I_i \times w_{ij}$ — used to **rank engineering parameters**.
- A **Positive (+) correlation in the roof** means improving one How helps another. A **Negative (–) correlation** means improving one How hurts another — a design trade-off.
- The **Whats** of one phase become the **Hows** of the next phase — this is what "**deployment**" literally means.
- QFD has **four sequential phases**: Product Planning (HOQ-I) → Parts Deployment (HOQ-II) → Process Planning (HOQ-III) → Production Planning (HOQ-IV).
- **Key benefit:** The House of Quality is the only tool that simultaneously shows **customer priorities, technical priorities, trade-offs, and competitive benchmarks** in a single visual artifact.
- **KTU exam tip:** When asked to "explain the House of Quality", always provide (i) a labelled diagram, (ii) a numbered list of the six components, and (iii) at least one formula. A 3-mark short answer that gives only a definition is incomplete.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: QFD Matrix
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** QFD Matrix

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Quality Function Deployment (QFD)** is a structured, customer-driven planning and engineering methodology that translates the *Voice of the Customer (VOC)* into specific, measurable **technical/engineering characteristics** throughout the product development cycle. The central tool used to perform this translation is the **QFD Matrix**, universally known as the **House of Quality (HOQ)**.

The House of Quality is a single-page, multi-room, visual matrix that simultaneously displays:
1. **Customer Requirements (CRs)** — the *WHATs* (the voice of the customer).
2. **Engineering/Technical Requirements (TRs)** — the *HOWs* (the language of engineering).
3. The **Relationship Matrix** between WHATs and HOWs.
4. The **Roof** — inter-relationships among the HOWs themselves.
5. **Customer Importance Ratings**, **Technical Importance Ratings**, and **Competitive Benchmarks**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Examination-Ready):**
> *"The QFD Matrix, or House of Quality, is a graphical and analytical planning tool that systematically links customer wants (WHATs) to engineering characteristics (HOWs), enabling cross-functional teams to design products that deliver the highest possible customer satisfaction at minimum cost."*

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator" Mental Model

Imagine a customer from **Japan** speaking only Japanese, and an engineer from **Kerala** speaking only Malayalam. They need to communicate about a product. Without translation, the engineer will guess what the customer wants — and likely guess wrong. 

The **House of Quality acts as a certified translator's notebook**:
- **Left wall (WHATs)** = Customer's sentences written in Japanese.
- **Ceiling (HOWs)** = Engineer's technical vocabulary written in Malayalam.
- **Central room (Relationship Matrix)** = The translator's dictionary, showing which technical term corresponds to which customer desire and how strongly.
- **Roof** = A grammar check that warns the engineer when two technical solutions conflict (e.g., "making it stronger may make it heavier").
- **Right wall** = How well competitors are satisfying each customer demand (competitive intelligence).
- **Bottom** = The translated priority — which engineering characteristic the engineer must focus on FIRST to please the customer MOST.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why HOQ is shaped like a "House":** The matrix literally resembles the cross-section of a house — the *left wall* holds customer wants, the *ceiling* holds engineering specs, the *roof* shows how the specs interact, and the *floor* gives the priorities. The terminology is metaphorical and accepted worldwide in quality literature.

## 1.3 Purpose & Significance in Engineering

The HOQ solves three classical engineering dilemmas:
- **The Voice Problem:** Customers say *"I want a comfortable car"* — but how does the engineer quantify "comfort"?
- **The Priority Problem:** With 50 customer wants and 30 technical specs, which one matters most?
- **The Conflict Problem:** Improving one spec (e.g., reducing weight) may worsen another (e.g., durability). The Roof matrix exposes these conflicts *before* any metal is cut.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Anchor:** The QFD Matrix is the *first* step of QFD, called **Product Planning**. It belongs to the *front-end* of the product development lifecycle, before Design of Experiments (DOE), Tolerance Design, or Failure Mode Analysis (FMEA). Module 4 of PERUT636 treats the HOQ as the bridge between market research and engineering design.

## 1.4 The Eight Rooms of the House of Quality

| # | Room Name | Content | Symbolism |
|---|-----------|---------|-----------|
| 1 | Left Wall | Customer Requirements (WHATs) | Voice of Customer |
| 2 | Ceiling | Technical Requirements (HOWs) | Voice of Engineer |
| 3 | Central Room | Relationship Matrix | WHAT × HOW links |
| 4 | Roof | Inter-HOW Correlations | HOW × HOW links |
| 5 | Right Wall | Customer Competitive Assessment | Competitor benchmarking |
| 6 | Far Right | Customer Importance Ratings (Cᵢ) | Priority weights |
| 7 | Bottom | Technical Importance Ratings (Tⱼ) | Engineering priorities |
| 8 | Far Bottom | Target Values / Spec Limits | Engineering targets |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Sequential Steps to Construct the QFD Matrix

The construction of the House of Quality is a disciplined, sequential process. Each step has a *why* and a *how*:

### Step 1 — Identify the Customer Requirements (WHATs)
- **Why:** Without a clear list of customer wants, no engineering decision has a customer anchor.
- **How:** Methods include customer interviews, focus groups, complaint logs, surveys (Kano Model can be used to classify them), and market research. CRs are typically expressed in the customer's own language — *non-technical*.
- **Output:** A flat, de-duplicated list, usually **8–20** CRs (e.g., *easy to grip*, *durable*, *low cost*, *lightweight*).

### Step 2 — Identify the Engineering/Technical Requirements (HOWs)
- **Why:** Translates fuzzy customer wants into measurable engineering quantities.
- **How:** Cross-functional brainstorming by designers, manufacturing, quality engineers, and suppliers. TRs must be **measurable** (e.g., *tensile strength in MPa*, *weight in grams*, *cycle time in seconds*).
- **Output:** A flat list of **10–40** TRs.

### Step 3 — Populate the Central Relationship Matrix
- **Why:** Determines *which* technical characteristics actually drive *which* customer requirements, and *how strongly*.
- **How:** Each cell of the (CR × TR) matrix is filled with one of four symbols:

| Symbol | Meaning | Numerical Weight (Standard) | Numerical Weight (Compact) |
|--------|---------|:----------------------------:|:--------------------------:|
| **● (Solid Circle)** | Strong relationship | **9** | **5** |
| **○ (Open Circle)** | Moderate relationship | **3** | **3** |
| **△ (Triangle)** | Weak relationship | **1** | **1** |
| **Blank** | No relationship | **0** | **0** |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Note:** Most KTU textbook problems use the **9-3-1 scale**. Always state the scale you are using in your answer. Marks are deducted when students mix scales inside one calculation.

### Step 4 — Build the Roof (Inter-HOW Correlation Matrix)
- **Why:** Engineering characteristics interact. Boosting one may unintentionally harm another. The Roof exposes these *trade-offs* before they become expensive redesigns.
- **How:** A symmetric matrix placed on top of the ceiling, comparing HOWs against HOWs. Standard symbols:

| Symbol | Meaning |
|--------|---------|
| **++ (Strong Positive)** | Synergistic — improving one strongly improves the other |
| **+ (Positive)** | Mild synergy |
| **Blank** | Independent / No meaningful relationship |
| **− (Negative)** | Mild trade-off |
| **− − (Strong Negative)** | Strong conflict — must be carefully balanced |

### Step 5 — Assign Customer Importance Ratings (Cᵢ)
- **Why:** Not all customer wants are equally important. We need a numerical weight per CR.
- **How:** On a 1–5 scale (1 = least important, 5 = most important), customers rate each CR. The **Cᵢ** value is the *mean* rating given by the surveyed customer sample.

### Step 6 — Compute Technical Importance Ratings (Tⱼ)
This is the **central quantitative output** of the HOQ — it ranks the engineering characteristics by their impact on customer satisfaction.
- **How:** For each technical characteristic *j*, sum the products of customer importance and relationship weight over all customer requirements:

$$
T_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot R_{ij}
$$

Where:
- $T_j$ = Technical Importance Rating of the $j^{th}$ engineering characteristic.
- $C_i$ = Customer Importance Rating of the $i^{th}$ customer requirement.
- $R_{ij}$ = Relationship strength between CR$_i$ and TR$_j$ (numerical value 0, 1, 3, or 9).
- $n$ = Total number of customer requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The TR with the **highest Tⱼ** is the engineering characteristic that the design team should focus on FIRST. It has the largest leverage on customer satisfaction.

### Step 7 — Competitive Benchmarking (Optional but Exam-Frequent)
- **How:** Compare your product and competitors' products on (a) customer satisfaction and (b) technical performance. This gives the **Sales Point** and **Improvement Ratio** for each TR, which are then used to revise Tⱼ into a *normalized* technical importance:

$$
T_j^{\text{norm}} = T_j \times I_j \times S_j
$$

Where $I_j$ = Improvement Ratio (your level / competitor's level), and $S_j$ = Sales Point (1 = no impact, 1.2 = strong sales impact).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Symbol | Units / Notes |
|---|---------|------------------|---------------|
| 1 | Customer Importance | $C_i \in \lbrace 1,2,3,4,5 \rbrace$ | Unitless rating |
| 2 | Relationship Strength | $R_{ij} \in \lbrace 0, 1, 3, 9 \rbrace$ | Standard 9-3-1 scale |
| 3 | **Raw Technical Importance** | $\displaystyle T_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot R_{ij}$ | Sum over all CRs |
| 4 | Normalized Tech. Importance | $T_j^{\text{norm}} = T_j \cdot I_j \cdot S_j$ | Benchmark-included |
| 5 | Improvement Ratio | $I_j = \dfrac{\text{Our level}_j}{\text{Competitor level}_j}$ | Numerical ratio |
| 6 | Sales Point | $S_j \in \lbrace 1.0, 1.1, 1.2 \rbrace$ | Subjective sales impact |
| 7 | Roof Positive Symbol | $++$ or $+$ | Synergy |
| 8 | Roof Negative Symbol | $-$ or $--$ | Conflict / Trade-off |
| 9 | Absolute Weight (%) | $\%T_j = \dfrac{T_j}{\sum_j T_j} \times 100$ | Prioritization % |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

The HOQ is widely used across industries:
- **Automotive:** Translating driver wishes (quiet cabin, low fuel use) into NVH specs, mass, and aerodynamic targets.
- **Software:** Translating user stories (easy login, fast search) into latency, throughput, and UI metrics.
- **Medical Devices:** Translating clinician needs (easy sterilization, biocompatibility) into ASTM/ISO engineering parameters.
- **Service Industries:** Translating passenger needs (on-time, courteous staff) into process KPIs (boarding time, training hours).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **QFD and Six Sigma:** HOQ is the front-end *Define* and *Measure* phase of any DMAIC project. Without an HOQ, improvement efforts may optimize the wrong engineering characteristic.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Numerical Example & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Illustrative Engineering Scenario

A startup company wants to design a **reusable water bottle**. The marketing team conducted a customer survey and identified **four Customer Requirements (WHATs)**. The engineering team identified **four Technical Requirements (HOWs)**. The cross-functional team has rated each customer requirement on a 1–5 scale and the relationship matrix using the **9-3-1 scale**.

### Given Data

| Customer Requirement (WHAT) | Customer Importance $C_i$ |
|------------------------------|:--------------------------:|
| **CR₁:** Easy to grip | **5** |
| **CR₂:** Durable | **5** |
| **CR₃:** Low cost | **4** |
| **CR₄:** Lightweight | **3** |

| Technical Requirement (HOW) | Unit |
|------------------------------|------|
| **TR₁:** Outer diameter | mm |
| **TR₂:** Wall thickness | mm |
| **TR₃:** Material tensile strength | MPa |
| **TR₄:** Cycle time | s |

### Given Relationship Matrix (Customer × Technical)

$$
\begin{aligned}
R = \begin{bmatrix}
\text{CR}_1 \text{ (Easy to grip)} \\
\text{CR}_2 \text{ (Durable)} \\
\text{CR}_3 \text{ (Low cost)} \\
\text{CR}_4 \text{ (Lightweight)}
\end{bmatrix}
\!
\begin{bmatrix}
9 & 0 & 1 & 0 \\
0 & 9 & 9 & 0 \\
3 & 1 & 0 & 9 \\
3 & 3 & 0 & 1
\end{bmatrix}
\!\!
\begin{array}{l}
\leftarrow \text{TR}_1 \text{ (OD)} \\
\leftarrow \text{TR}_2 \text{ (Wall)} \\
\leftarrow \text{TR}_3 \text{ (Strength)} \\
\leftarrow \text{TR}_4 \text{ (Cycle time)}
\end{array}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Calculation of Technical Importance

The governing equation is:

$$
T_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot R_{ij}
$$

### Step 3.2.1 — Compute $T_1$ (Outer Diameter)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= C_1 \cdot R_{1,1} + C_2 \cdot R_{2,1} + C_3 \cdot R_{3,1} + C_4 \cdot R_{4,1} \\
T_1 &= (5)(9) + (5)(0) + (4)(3) + (3)(3) \\
T_1 &= 45 + 0 + 12 + 9 \\
T_1 &= 66
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Outer diameter has a technical importance of **66**, driven primarily by grip (45) and partially by cost and weight (12 + 9).

### Step 3.2.2 — Compute $T_2$ (Wall Thickness)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= C_1 \cdot R_{1,2} + C_2 \cdot R_{2,2} + C_3 \cdot R_{3,2} + C_4 \cdot R_{4,2} \\
T_2 &= (5)(0) + (5)(9) + (4)(1) + (3)(3) \\
T_2 &= 0 + 45 + 4 + 9 \\
T_2 &= 58
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Wall thickness is dominated by durability (45) and has moderate impact on weight (9).

### Step 3.2.3 — Compute $T_3$ (Tensile Strength)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_3 &= C_1 \cdot R_{1,3} + C_2 \cdot R_{2,3} + C_3 \cdot R_{3,3} + C_4 \cdot R_{4,3} \\
T_3 &= (5)(1) + (5)(9) + (4)(0) + (3)(0) \\
T_3 &= 5 + 45 + 0 + 0 \\
T_3 &= 50
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Tensile strength matters almost exclusively for durability.

### Step 3.2.4 — Compute $T_4$ (Cycle Time)

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_4 &= C_1 \cdot R_{1,4} + C_2 \cdot R_{2,4} + C_3 \cdot R_{3,4} + C_4 \cdot R_{4,4} \\
T_4 &= (5)(0) + (5)(0) + (4)(9) + (3)(1) \\
T_4 &= 0 + 0 + 36 + 3 \\
T_4 &= 39
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Cycle time matters mainly for cost (36) and slightly for weight (3).

## 3.3 Summary Analytical Matrix — Final HOQ Output

| Technical Requirement | $T_j$ (Raw) | $\%T_j$ (Normalized Priority) | Engineering Action |
|------------------------|:-----------:|:-----------------------------:|--------------------|
| **Outer diameter** | **66** | **30.7 %** | **1st Priority — Tune for grip & cost** |
| **Wall thickness** | **58** | **27.0 %** | **2nd Priority — Critical for durability** |
| **Tensile strength** | **50** | **23.3 %** | **3rd Priority — Select strong material** |
| **Cycle time** | **39** | **18.1 %** | **4th Priority — Optimize mold & process** |
| **Total** | **213** | **100 %** | — |

The percentage is calculated as:

$$
\%T_j = \frac{T_j}{\sum_{j=1}^{m} T_j} \times 100
$$

For example, for TR₁:

$$
\%T_1 = \frac{66}{213} \times 100 = 30.99 \approx 30.7 \%
$$

(The minor rounding differences across rows sum to 100 % due to display precision.)

## 3.4 Roof Matrix Interpretation (Trade-Off Analysis)

A typical roof for this example would show:

| | OD | Wall | Strength | Cycle |
|---|:--:|:----:|:--------:|:-----:|
| **OD** | — | **−** | blank | **+** |
| **Wall** | − | — | **+** | **−** |
| **Strength** | blank | + | — | blank |
| **Cycle** | + | − | blank | — |

- **OD ↑ (larger)  ⟷  Wall ↓ (thinner):** Negative trade-off. A larger diameter allows thinner walls to maintain rigidity.
- **Strength ↑  ⟷  Cycle ↓:** Positive synergy. Stronger materials often cycle faster (better flow).
- **OD ↑  ⟷  Cycle ↑:** Positive synergy. Larger parts cycle faster.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examiner's Logic:** When a CR maps **strongly** to multiple TRs, the design team gains flexibility — they can satisfy the same customer want through different engineering routes. When a TR maps strongly to multiple CRs, that TR is a **critical-to-quality (CTQ) parameter** and must be tightly controlled.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram 1 — House of Quality Layout (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **eight functional rooms** of the HOQ and the data flow between them. This serves as the equivalent of a physical drawing of the House.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef room fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef output fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#15803d,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;

    leftWall["LEFT WALL: Customer Requirements WHATs"]:::room
    ceiling["CEILING: Technical Requirements HOWs"]:::room
    central["CENTRAL ROOM: Relationship Matrix WHAT x HOW"]:::room
    roof["ROOF: Inter-HOW Correlations"]:::room
    rightWall["RIGHT WALL: Customer Competitive Assessment"]:::room
    farRight["FAR RIGHT: Customer Importance Ci"]:::room
    bottom["BOTTOM: Technical Importance Tj"]:::room
    farBottom["FAR BOTTOM: Target Values and Spec Limits"]:::room

    leftWall --> central
    ceiling --> central
    central --> bottom
    farRight --> bottom
    roof -.tradeoffs.-> ceiling
    rightWall -.benchmarks.-> leftWall
    bottom --> farBottom

    note1["Step 1 to 7 Construction Sequence"]:::output
    leftWall --> note1 --> central --> bottom
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram 2 — Construction Sequence Flow

This diagram illustrates the **stepwise construction logic** of the QFD Matrix from raw customer voice to engineering priorities.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef step fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1d4ed8,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;
    classDef artifact fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:1.5px,color:#000;

    A["Step 1: Gather Voice of Customer"]:::step --> A1["WHATs list - non-technical language"]:::artifact
    A1 --> B["Step 2: Brainstorm Technical Requirements"]:::step
    B --> B1["HOWs list - measurable engineering specs"]:::artifact
    B1 --> C["Step 3: Build Relationship Matrix"]:::step
    C --> C1["Use symbols 9 / 3 / 1 / 0"]:::artifact
    C1 --> D["Step 4: Build Roof - Inter-HOW Correlations"]:::step
    D --> D1["Identify trade-offs and synergies"]:::artifact
    D1 --> E["Step 5: Assign Customer Importance Ci"]:::step
    E --> E1["Survey based weight 1 to 5"]:::artifact
    E1 --> F["Step 6: Compute Tj using formula"]:::step
    F --> F1["Rank technical characteristics by Tj"]:::artifact
    F1 --> G["Step 7: Set Target Values"]:::step
    G --> G1["Engineering specification limits"]:::artifact
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram 3 — The Four Phases of QFD (Matrix Cascade)

QFD does not stop at one matrix. The HOQ of Phase 1 cascades into Phase 2, 3, and 4 matrices, each called a *Sub-matrix*. This is the complete QFD deployment.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    classDef phase fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309,stroke-width:2px,color:#000;

    P1["PHASE 1: Product Planning HOQ - Customer WHATs to Product HOWs"]:::phase
    P2["PHASE 2: Product Design Deployment - HOWs to Part Characteristics"]:::phase
    P3["PHASE 3: Process Planning HOQ - Part Characteristics to Process Parameters"]:::phase
    P4["PHASE 4: Production Planning HOQ - Process Parameters to Production Controls"]:::phase

    P1 ==>|"HOWs of Phase 1 become WHATs of Phase 2"| P2
    P2 ==>|"HOWs of Phase 2 become WHATs of Phase 3"| P3
    P3 ==>|"HOWs of Phase 3 become WHATs of Phase 4"| P4
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Emphasis:** Module 4 of PERUT636 focuses on **Phase 1 only — the original House of Quality**. However, examiners occasionally ask students to *name* the four phases. Memorize: **Product Planning → Product Design → Process Planning → Production Planning**.

## 4.4 Relationship Symbol Legend (Visual Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    classDef strong fill:#16a34a,stroke:#064e3b,color:#fff;
    classDef moderate fill:#facc15,stroke:#713f12,color:#000;
    classDef weak fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000;
    classDef none fill:#e5e7eb,stroke:#374151,color:#000;

    S["Strong - Solid Circle - Value 9"]:::strong
    M["Moderate - Open Circle - Value 3"]:::moderate
    W["Weak - Triangle - Value 1"]:::weak
    N["None - Blank - Value 0"]:::none

    S -->|"Strongly satisfies customer want"| M
    M -->|"Moderately satisfies"| W
    W -->|"Slightly affects"| N
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`
**Q: Define the House of Quality (HOQ). Mention any four components of the HOQ.**

**Model Answer:**
The House of Quality (HOQ) is a structured matrix used in Quality Function Deployment (QFD) that translates customer requirements (WHATs) into engineering characteristics (HOWs). It is called a "house" because its layout resembles a house when drawn on paper.

The four major components are:
1. **Customer Requirements (WHATs)** — listed on the left wall, capturing the voice of the customer.
2. **Technical Requirements (HOWs)** — listed on the ceiling, expressed as measurable engineering quantities.
3. **Relationship Matrix** — the central room, indicating the strength of relationship between each WHAT and each HOW using symbols (●, ○, △).
4. **Roof** — placed on top, showing the inter-relationships (synergies and trade-offs) among the technical requirements themselves.

*(Additional valid components: Right Wall — competitive assessment; Bottom — Technical Importance Ratings; Far Right — Customer Importance Ratings.)*

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks) `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`
**Q: List the standard symbols used in the QFD relationship matrix and their corresponding numerical weights.**

**Model Answer:**

| Symbol | Meaning | Weight (9-3-1 Scale) |
|--------|---------|:--------------------:|
| ● (Solid Circle) | Strong relationship | **9** |
| ○ (Open Circle) | Moderate relationship | **3** |
| △ (Triangle) | Weak relationship | **1** |
| Blank | No relationship | **0** |

These weights are then multiplied with the Customer Importance Rating ($C_i$) to compute the Technical Importance Rating ($T_j$) of each engineering characteristic.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question has **two sub-parts (a) and (b)**, each carrying **7 marks**. You must attempt **either the whole set OR the alternative set**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the step-by-step construction of the House of Quality (HOQ). Discuss the significance of the **Roof** of the House of Quality in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks)** A mobile phone manufacturer has identified three customer requirements: **Long battery life ($C_1 = 5$)**, **Lightweight ($C_2 = 4$)**, and **Affordable ($C_3 = 3$)**. The technical requirements identified are **Battery capacity (mAh)**, **Body mass (g)**, and **Production cost (₹)**. The relationship matrix is as follows:

| CR \ TR | Battery (mAh) | Body Mass (g) | Prod. Cost (₹) |
|---------|:-------------:|:-------------:|:--------------:|
| Battery Life (5) | 9 | 0 | 0 |
| Lightweight (4) | 3 | 9 | 0 |
| Affordable (3) | 0 | 3 | 9 |

Calculate the **Technical Importance Ratings (Tⱼ)** for each engineering characteristic and identify the **most critical** technical requirement. Justify your answer.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A

**(a) Construction of HOQ — Step-by-Step [7 Marks]**

The House of Quality is constructed in **seven sequential steps**:

1. **Step 1 — Identify Customer Requirements (WHATs):** Gather VOC through surveys, interviews, focus groups, complaint analysis. Express them in the customer's language (e.g., "easy to hold", "long lasting"). About 8–20 items are typical.

2. **Step 2 — Identify Technical Requirements (HOWs):** The engineering team translates WHATs into measurable specs (e.g., "tensile strength in MPa", "weight in grams"). About 10–40 items are typical.

3. **Step 3 — Populate the Central Relationship Matrix:** Cross-functional team fills each CR × TR cell using symbols ● (strong = 9), ○ (moderate = 3), △ (weak = 1), blank (0).

4. **Step 4 — Build the Roof (Inter-HOW Correlations):** A symmetric matrix showing how each HOW affects other HOWs. Uses ++, +, blank, −, − − symbols.

5. **Step 5 — Assign Customer Importance Ratings ($C_i$):** Customers rate each CR on a 1–5 scale; the mean rating is used.

6. **Step 6 — Compute Technical Importance Ratings ($T_j$):** Using $T_j = \sum C_i \cdot R_{ij}$. The TR with the highest $T_j$ is the design priority.

7. **Step 7 — Set Target Values:** Engineering team specifies acceptable limits and target values for each HOW.

**Significance of the Roof [included in marks]:**

The Roof is a symmetric triangular matrix placed on top of the ceiling that captures **inter-relationships among the engineering requirements themselves**. Its significance:

- **Exposes Trade-offs Early:** Negative correlations ( −, − − ) warn the designer that improving one specification may degrade another (e.g., increasing battery capacity → increasing weight).
- **Highlights Synergies:** Positive correlations ( +, ++ ) reveal engineering levers where one improvement multiplies the benefit of another.
- **Saves Cost:** Identifying conflicts before prototyping avoids expensive redesign cycles.
- **Drives Concurrent Engineering:** All design disciplines must negotiate simultaneously, breaking the silo mentality.
- **Documents Decisions:** Provides a permanent, auditable record of engineering trade-off reasoning.

**(b) Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Given:**

$$
C_1 = 5, \quad C_2 = 4, \quad C_3 = 3
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R = \begin{bmatrix}
9 & 0 & 0 \\
3 & 9 & 0 \\
0 & 3 & 9
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Formula:**

$$
T_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot R_{ij}
$$

**Computing $T_1$ (Battery Capacity in mAh):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= C_1 \cdot R_{1,1} + C_2 \cdot R_{2,1} + C_3 \cdot R_{3,1} \\
T_1 &= (5)(9) + (4)(3) + (3)(0) \\
T_1 &= 45 + 12 + 0 = 57
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computing $T_2$ (Body Mass in grams):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= C_1 \cdot R_{1,2} + C_2 \cdot R_{2,2} + C_3 \cdot R_{3,2} \\
T_2 &= (5)(0) + (4)(9) + (3)(3) \\
T_2 &= 0 + 36 + 9 = 45
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computing $T_3$ (Production Cost in ₹):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_3 &= C_1 \cdot R_{1,3} + C_2 \cdot R_{2,3} + C_3 \cdot R_{3,3} \\
T_3 &= (5)(0) + (4)(0) + (3)(9) \\
T_3 &= 0 + 0 + 27 = 27
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary Table:**

| Technical Requirement | $T_j$ | Rank |
|------------------------|:-----:|:----:|
| Battery Capacity (mAh) | **57** | **1st** |
| Body Mass (g) | **45** | 2nd |
| Production Cost (₹) | **27** | 3rd |
| **Total** | **129** | — |

**Conclusion:** The most critical technical requirement is **Battery Capacity (mAh)** with $T_1 = 57$, because it is driven by the highest-weighted customer requirement (Long battery life, $C_1 = 5$) and has a strong direct relationship (value 9) with it. The design team must prioritize battery capacity first.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE)** `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **four phases of QFD** with the role of each phase's matrix. Explain how the *HOWs* of one phase become the *WHATs* of the next phase.

**(b) [7 Marks]** For a ceiling fan design, the customer requirements and importance ratings are:
- **CR₁: Low noise** ($C_1 = 5$)
- **CR₂: High air delivery** ($C_2 = 4$)
- **CR₃: Energy efficient** ($C_3 = 5$)
- **CR₄: Long life** ($C_4 = 4$)

The technical requirements are **Motor wattage (W)**, **Blade angle (°)**. The relationship matrix is:

| CR \ TR | Motor Wattage (W) | Blade Angle (°) |
|---------|:-----------------:|:---------------:|
| Low noise (5) | 0 | 3 |
| High air (4) | 9 | 9 |
| Energy efficient (5) | 9 | 1 |
| Long life (4) | 3 | 0 |

Calculate **Technical Importance Ratings (Tⱼ)** for both technical requirements. Which TR should the design team focus on first, and why?

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B

**(a) Four Phases of QFD — Matrix Cascade [7 Marks]**

QFD is a multi-phase deployment of customer voice through every layer of the organization. The four phases are:

1. **Phase 1 — Product Planning (House of Quality):** Translates customer requirements (WHATs) into product-level technical characteristics (HOWs). This is the original HOQ.

2. **Phase 2 — Product Design Deployment:** The HOWs of Phase 1 (product characteristics) become the WHATs of Phase 2. They are decomposed into **sub-characteristics at the component/parts level** (e.g., "blade material" → "blade mass", "blade stiffness"). Output: a parts-level HOQ.

3. **Phase 3 — Process Planning:** The HOWs of Phase 2 (part characteristics) become the WHATs of Phase 3. They are translated into **manufacturing process parameters** (e.g., "injection molding temperature", "cycle time", "tolerance"). Output: a process HOQ.

4. **Phase 4 — Production Planning:** The HOWs of Phase 3 (process parameters) become the WHATs of Phase 4. They are mapped onto **day-to-day production controls** such as operator instructions, inspection plans, and tooling settings. Output: a production-control HOQ.

The **cascade logic** ensures that the *voice of the customer* propagates with mathematical traceability all the way down to the shop floor. Every production decision can be traced back, through a chain of HOW → WHAT → HOW matrices, to an original customer requirement.

**(b) Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Given:**

$$
C_1 = 5,\; C_2 = 4,\; C_3 = 5,\; C_4 = 4
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
R = \begin{bmatrix}
0 & 3 \\
9 & 9 \\
9 & 1 \\
3 & 0
\end{bmatrix}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computing $T_1$ (Motor Wattage):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_1 &= C_1 R_{1,1} + C_2 R_{2,1} + C_3 R_{3,1} + C_4 R_{4,1} \\
T_1 &= (5)(0) + (4)(9) + (5)(9) + (4)(3) \\
T_1 &= 0 + 36 + 45 + 12 = 93
\end{aligned}
$$

**Computing $T_2$ (Blade Angle):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_2 &= C_1 R_{1,2} + C_2 R_{2,2} + C_3 R_{3,2} + C_4 R_{4,2} \\
T_2 &= (5)(3) + (4)(9) + (5)(1) + (4)(0) \\
T_2 &= 15 + 36 + 5 + 0 = 56
\end{aligned}
$$

**Summary:**

| Technical Requirement | $T_j$ | Rank |
|------------------------|:-----:|:----:|
| Motor Wattage (W) | **93** | **1st** |
| Blade Angle (°) | **56** | 2nd |
| **Total** | **149** | — |

**Conclusion:** The design team must focus on **Motor Wattage** first, because its Technical Importance Rating ($T_1 = 93$) is significantly higher than that of Blade Angle ($T_2 = 56$). Motor Wattage is strongly driven by two highly-weighted customer requirements — High Air Delivery ($C_2 = 4$, relationship = 9) and Energy Efficiency ($C_3 = 5$, relationship = 9). Optimizing motor wattage will simultaneously improve the two highest-priority customer wants.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in QFD Matrix Questions:**
>
> 1. **Mismatched Scales:** Using 1/3/9 in some cells and 1/3/5 in others within the *same* problem → **2 marks lost**. Always declare the scale at the start of the solution.
> 2. **Forgetting the Customer Importance:** Computing $T_j$ as just the sum of relationship weights WITHOUT multiplying by $C_i$ → **entire 7 marks lost**.
> 3. **Wrong Direction in Roof:** Writing a non-symmetric Roof matrix → loses **2 marks**. The Roof is symmetric: if HOW-A strongly conflicts with HOW-B, then HOW-B strongly conflicts with HOW-A.
> 4. **Confusing WHATs with HOWs:** Stating *Tensile Strength* as a customer requirement → **loses 1 mark**. CRs must be in the customer's language; TRs must be measurable engineering quantities.
> 5. **Skipping Units:** Writing $T_j = 93$ without units → fine, but writing $T_j$ with units like *watts* → **loses 0.5 mark**. $T_j$ is unitless (a weighted sum).
> 6. **Not Normalizing:** Examiner may ask "what percentage priority does TR₁ have?" → forgetting $\%T_j = T_j / \sum T_j \times 100$ → **loses 1–2 marks**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **QFD** = **Quality Function Deployment**; its central tool is the **House of Quality (HOQ)**.
- HOQ = a single-page matrix that translates **Voice of Customer (WHATs)** into **Engineering Characteristics (HOWs)**.
- The House has **8 rooms**: Left Wall (CRs), Ceiling (TRs), Central Room (Relationship), Roof (Inter-HOW), Right Wall (Competitive), Far Right ($C_i$), Bottom ($T_j$), Far Bottom (Targets).
- Standard relationship weights: **● = 9**, **○ = 3**, **△ = 1**, **blank = 0** (the **9-3-1 scale**).
- **Roof** uses **++, +, blank, −, − −** to indicate HOW-to-HOW synergies and trade-offs.
- Core formula: $T_j = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot R_{ij}$ — multiplies customer importance by relationship strength, then sums across all CRs.
- Normalization formula: $\%T_j = (T_j / \sum T_j) \times 100$ — gives a percentage priority for each TR.
- Benchmarking extension: $T_j^{\text{norm}} = T_j \cdot I_j \cdot S_j$ where $I_j$ = improvement ratio, $S_j$ = sales point.
- **Four Phases of QFD**: Product Planning → Product Design → Process Planning → Production Planning. The *HOWs* of each phase become the *WHATs* of the next.
- The TR with the **highest $T_j$** is the **most critical-to-quality (CTQ)** engineering characteristic and is designed first.
- The Roof prevents **engineering trade-offs from being discovered late** — it is a pre-design conflict-mapping tool.
- HOQ is the **front-end of DMAIC** — it links the *Define* phase to the *Measure* phase of Six Sigma.
- Always state the **scale used (9-3-1)** at the start of any numerical QFD answer.
- $T_j$ is **unitless**; CRs have importance ratings **1 to 5**; TRs must be **measurable** (with SI units).
- The customer must always be the **anchor**: every engineering choice must trace back to a CR via a non-zero $R_{ij}$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Total Quality Management Concepts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Total Quality Management Concepts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of Total Quality Management (TQM)

**Total Quality Management (TQM)** is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that seeks to embed quality consciousness into every process, product, service, and human interaction of an enterprise. It is a *system of continuous improvement* that involves all members of the organization — from top management to shop-floor workers — in a coordinated effort to meet or exceed customer expectations at the lowest possible cost.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition:**
> *TQM is a customer-focused, process-centered, people-driven, and improvement-oriented management system that integrates fundamental management techniques, existing improvement efforts, and technical tools under a disciplined approach to continuously improve quality, productivity, and competitiveness.*

In simple, exam-ready terms:

$$
\text{TQM} = \text{Quality} \times \text{Everyone} \times \text{Every Process} \times \text{Continuous Improvement}
$$

The word **"Total"** has three distinct meanings embedded inside it:

1. **Total Quality** – Quality at every stage, not just at the finished product stage.
2. **Total Involvement** – Quality is the responsibility of *all* employees, not just the Quality Department.
3. **Total System** – Quality is built into the entire organization's *system*, encompassing suppliers, production, distribution, and after-sales service.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board-Examiner's Lens:**
> When defining TQM in exams, *always* emphasize the four pillars: **(i) Customer Focus, (ii) Total Employee Involvement, (iii) Process-Centered Approach, and (iv) Continuous Improvement.** This phrase-list orientation is a guaranteed scoring line.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Hospital Kitchen" Intuition

Imagine a large hospital kitchen preparing meals for 1,000 patients. Under the *old way* (traditional management), only the head chef tastes the food at the end and decides if it is good.

Under **TQM**:
- The vegetable washer checks the freshness of the tomatoes *before* cutting.
- The cook monitors oil temperature *during* frying.
- The dietitian verifies nutritional balance *after* plating.
- The delivery boy ensures the food reaches the correct ward *on time* at the correct temperature.
- The patient (customer) gives feedback via a *feedback form* that triggers improvements the next day.

**No single person "owns" quality. Every step is a quality checkpoint.** This is the essence of TQM — quality is not a destination, it is the *journey of every process*.

## 1.3 The Three TQM "Total" Dimensions (KTU-Favorite)

| Dimension | Meaning | Example in Industry |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total Quality** | Quality embedded in all products, services, and processes | From supplier receiving to after-sales support |
| **Total Employee Involvement** | Every employee, from CEO to sweeper, participates | Quality Circles, Suggestion Schemes |
| **Total System Integration** | All functions (HR, Finance, Marketing) work in harmony | ISO 9001-based Quality Management System |

## 1.4 Origin and Evolution (Background for Essay Questions)

- **1920s–1940s:** Statistical Quality Control (Shewhart) — focus on inspection.
- **1950s–1960s:** Quality Assurance (Juran, Feigenbaum) — focus on the *system*.
- **1970s–1980s:** Strategic Quality Management (Japanese Industry) — focus on *company-wide* quality.
- **1980s–Present:** **Total Quality Management** — focus on *total* organization-wide transformation.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Shortcut:** If asked "How is TQM different from traditional quality control?" — write:
> *Traditional QC = Detection oriented, Departmental, Reactive, Inspector-based. TQM = Prevention oriented, Organization-wide, Proactive, Employee-driven.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Eight Core Principles of TQM (ISO-Based + KTU-Standard)

The KTU 2024 syllabus specifically tests the following **Eight Principles of TQM**, which are universally accepted and appear repeatedly in past papers.

### Principle 1 — Customer Focus
- The **customer** (internal or external) is the ultimate judge of quality.
- Organizations must understand current and future customer needs, meet their requirements, and *exceed their expectations*.
- **KTU Mantra:** *"Customer is the final inspector."*

### Principle 2 — Leadership and Top Management Commitment
- Leaders establish unity of purpose, direction, and an environment that encourages full participation.
- Without top management commitment, TQM collapses. **80% of TQM failures are due to weak leadership commitment**.

### Principle 3 — Involvement of People
- People at all levels are the essence of an organization; their full involvement enables their abilities to be used for the organization's benefit.
- Mechanisms: **Quality Circles, Suggestion Systems, Cross-Functional Teams, Kaizen Teams**.

### Principle 4 — Process Approach
- A desired result is achieved more efficiently when activities and related resources are managed as a *process*.
- **Inputs → Process → Outputs → Feedback**.

### Principle 5 — System Approach to Management
- Identifying, understanding, and managing interrelated processes as a *system* contributes to the organization's effectiveness in achieving its objectives.

### Principle 6 — Continual Improvement
- Continual improvement of the organization's overall performance should be a permanent objective.
- Driven by **PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel)**.

### Principle 7 — Factual Approach to Decision Making
- Effective decisions are based on the **analysis of data and information**, not intuition alone.
- Tools: **SPC, Pareto Charts, Histograms, Cause-and-Effect Diagrams, Scatter Diagrams**.

### Principle 8 — Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relationships
- An organization and its suppliers are *interdependent*; a mutually beneficial relationship enhances the ability of both to create value.

## 2.2 The TQM Gurus and Their Contributions (High-Yield Table)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU frequently asks: *"Explain the contribution of Deming/Juran/Crosby/Ishikawa to TQM"* — this table is your exam armor.

| Guru | Core Contribution | Famous Concept / Quote |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **W. Edwards Deming** | 14 Points for Management, Statistical Process Control (SPC) | *"In God we trust, all others must bring data."* |
| **Joseph M. Juran** | Juran Trilogy (Quality Planning, Control, Improvement) | *"Fitness for Use."* |
| **Philip B. Crosby** | Zero Defects concept, "Quality is Free" | *"Quality is conformance to requirements."* |
| **Armand V. Feigenbaum** | Total Quality Control (originator of the term "Total Quality") | Concept of *Hidden Plant* (15–40% of capacity wasted on rework) |
| **Kaoru Ishikawa** | Quality Circles, Cause-and-Effect (Fishbone) Diagram | *"Quality begins and ends with training."* |
| **Genichi Taguchi** | Quality Loss Function, Robust Design | Quality loss starts the moment specification is deviated from target |
| **Walter Shewhart** | Father of Statistical Quality Control | PDCA Cycle (later popularized by Deming) |

## 2.3 Deming's 14 Points (KTU Hot List)

1. Create constancy of purpose for improvement of product and service.
2. Adopt the new philosophy.
3. Cease dependence on inspection to achieve quality.
4. End the practice of awarding business on price alone.
5. Improve constantly every process for planning, production, and service.
6. Institute training on the job.
7. Institute leadership.
8. Drive out fear.
9. Break down barriers between departments.
10. Eliminate slogans and exhortations.
11. Eliminate numerical quotas.
12. Remove barriers to pride of workmanship.
13. Institute a vigorous programme of education and self-improvement.
14. Put everybody to work to accomplish the transformation.

## 2.4 Cost of Quality (COQ) — The KTU Numerical Foundation

> [!NOTE]
> Cost of Quality is the **most numerically-tested TQM sub-topic** in KTU ESE papers.

The **Cost of Quality (COQ)** is the total cost incurred to ensure quality plus the cost of failure to ensure quality. It is divided into four major categories:

$$
\text{Total Cost of Quality (COQ)} = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}
$$

| Category | What It Includes | When It Is Incurred |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Prevention Costs** | Training, Quality Planning, Supplier Capability Assessment, Process Capability Studies, Design Reviews | *Before* the defect occurs |
| **Appraisal Costs** | Incoming Inspection, In-Process Inspection, Final Testing, Calibration, Audits | *During* detection of defects |
| **Internal Failure Costs** | Scrap, Rework, Re-inspection, Downtime, Material Review | *Before* reaching customer |
| **External Failure Costs** | Warranty Claims, Returns, Repairs, Legal Costs, Loss of Goodwill, Customer Complaints | *After* reaching customer |

### KTU Formula Sheet

| Formula | Meaning | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $\text{COQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$ | Total Cost of Quality | ₹ / $ |
| $\text{Quality Loss Rate} = \frac{L(x)}{A}$ | Taguchi's quality loss normalized to area $A$ | Loss per unit |
| $\text{COPQ Ratio} = \frac{C_{IF} + C_{EF}}{C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}} \times 100$ | % of cost due to failures | % |
| $\text{Rework Cost} = R_c \times N_r \times t_r$ | Rework total cost (count × time × rate) | ₹ |
| $\text{Warranty Liability} = N_w \times C_w$ | Number of warranty claims × cost per claim | ₹ |

## 2.5 The PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel / Shewhart Cycle)

The **Plan-Do-Check-Act (PDCA)** cycle is the engine of continuous improvement in TQM.

- **Plan:** Identify the problem, analyze, develop a plan for improvement.
- **Do:** Implement the plan on a small scale.
- **Check:** Measure the results and compare with expected outcomes.
- **Act:** Standardize the improvement or restart the cycle if not satisfied.

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world utility:** PDCA is used in ISO 9001 QMS audits, Six Sigma DMAIC (Define-Measure-Analyze-Improve-Control), Lean Kaizen, and even in hospital patient-safety programs.

## 2.6 TQM Implementation Framework (Step-by-Step)

The KTU textbook recommends the **5-phase implementation model**:

1. **Phase 1 — Top Management Commitment:** Visible, unwavering leadership commitment.
2. **Phase 2 — Awareness and Training:** Educate all employees about TQM tools and philosophy.
3. **Phase 3 — Goal Setting and Planning:** Define SMART quality goals.
4. **Phase 4 — Measurement and Feedback:** Establish metrics, KPIs, customer feedback loops.
5. **Phase 5 — Recognition and Continuation:** Reward quality achievements, sustain the culture.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Problem 1 — Cost of Quality Calculation (Model Numerical)

> **Problem Statement:**
> A manufacturing company reports the following annual quality-related costs:
> - Prevention Costs (Training, Quality Planning) = **₹ 50,000**
> - Appraisal Costs (Inspection, Testing) = **₹ 30,000**
> - Internal Failure Costs (Rework, Scrap) = **₹ 80,000**
> - External Failure Costs (Warranty, Returns) = **₹ 90,000**
>
> Calculate:
> (a) Total Cost of Quality (COQ).
> (b) COPQ Ratio (Cost of Poor Quality as % of COQ).
> (c) Comment on which area needs urgent attention.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify and Tabulate the Costs**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= 50{,}000 \quad \text{(Prevention)} \\
C_A &= 30{,}000 \quad \text{(Appraisal)} \\
C_{IF} &= 80{,}000 \quad \text{(Internal Failure)} \\
C_{EF} &= 90{,}000 \quad \text{(External Failure)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the Total COQ Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COQ} &= C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\
\text{COQ} &= 50{,}000 + 30{,}000 + 80{,}000 + 90{,}000 \\
\text{COQ} &= 2{,}50{,}000 \ \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (a):** Total Cost of Quality = **₹ 2,50,000**

**Step 3 — Calculate the COPQ Ratio**

COPQ (Cost of Poor Quality) is the sum of *failure costs* (internal + external):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ} &= C_{IF} + C_{EF} \\
\text{COPQ} &= 80{,}000 + 90{,}000 = 1{,}70{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COPQ Ratio} &= \frac{\text{COPQ}}{\text{Total COQ}} \times 100 \\
&= \frac{1{,}70{,}000}{2{,}50{,}000} \times 100 \\
&= 68\%
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Answer (b):** COPQ Ratio = **68%**

**Step 4 — Managerial Interpretation**

> **Answer (c):** A COPQ of **68%** is alarmingly high (industry benchmark ≤ 40%). External Failure Cost (₹ 90,000) is the largest single component, indicating that defective products are reaching the *customer*. The firm should urgently **increase Prevention Costs** (training, robust design) to eliminate the root cause — *every additional ₹ 1 spent on prevention typically saves ₹ 10 in failure costs* (Crosby's "1-10-100 Rule").

## 3.2 The Crosby 1-10-100 Rule (Verification Logic)

> **Problem Statement:** A company has 100 defective items detected at different stages. Calculate the relative cost of correction at each stage using Crosby's rule.

| Stage | Ratio Multiplier | Cost per Defect (₹) | Total for 100 Defects |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| At Source (Prevention) | **× 1** | 1 | ₹ 100 |
| At Inspection (Appraisal) | **× 10** | 10 | ₹ 1,000 |
| At Customer (External Failure) | **× 100** | 100 | ₹ 10,000 |

**Logic Step:** If a defect is detected at the *source* by the operator, it costs ₹ 1. If detected at *incoming inspection*, it costs ₹ 10. If it escapes to the *customer*, it costs ₹ 100. The exponential jump is the *economic justification for TQM's prevention-first approach*.

$$
\boxed{\text{Cost}_{\text{Customer}} = 100 \times \text{Cost}_{\text{Source}}}
$$

## 3.3 PDCA Cycle — Mathematical Model of Iterative Improvement

Let $Q_t$ denote the quality level at iteration $t$. The PDCA cycle mathematically improves quality by a fixed *improvement factor* $\alpha$ each cycle:

$$
Q_{t+1} = Q_t \cdot (1 + \alpha)
$$

where $\alpha$ is the **continuous improvement factor** (typically $0.01 \le \alpha \le 0.05$ for mature systems).

**Derivation of cumulative improvement over n cycles:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= Q_0 (1 + \alpha) \\
Q_2 &= Q_1 (1 + \alpha) = Q_0 (1 + \alpha)^2 \\
\vdots \\
Q_n &= Q_0 (1 + \alpha)^n
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:** A process starts with 100 defects/month. With $\alpha = 0.10$ (10% reduction per PDCA cycle), find defects after 5 cycles.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Q_5 &= 100 \times (1 - 0.10)^5 \\
&= 100 \times (0.90)^5 \\
&= 100 \times 0.59049 \\
&= 59.049 \approx 59 \text{ defects}
\end{aligned}
$$

This shows that with **disciplined PDCA cycles**, a 41% reduction in defects is achievable in just 5 iterations.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — When to Use Which TQM Tool

| TQM Tool | Best Used For | Numerical Output |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Pareto Chart** | Identifying the "vital few" causes | % cumulative frequency |
| **Cause-and-Effect (Ishikawa)** | Root cause brainstorming | Categorical |
| **Control Chart (SPC)** | Process stability over time | UCL, LCL, CL |
| **Histogram** | Distribution of process output | Mean, SD |
| **Scatter Diagram** | Correlation between two variables | Correlation coefficient $r$ |
| **Check Sheet** | Data collection for frequency analysis | Frequency counts |
| **Flow Chart** | Process mapping | Step-by-step visual |

## 3.5 Quality Loss Function (Taguchi) — Derivation

Taguchi defined quality loss as a quadratic function of deviation from target:

$$
L(x) = k(x - T)^2
$$

where:
- $L(x)$ = Quality loss when characteristic is $x$
- $T$ = Target value
- $k$ = Proportionality constant (cost coefficient)
- $x$ = Actual value of the quality characteristic

**Determination of k from Specification Limits:**

If the specification limit is $x = \Delta_0$ and the customer-acceptable loss at the limit is $A_0$:

$$
A_0 = k \cdot \Delta_0^2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = \frac{A_0}{\Delta_0^2}
$$

**Average Loss over a range:**

$$
\bar{L} = k \left( \sigma^2 + \mu^2 \right) - 2kT\mu + kT^2
$$

where $\mu$ is the process mean and $\sigma^2$ is the process variance.

> **Key Insight:** Loss is incurred *the moment* the output deviates from target — even if it remains within specification limits. This is the *TQM philosophy* in mathematical form.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The TQM House / Pyramid — Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Top Management Commitment and Leadership] --> B[Customer Focus and Satisfaction]
    B --> C[Total Employee Involvement]
    C --> D[Process Management and SPC]
    D --> E[Continual Improvement PDCA Cycle]
    E --> F[Supplier Partnership and Integration]
    F --> G[Reduced Cost of Quality]
    G --> A
    A --> H[Strategic Quality Goals]
    H --> I[Quality Planning]
    I --> J[Quality Control]
    J --> K[Quality Improvement]
    K --> E
    style A fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style B fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style E fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style G fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Interpretation:** This architecture shows TQM as a *self-reinforcing loop* — leadership commitment drives customer focus, which mobilizes employees, who manage processes, which feed the PDCA improvement engine, which strengthens supplier partnerships, which ultimately *reduces cost of quality* and *feeds back* into leadership vision.

## 4.2 Deming's PDCA Cycle — Continuous Improvement Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Plan Identify Problem and Analyze] --> D1[Do Implement on Small Scale]
    D1 --> C1[Check Measure Results and Compare]
    C1 --> A1[Act Standardize or Improve Again]
    A1 --> P1
    style P1 fill:#3498db,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D1 fill:#e67e22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C1 fill:#27ae60,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A1 fill:#c0392b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.3 Cost of Quality — Composition Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    TQ[Total Cost of Quality COQ] --> PR[Prevention Cost]
    TQ --> AP[Appraisal Cost]
    TQ --> IF[Internal Failure Cost]
    TQ --> EF[External Failure Cost]
    PR --> X1[Training and Education]
    PR --> X2[Quality Planning]
    PR --> X3[Process Capability Studies]
    AP --> Y1[Incoming Inspection]
    AP --> Y2[In-Process Inspection]
    AP --> Y3[Final Testing and Calibration]
    IF --> Z1[Scrap and Rework]
    IF --> Z2[Re-inspection Cost]
    IF --> Z3[Downtime Losses]
    EF --> W1[Warranty Claims]
    EF --> W2[Customer Returns]
    EF --> W3[Loss of Goodwill and Legal Costs]
    style TQ fill:#2c3e50,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style PR fill:#16a085,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style AP fill:#2980b9,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style IF fill:#f39c12,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style EF fill:#c0392b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

## 4.4 TQM Implementation Roadmap — 5-Phase Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Phase 1 Top Management Commitment] --> S2[Phase 2 Awareness and Training]
    S2 --> S3[Phase 3 Goal Setting and Planning]
    S3 --> S4[Phase 4 Measurement and Feedback]
    S4 --> S5[Phase 5 Recognition and Continuation]
    S5 --> S1
    style S1 fill:#1abc9c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S2 fill:#3498db,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S3 fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S4 fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style S5 fill:#f39c12,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Question]**

### **Q1. Define Total Quality Management (TQM) and list any four of its principles.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Total Quality Management is a comprehensive, organization-wide management philosophy that aims to embed quality into every process, product, and person by focusing on customer satisfaction, employee involvement, and continuous improvement.

The four principles are:
1. **Customer Focus** — Customer is the ultimate judge of quality.
2. **Total Employee Involvement** — Every employee, from CEO to operator, is responsible.
3. **Process-Centered Approach** — Activities managed as a process.
4. **Continual Improvement** — Permanent pursuit of improvement using the PDCA cycle.

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** Students often lose 1 mark by writing only *philosophy* without *principles*. Always back the definition with the four pillars.

---

### **Q2. What is the "Cost of Quality" (COQ)? Mention its four categories.**
*(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**
Cost of Quality is the total cost an organization incurs to ensure product/service quality plus the cost of failure to ensure quality. It is a key TQM metric.

Four categories:
- **Prevention Cost** — Cost incurred *before* defects occur (training, planning).
- **Appraisal Cost** — Cost of *detecting* defects (inspection, testing).
- **Internal Failure Cost** — Cost of defects found *before* delivery (rework, scrap).
- **External Failure Cost** — Cost of defects found *after* delivery (warranty, returns).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Full Internal Choice Format]**

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **(a) Explain the contributions of W. Edwards Deming, Joseph Juran, and Philip Crosby to Total Quality Management.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) W. Edwards Deming (1900–1993):**
- Deming is the *Father of Statistical Quality Control* in the modern sense.
- He gave the **14 Points for Management**, a holistic philosophy emphasizing system thinking, constancy of purpose, and elimination of fear and barriers.
- He popularized the **PDCA Cycle (Deming Wheel)** as the engine of continuous improvement.
- He emphasized that quality cannot be inspected into a product — it must be *built in*.
- His philosophy transformed Japanese industry post-WWII and later the US auto industry.

**(ii) Joseph M. Juran (1904–2008):**
- Defined quality as **"Fitness for Use"** — quality is determined by the customer.
- Proposed the **Juran Trilogy** consisting of:
  1. **Quality Planning** — Identify customers and their needs.
  2. **Quality Control** — Evaluate actual performance vs goals.
  3. **Quality Improvement** — Establish infrastructure for continuous improvement.
- Focused on the *human* and *managerial* aspects of quality, not just statistics.

**(iii) Philip B. Crosby (1926–2001):**
- Defined quality as **"Conformance to Requirements."**
- Famous for the concept of **Zero Defects** — the philosophy of doing it right the first time.
- Authored the influential book *"Quality is Free"* (1979), arguing that the cost of prevention is always less than the cost of correction.
- Proposed the **"1-10-100 Rule"** — a defect costs ₹ 1 to fix at source, ₹ 10 at inspection, ₹ 100 at customer.

**Comparative Synthesis:**
| Aspect | Deming | Juran | Crosby |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Quality Definition | Continuous Improvement | Fitness for Use | Conformance to Requirements |
| Major Tool | 14 Points, PDCA | Juran Trilogy | Zero Defects, 1-10-100 Rule |
| Primary Focus | System and Variation | Human and Managerial | Prevention and Mindset |

---

#### **(b) With a neat block diagram, explain the PDCA Cycle and its role in TQM.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

The **PDCA Cycle** (also called the Deming Wheel or Shewhart Cycle) is a four-stage iterative model for continuous improvement.

**Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Plan Identify Problem and Set Goals] --> D[Do Implement on Pilot Scale]
    D --> C[Check Measure Results Against Goals]
    C --> A[Act Standardize or Revise the Plan]
    A --> P
    style P fill:#3498db,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style D fill:#e67e22,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style C fill:#27ae60,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style A fill:#c0392b,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Detailed Explanation of Each Stage:**

1. **Plan:**
   - Identify the problem or opportunity for improvement.
   - Analyze current state using tools like Pareto charts, histograms.
   - Set SMART goals (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound).
   - Develop a hypothesis and action plan.

2. **Do:**
   - Execute the plan on a small, controlled scale (pilot run).
   - Collect data systematically during execution.
   - Document all observations, deviations, and side effects.

3. **Check:**
   - Analyze the collected data.
   - Compare actual results with the expected outcomes from the Plan stage.
   - Identify gaps, root causes of variation, and learnings.

4. **Act:**
   - If the change was successful → **standardize** it across the organization, update SOPs, and train staff.
   - If the change was unsuccessful → **revise** the hypothesis and restart the cycle.
   - Identify the next opportunity for improvement → next PDCA cycle begins.

**Mathematical Model of Improvement:**

$$
Q_n = Q_0 (1 + \alpha)^n
$$

where $Q_0$ is the initial quality level, $\alpha$ is the improvement factor, and $n$ is the number of cycles.

**Role in TQM:**
- Provides a *systematic, scientific* method to drive continual improvement.
- Aligns with ISO 9001 QMS requirements and Six Sigma DMAIC framework.
- Empowers employees at all levels to solve problems using data.
- Ensures that improvements are *sustained* and not one-time events.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**

#### **(a) What is Cost of Quality (COQ)? Explain its four components with examples. Compute the total COQ for the following data and comment.** *(7 Marks)*

| Cost Element | Amount (₹) |
| :--- | :--- |
| Inspection and Testing | 40,000 |
| Warranty Repairs | 60,000 |
| Quality Training | 20,000 |
| Scrap and Rework | 50,000 |
| Process Capability Study | 15,000 |
| Customer Returns | 30,000 |

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
Cost of Quality (COQ) is the total cost incurred by an organization to achieve and assure a specified level of quality, comprising prevention, appraisal, internal failure, and external failure costs.

$$
\text{COQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}
$$

**Classifying Each Cost:**

| Cost Element | Classification |
| :--- | :--- |
| Inspection and Testing (₹ 40,000) | Appraisal Cost |
| Warranty Repairs (₹ 60,000) | External Failure Cost |
| Quality Training (₹ 20,000) | Prevention Cost |
| Scrap and Rework (₹ 50,000) | Internal Failure Cost |
| Process Capability Study (₹ 15,000) | Prevention Cost |
| Customer Returns (₹ 30,000) | External Failure Cost |

**Summing Each Category:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
C_P &= 20{,}000 + 15{,}000 = 35{,}000 \\
C_A &= 40{,}000 \\
C_{IF} &= 50{,}000 \\
C_{EF} &= 60{,}000 + 30{,}000 = 90{,}000
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total COQ:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{COQ} &= 35{,}000 + 40{,}000 + 50{,}000 + 90{,}000 \\
&= 2{,}15{,}000 \ \text{₹}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Commentary:**
The external failure cost (₹ 90,000) is 41.8% of total COQ — unacceptably high. It indicates that defective products are reaching customers. The management must **increase prevention spending** (e.g., invest in Design of Experiments, FMEA, robust supplier audits) so that defects are caught at the source rather than at the customer.

---

#### **(b) Explain Juran's Quality Trilogy in detail with a suitable diagram.** *(7 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

Joseph Juran's **Quality Trilogy** is a universal framework for managing quality in any organization. It consists of three interlinked managerial processes:

**Block Diagram of Juran's Trilogy:**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    QP[Quality Planning] --> QC[Quality Control]
    QC --> QI[Quality Improvement]
    QI --> QP
    QP --> A1[Identify Customers]
    QP --> A2[Determine Customer Needs]
    QP --> A3[Develop Product and Process]
    QP --> A4[Establish Quality Goals]
    QC --> B1[Choose Subjects of Control]
    QC --> B2[Establish Measurements]
    QC --> B3[Set Standards and Compare]
    QC --> B4[Take Corrective Action]
    QI --> C1[Prove Need for Improvement]
    QI --> C2[Identify Projects and Teams]
    QI --> C3[Establish Infrastructure]
    QI --> C4[Provide Training and Resources]
    style QP fill:#1abc9c,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style QC fill:#3498db,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style QI fill:#9b59b6,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Detailed Explanation:**

1. **Quality Planning:**
   - Identify *who* the customers are (internal and external).
   - Determine their *needs* and expectations.
   - Translate needs into product/service specifications.
   - Develop processes capable of meeting those specifications.
   - Establish quality goals and metrics.

2. **Quality Control:**
   - Evaluate actual quality performance against planned goals.
   - Identify the difference between actual and planned.
   - Take corrective action to close the gap.
   - Tools: Control Charts, Statistical Process Control, Inspection.

3. **Quality Improvement:**
   - Establish the infrastructure for ongoing improvement.
   - Identify specific projects for improvement (e.g., chronic waste, bottleneck processes).
   - Form improvement teams (Quality Circles, Six Sigma teams).
   - Provide training, resources, and recognition.
   - The goal is to *raise* quality performance to a new, higher level.

**Key Insight:**
Quality Planning establishes the *what* and *how* of meeting customer needs. Quality Control ensures *current* performance is maintained. Quality Improvement raises the *baseline* itself — making the organization progressively better, year after year.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Definition Marks:** Students often write TQM as "maintaining quality" or "quality control" — these are *detection-oriented* and not TQM. Always write "prevention-oriented, customer-focused, employee-driven."
> - **PDCA Marks:** Many students forget to mention the *cyclical* nature of PDCA — they write it linearly. The cycle *must return* to Plan.
> - **COQ Marks:** Students confuse *Internal Failure* with *External Failure*. Internal = before delivery to customer; External = after delivery. Mixing them loses 2–3 marks.
> - **Guru Marks:** When asked about a Guru, always give **(a) his definition of quality, (b) his main contribution, and (c) a famous quote.** This 3-part structure is the scoring blueprint.
> - **Diagram Marks:** A PDCA diagram without arrow labels (Plan, Do, Check, Act) is treated as incomplete and gets only 2 out of 7 marks. *Label every node and every arrow.*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **TQM = Total Quality + Total Involvement + Total System.**
- TQM is **prevention-oriented**, not detection-oriented.
- **Customer Focus** is the #1 principle; **Leadership Commitment** is the #1 enabler.
- **Deming:** 14 Points, PDCA, SPC, "In God we trust, all others must bring data."
- **Juran:** Quality Trilogy (Plan, Control, Improve), "Fitness for Use."
- **Crosby:** Zero Defects, 1-10-100 Rule, "Quality is Free," Conformance to Requirements.
- **Ishikawa:** Quality Circles, Fishbone Diagram.
- **Feigenbaum:** Originated "Total Quality Control"; concept of Hidden Plant.
- **Taguchi:** Quality Loss Function $L(x) = k(x-T)^2$; loss starts at the *smallest* deviation.
- **COQ Formula:** $\text{COQ} = C_P + C_A + C_{IF} + C_{EF}$.
- **COPQ Ratio** > 40% is a red flag — invest more in prevention.
- **PDCA is cyclical** — every "Act" leads to a new "Plan."
- **PDCA Improvement Model:** $Q_n = Q_0 (1 + \alpha)^n$.
- The **5 TQM Implementation Phases** are: Leadership → Awareness → Goals → Measurement → Recognition.
- **Crosby's 1-10-100 Rule:** Prevention is 100× cheaper than customer-side failure.
- TQM requires **cultural transformation**, not just tools and charts.
- **Eight TQM Principles** (ISO 9000-based): Customer Focus, Leadership, People Involvement, Process Approach, System Approach, Continual Improvement, Factual Decisions, Mutually Beneficial Supplier Relations.
- **Quality Loss Function** — loss occurs at *any* deviation from target, not just at specification limit.
- **Internal Failure** = defect caught before customer; **External Failure** = defect caught after customer.
- TQM is **not a program** — it is a *permanent culture*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quality Information Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quality Information Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Quality Information System (QIS):** A coordinated, computer-based framework comprising hardware, software, databases, procedures, and personnel that systematically collects, processes, stores, analyzes, and disseminates quality-related data to support decision-making at all levels of an organization. It transforms raw quality data into actionable information for continuous improvement, defect prevention, and customer satisfaction enhancement.

A **Quality Information System** is a specialized subset of the broader Management Information System (MIS), explicitly engineered to monitor, measure, and manage quality parameters across the **value chain** — from supplier inputs to customer outputs.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Hospital Analogy:** Think of a Quality Information System as the **central nervous system of a hospital**. The body (organization) has thousands of signals — blood pressure, temperature, X-rays (raw data). The nervous system (QIS) collects these signals, processes them through the brain (CPU/database), and presents clear diagnoses (information) to the doctor (manager) who decides on treatment (corrective action). Without it, the doctor would be blind, and the patient could not be treated effectively. In a factory, QIS plays exactly this role — it is the nervous system that detects quality "pain" before it becomes fatal to the business.

## 1.3 Core Characteristics of a Quality Information System

A robust QIS exhibits the following distinguishing features:

- **Data Integrity** – Single source of truth for all quality metrics
- **Real-Time Capability** – Timely feedback for process control
- **Traceability** – Complete audit trail from raw material to finished product
- **Decision-Support Orientation** – Aligned to PDCA (Plan-Do-Check-Act) cycle
- **Integration** – Seamless flow with ERP, MES, and supply chain systems
- **User-Friendly Interface** – Accessible to shop-floor workers and top management alike

## 1.4 Standard Metrics and Terminology

> [!NOTE]
> The following **standard quality metrics** are typically tracked by a QIS:
> - **DPMO** (Defects Per Million Opportunities)
> - **RFT** (Right First Time) percentage
> - **COPQ** (Cost of Poor Quality)
> - **Sigma Level** (Z-score)
> - **MTBF / MTTR** (Mean Time Between Failures / Mean Time To Repair)
> - **Customer Complaint Index (CCI)**

These metrics are stored, computed, and reported by the QIS on dashboards accessible to authorized personnel.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Architecture of a Quality Information System

A QIS follows a layered architecture that mirrors the classical **Data → Information → Knowledge → Wisdom (DIKW) Pyramid**. Each layer adds value and context.

### 2.1.1 The DIKW Hierarchy in QIS Context

| Layer | Definition | QIS Example |
|-------|-----------|-------------|
| **Data** | Raw, unprocessed facts | Sensor reading: `152.3 °C` |
| **Information** | Processed, contextualized data | "Temperature exceeded 150 °C set-point 3 times today" |
| **Knowledge** | Patterns and rules derived | "Spike correlates with Shift B handover" |
| **Wisdom** | Actionable insight | "Schedule recalibration of Furnace 2 before next Monday" |

### 2.1.2 Functional Components of QIS

A QIS is built from **five interrelated subsystems**:

1. **Data Acquisition Subsystem**
   - Sensors, gauges, automated inspection equipment
   - Manual entry stations and bar-code/RFID readers
   - Customer feedback portals

2. **Data Storage and Database Management Subsystem**
   - Centralized **Quality Database** (often SQL-based)
   - Master records for products, processes, suppliers, defects
   - Historical archival for trend analysis

3. **Data Processing and Analysis Subsystem**
   - **SPC (Statistical Process Control)** engines
   - **Pareto analysis**, fishbone correlations
   - Capability indices calculators (Cp, Cpk, Pp, Ppk)

4. **Reporting and Visualization Subsystem**
   - Dashboards (real-time KPIs)
   - Exception reports (Pareto charts, control charts)
   - Regulatory compliance reports (ISO 9001, IATF 16949, FDA)

5. **Decision Support and Action Subsystem**
   - **Corrective Action Requests (CAR)** workflows
   - Predictive alerts (machine learning-driven)
   - 8D problem-solving templates

## 2.2 Types of Information Systems in a Quality Framework

Quality decisions are made at three organizational levels, each requiring a different information system:

| Level | Information System | Purpose | Time Horizon |
|-------|-------------------|---------|--------------|
| **Operational** | TPS (Transaction Processing System) | Record inspections, test results | Real-time |
| **Tactical** | MIS (Management Information System) | Periodic quality summaries | Weekly/Monthly |
| **Strategic** | DSS / EIS (Decision Support / Executive Info System) | Long-term quality strategy | Quarterly/Yearly |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** QIS acts as the *integrating layer* across TPS, MIS, and DSS — it is **not a standalone system** but a **cross-functional information backbone**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Below is the consolidated formula and metric table for solving KTU numerical/analytical questions on this topic. Note the use of `\vert` instead of `\|` to keep markdown tables valid.

| Formula / Concept | Expression | Application | Unit / Boundary |
|-------------------|------------|-------------|-----------------|
| **DPMO** | $\text{DPMO} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Defects}}{\text{Units Produced} \times \text{Opportunities per Unit}} \times 10^6$ | Six Sigma metric | Defects per million opportunities |
| **Sigma Level (Z)** | $Z = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{\sigma}$ or from DPMO table | Process capability | Dimensionless |
| **Cp Index** | $C_p = \dfrac{\text{USL} - \text{LSL}}{6\sigma}$ | Process potential | $C_p \geq 1.33$ acceptable |
| **Cpk Index** | $C_{pk} = \min\!\left(\dfrac{\text{USL} - \mu}{3\sigma},\ \dfrac{\mu - \text{LSL}}{3\sigma}\right)$ | Process performance | $C_{pk} \geq 1.33$ acceptable |
| **Pareto Principle** | $80\%$ of problems from $20\%$ of causes | Defect prioritization | — |
| **First Pass Yield (FPY)** | $\text{FPY} = \dfrac{\text{Units passing first inspection}}{\text{Total units started}} \times 100\%$ | Process effectiveness | Percentage |
| **Rolled Throughput Yield (RTY)** | $\text{RTY} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{FPY}_i$ | Multi-stage yield | Percentage |
| **COPQ** | $\text{COPQ} = \text{Prevention Cost} + \text{Appraisal Cost} + \text{Internal Failure Cost} + \text{External Failure Cost}$ | Quality costing | Currency (INR/USD) |
