$$h(t) = \frac{t}{\sigma^2} \qquad \text{for} \quad t \ge 0$$

where $\sigma > 0$ is the **scale parameter** (units of time).

### Construction of Reliability Function

**Step 1 — Cumulative Hazard:**
$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{\tau}{\sigma^2}\, d\tau = \frac{t^2}{2\sigma^2}$$

**Step 2 — Reliability Function:**
$$R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\frac{t^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Probability Density Function:**
$$f(t) = \frac{t}{\sigma^2}\,\exp\!\left[-\frac{t^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$$

**Step 4 — Cumulative Distribution Function:**
$$F(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\frac{t^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Identity:** The Rayleigh Model is mathematically equivalent to a **Weibull Distribution** with shape parameter $\beta = 2$ and scale parameter $\alpha = \sigma\sqrt{2}$. Always remember this equivalence — it appears frequently in KTU problems.

---

## 2.3 Model 3 — Weibull Hazard Model (The Workhorse)

### Physical Meaning
The **Weibull Distribution** (proposed by Waloddi Weibull, 1951) is the *most versatile* parametric model in reliability engineering. It can represent **all three regions** of the bath-tub curve by adjusting a single shape parameter $\beta$:

- $\beta < 1$ → **Decreasing hazard** (infant mortality / early-life failures)
- $\beta = 1$ → **Constant hazard** (memoryless, reduces to Exponential)
- $\beta > 1$ → **Increasing hazard** (wear-out phase)

The hazard function is postulated as:

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\!\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta - 1} \qquad \text{for} \quad t \ge 0$$

where $\alpha > 0$ is the **scale parameter** (characteristic life at which $R = e^{-1} \approx 0.368$) and $\beta > 0$ is the **shape parameter**.

### Construction of Reliability Function

**Step 1 — Cumulative Hazard:**
$$H(t) = \int_{0}^{t} \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\!\left(\frac{\tau}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} d\tau = \left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}$$

**Step 2 — Reliability Function:**
$$R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

**Step 3 — Probability Density Function:**
$$f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\!\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}\exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

**Step 4 — CDF:**
$$F(t) = 1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Master Comparison Table — Print This Before Every KTU Exam**

| Quantity | **Linear Model** | **Rayleigh Model** | **Weibull Model** |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Parameters** | $h_0, b$ | $\sigma$ | $\alpha, \beta$ |
| **Hazard $h(t)$** | $h_0 + bt$ | $\dfrac{t}{\sigma^2}$ | $\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$ |
| **Cum. Hazard $H(t)$** | $h_0 t + \dfrac{b t^2}{2}$ | $\dfrac{t^2}{2\sigma^2}$ | $\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}$ |
| **Reliability $R(t)$** | $\exp\!\left[-h_0 t - \dfrac{b t^2}{2}\right]$ | $\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{t^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$ | $\exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$ |
| **PDF $f(t)$** | $(h_0 + bt)\exp\!\left[-h_0 t - \dfrac{b t^2}{2}\right]$ | $\dfrac{t}{\sigma^2}\exp\!\left[-\dfrac{t^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$ | $\dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}\exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$ |
| **CDF $F(t)$** | $1 - \exp\!\left[-h_0 t - \dfrac{b t^2}{2}\right]$ | $1 - \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{t^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$ | $1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$ |
| **MTTF** | No closed form (error function) | $\sigma\sqrt{\dfrac{\pi}{2}}$ | $\alpha\,\Gamma\!\left(1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ |
| **Special Case** | $b=0 \Rightarrow$ Exponential | $\beta=2$ Weibull | $\beta=1 \Rightarrow$ Exponential |
| **Best Application** | Gradual wear-out | Fatigue cracks | Universal fit |

### Engineering Utility — Real-World Mapping

- **Linear Model**: Capacitor degradation, gradual insulation breakdown.
- **Rayleigh Model**: Rolling-contact bearings, fatigue crack initiation, vector magnitude in CMM metrology.
- **Weibull Model**: Ball bearings, vacuum tubes, ceramic capacitors, wind turbine gearbox failures, **and every other component where you have failure time data and need a flexible model**.

---

## 2.5 Evaluation of Design by Tests — Brief Contextual Framework

Reliability tests feed data into the above parametric models through the following **standardized KTU-recognized test plans**:

1. **Attribute (Binomial) Testing** — Success/Failure outcomes only; uses Binomial confidence bounds.
2. **Variable (Chi-Square) Testing** — Records exact failure times; estimates $\lambda$ for exponential, or $\beta, \alpha$ for Weibull using MLE or probability plotting.
3. **Sequential Testing (Wald SPRT)** — Tests until a decision (accept/reject) is reached, minimizing average sample size.

The **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** procedure is the most common method used to extract Weibull parameters $\beta, \alpha$ from censored test data, but probability plotting on Weibull paper is the classical KTU-favored method for small samples.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Solutions

## 3.1 Derivation of MTTF for the Rayleigh Hazard Model

> **Problem:** Given a Rayleigh distribution with parameter $\sigma = 500$ hours, find the Mean Time To Failure (MTTF).

The MTTF for any continuous distribution is:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\, dt \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt$$

**Using the Reliability Integral Form:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\, dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left[-\frac{t^2}{2\sigma^2}\right] dt$$

**Apply substitution:** Let $u = \dfrac{t}{\sigma\sqrt{2}}$, so $t = \sigma\sqrt{2}\,u$ and $dt = \sigma\sqrt{2}\,du$.

When $t = 0 \Rightarrow u = 0$; when $t \to \infty \Rightarrow u \to \infty$.

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} \exp\!\left[-u^2\right] \sigma\sqrt{2}\, du = \sigma\sqrt{2} \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u^2}\, du$$

**Apply the Gaussian Integral Identity:** $\displaystyle\int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-u^2}\, du = \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$.

$$\text{MTTF} = \sigma\sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} = \sigma\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}}$$

**Numerical Evaluation** (with $\sigma = 500$ hr):

$$\text{MTTF} = 500 \times \sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}} = 500 \times \sqrt{1.5708} = 500 \times 1.2533 = 626.66 \text{ hours}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation of MTTF for the Weibull Hazard Model (General Case)

The general Weibull MTTF is:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\!\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}\exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right] dt$$

**Substitution:** Let $u = \left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}$, so $du = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha^{\beta}} t^{\beta - 1}\, dt$.

This implies $t^{\beta}\, du = \beta \cdot \dfrac{t^{\beta}}{\alpha^{\beta}}\, dt = \beta \cdot \left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta} dt$. We solve for $dt$:

$$dt = \frac{\alpha^{\beta}}{\beta\, t^{\beta - 1}}\, du$$

**Substitute into the integral:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\!\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1} e^{-u} \cdot \frac{\alpha^{\beta}}{\beta\, t^{\beta - 1}}\, du$$

**Simplify the pre-exponential factor:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot \frac{t^{\beta - 1}}{\alpha^{\beta - 1}} \cdot \frac{\alpha^{\beta}}{\alpha} \cdot \frac{1}{t^{\beta - 1}} \cdot e^{-u}\, du = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{t}{\alpha} \cdot e^{-u}\, du$$

But from our substitution, $u = \left(\dfrac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}$, which gives $\dfrac{t}{\alpha} = u^{1/\beta}$. Thus:

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{1/\beta}\, e^{-u}\, du = \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) \cdot \alpha$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Euler Gamma Function definition**: $\displaystyle\Gamma(z) = \int_{0}^{\infty} u^{z-1} e^{-u}\, du$. Here $z = 1 + \dfrac{1}{\beta}$.

$$\boxed{\text{MTTF}_{\text{Weibull}} = \alpha \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right)}$$

**Verification for Rayleigh ($\beta = 2$):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \alpha \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{2}\right) = \alpha \cdot \Gamma\!\left(\frac{3}{2}\right) = \alpha \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$$

Since Rayleigh $\alpha = \sigma\sqrt{2}$:

$$\text{MTTF} = \sigma\sqrt{2} \cdot \frac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2} = \sigma\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}} \quad \checkmark$$

**Verification for Exponential ($\beta = 1$):**

$$\text{MTTF} = \alpha \cdot \Gamma(2) = \alpha \cdot 1! = \alpha \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Characteristic Life (B-x Life) for Weibull

The **B(x) life** is the time by which $x\%$ of the population has failed. Setting $F(t_{B(x)}) = x/100$:

$$1 - \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t_{B(x)}}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right] = \frac{x}{100}$$

$$\exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t_{B(x)}}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right] = 1 - \frac{x}{100}$$

$$\left(\frac{t_{B(x)}}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta} = -\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{x}{100}\right)$$

$$\boxed{t_{B(x)} = \alpha \left[-\ln\!\left(1 - \frac{x}{100}\right)\right]^{1/\beta}}$$

**Particularly useful:** $B(10)$ life (10% failure time, often used as design life):

$$t_{B(10)} = \alpha \left[-\ln(0.9)\right]^{1/\beta} = \alpha\,(0.10536)^{1/\beta}$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Linear Hazard Model Parameter Estimation

> **Problem:** A component undergoes a 1000-hour life test. 8 identical units are tested; 5 fail at times (hr): 120, 285, 410, 680, 900. The remaining 3 are censored at 1000 hr. Assuming a Linear Hazard model, derive the MLE equations and solve approximately.

The Linear Hazard model PDF and Reliability for $b = 0$ (Exponential assumption, simplest case) would give MLE $\hat{\lambda} = \dfrac{r}{\sum t_i + (n-r)T}$ where $r = 5$ failures, $n = 8$, $T = 1000$.

**Sum of failure times:**
$$\sum t_i = 120 + 285 + 410 + 680 + 900 = 2395 \text{ hr}$$

**Total test time (accounting for censoring):**
$$T_{\text{total}} = 2395 + 3 \times 1000 = 5395 \text{ hr}$$

**MLE estimate (under Exponential assumption, $\hat{\lambda}$):**

$$\hat{\lambda} = \frac{5}{5395} = 9.268 \times 10^{-4} \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

**MTTF estimate:**

$$\widehat{\text{MTTF}} = \frac{1}{\hat{\lambda}} = \frac{5395}{5} = 1079 \text{ hours}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In practice, for a Linear Hazard model, the MLE equations must be solved numerically (Newton-Raphson method) as they do not have closed-form solutions. KTU exam questions typically provide one parameter and ask for the other.

---

## 3.5 Worked Example — Weibull Parameter Estimation by Probability Plotting

> **Problem:** 10 ball bearings are tested to failure. Failure times (in hours): 3500, 4100, 4800, 5500, 6200, 7100, 8000, 8800, 9500, 10500. Estimate the Weibull parameters $\beta$ and $\alpha$.

**Step 1 — Compute the median rank (plotting position) for each failure:**

$$F_i = \frac{i - 0.3}{n + 0.4} \quad \text{(median rank formula)}$$

| $i$ | $t_i$ | $F_i$ |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | 3500 | 0.0675 |
| 2 | 4100 | 0.1654 |
| 3 | 4800 | 0.2633 |
| 4 | 5500 | 0.3612 |
| 5 | 6200 | 0.4591 |
| 6 | 7100 | 0.5570 |
| 7 | 8000 | 0.6549 |
| 8 | 8800 | 0.7528 |
| 9 | 9500 | 0.8507 |
| 10 | 10500 | 0.9486 |

**Step 2 — Transform variables for linearization:** Take $\ln[-\ln(1-F)]$ vs $\ln(t)$ — these should be linear with slope $\beta$ and intercept $-\beta \ln(\alpha)$.

For $i = 5$ (middle point): $F = 0.4591$, so $1-F = 0.5409$, $-\ln(0.5409) = 0.6145$, $\ln(0.6145) = -0.4866$. Also $\ln(6200) = 8.733$.

**Step 3 — Linear regression (slope = $\hat{\beta}$):**
$$\hat{\beta} \approx 2.4, \quad \hat{\alpha} \approx 6800 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — MTTF estimate:**

$$\widehat{\text{MTTF}} = 6800 \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{2.4}\right) = 6800 \cdot \Gamma(1.4167) \approx 6800 \times 0.886 \approx 6025 \text{ hours}$$

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Reliability Modeling Flow

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of Hazard Model Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Start Reliability Test] --> B0[Collect Failure Time Data]
    B0 --> C0{Test has exact failure times?}
    C0 -->|Yes| D0[Variable Data Test]
    C0 -->|No| E0[Attribute Test - Use Binomial Bounds]

    D0 --> F0{Plot data on Hazard axes}
    F0 --> G0[Linear trend in h-t?]
    F0 --> H0[h increases quadratically?]
    F0 --> I0[General curved trend?]

    G0 -->|Yes| J0[Fit LINEAR HAZARD MODEL]
    G0 -->|No| K0[Go to next check]

    H0 -->|Yes| L0[Fit RAYLEIGH MODEL - Weibull Beta=2]
    H0 -->|No| M0[Go to next check]

    I0 -->|Yes| N0[Fit WEIBULL MODEL - Estimate Beta and Alpha]

    J0 --> O0[Use MLE Newton-Raphson]
    L0 --> P0[Estimate sigma via MTTF equals sigma sqrt pi/2]
    N0 --> Q0[Use MLE or Probability Plotting]

    O0 --> R0[Compute MTTF, B10 Life, Confidence Bounds]
    P0 --> R0
    Q0 --> R0

    R0 --> S0[Make Design Accept or Reject Decision]

    style S0 fill:#166534,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style J0 fill:#b45309,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style L0 fill:#b45309,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
    style N0 fill:#b45309,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff,stroke-width:2px
```

## 4.2 Hazard Rate Behavior Comparison Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SUB1[Hazard Rate Behavior Models]
        A1[Linear Model] --> A2[h of t equals h0 plus b t]
        A2 --> A3[Linear increase starting from h0]

        B1[Rayleigh Model] --> B2[h of t equals t divided by sigma squared]
        B2 --> B3[Starts at 0 and rises quadratically]

        C1[Weibull Model] --> C2[h of t equals beta divided by alpha times t over alpha to power beta minus 1]
        C2 --> C3{Beta parameter region}

        C3 -->|Beta less than 1| D1[Decreasing hazard - Infant mortality]
        C3 -->|Beta equals 1| D2[Constant hazard - Exponential model]
        C3 -->|Beta greater than 1| D3[Increasing hazard - Wear-out phase]
        C3 -->|Beta equals 2| D4[Collapses to Rayleigh model]
    end

    style A1 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    style B1 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    style C1 fill:#0e7490,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    style D4 fill:#7c2d12,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
```

## 4.3 Reliability Test Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Define Reliability Target R_target and Confidence Level] --> Q0{Choose Test Plan}
    Q0 -->|Fixed Sample| R0[Binomial Test or Chi-Square Test]
    Q0 -->|Minimize Sample Size| S0[Sequential Probability Ratio Test SPRT]

    R0 --> T0[Conduct Test - Record Times]
    S0 --> T0

    T0 --> U0{Failure count vs Accept or Reject Bounds}
    U0 -->|Within Accept| V0[ACCEPT Design]
    U0 -->|Beyond Reject| W0[REJECT Design]
    U0 -->|Continue| X0[Test More Units]

    X0 --> U0

    V0 --> Y0[Fit Hazard Model to Estimate Beta and Alpha]
    W0 --> Z0[Redesign and Retest]

    Y0 --> AA0[Compute B10 Life and MTTF]
    AA0 --> AB0[Reliability Confirmed - Proceed to Production]

    style V0 fill:#15803d,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff
    style W0 fill:#b91c1c,stroke:#000000,color:#ffffff

```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Define the hazard function. Explain how the Linear hazard model represents a generalized form of the exponential distribution.**

**Model Answer:**

The **hazard function** $h(t)$ is the instantaneous rate of failure at time $t$, conditional on survival until $t$. It is defined mathematically as:

$$h(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

The Linear Hazard Model has the form $h(t) = h_0 + bt$.

When $b = 0$, the model reduces to $h(t) = h_0$, which is a **constant hazard** representing the **Exponential Distribution** with parameter $\lambda = h_0$. The corresponding reliability function becomes:

$$R(t) = e^{-h_0 t}$$

Thus the Linear Hazard Model is a direct generalization — it adds a time-dependent linear wear-out term $bt$ on top of the constant failure rate $h_0$, capturing both random failures (constant component) and gradual wear-out (linear component) within a single unified model.

> **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 for definition, 1 for showing the reduction to exponential, 1 for explanation of generalization.]**

---

### Question 2: `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**State the hazard function and reliability function for the Weibull distribution. Explain the significance of the shape parameter $\beta$.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Weibull Hazard Function**:

$$h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\alpha}\!\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta-1}$$

The **Weibull Reliability Function**:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{t}{\alpha}\right)^{\beta}\right]$$

**Significance of Shape Parameter $\beta$:**

- If $\beta < 1$: $h(t)$ is **decreasing** — represents infant mortality failures (early-life defects).
- If $\beta = 1$: $h(t)$ is **constant** — reduces to the Exponential distribution (memoryless, useful life).
- If $\beta > 1$: $h(t)$ is **increasing** — represents wear-out failures (aging components).
- If $\beta = 2$: Weibull reduces to the **Rayleigh distribution**.

This flexibility makes the Weibull model the most widely used parametric reliability model.

> **[3 Marks Breakdown: 1 for stating $h(t)$, 1 for stating $R(t)$, 1 for explaining $\beta$.]**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **[KTU 2024 Scheme Internal Choice: Solve EITHER Question A OR Question B]**

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** A machine component has a Rayleigh hazard model with parameter $\sigma = 1000$ hours. Compute:
> - (i) The reliability at $t = 800$ hours.
> - (ii) The probability that the component survives beyond 1500 hours, given it has already survived 500 hours.
> - (iii) The MTTF of the component.
> 
> **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** A Weibull-distributed component has $\alpha = 5000$ hours and $\beta = 2.5$. Find:
> - (i) The reliability at $t = 3000$ hours.
> - (ii) The B(10) life (time at which 10% will fail).
> - (iii) The MTTF.
> 
> **[7 Marks]**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Rayleigh Hazard Model Computations

**Given:** $\sigma = 1000$ hours

**(i) Reliability at $t = 800$ hours:**

The Rayleigh reliability function is:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\frac{t^2}{2\sigma^2}\right]$$

Substituting $t = 800$, $\sigma = 1000$:

$$R(800) = \exp\!\left[-\frac{(800)^2}{2 \times (1000)^2}\right] = \exp\!\left[-\frac{640000}{2000000}\right] = \exp[-0.32]$$

$$R(800) = 0.7261$$

**(ii) Conditional probability of surviving beyond 1500 hours given survival to 500 hours:**

By definition of conditional reliability:

$$R(1500 \mid 500) = \frac{R(1500)}{R(500)}$$

**Compute $R(1500)$:**

$$R(1500) = \exp\!\left[-\frac{(1500)^2}{2 \times 10^6}\right] = \exp\!\left[-\frac{2250000}{2000000}\right] = \exp[-1.125] = 0.3247$$

**Compute $R(500)$:**

$$R(500) = \exp\!\left[-\frac{(500)^2}{2 \times 10^6}\right] = \exp\!\left[-\frac{250000}{2000000}\right] = \exp[-0.125] = 0.8825$$

**Conditional reliability:**

$$R(1500 \mid 500) = \frac{0.3247}{0.8825} = 0.3679$$

**(iii) Mean Time To Failure:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \sigma\sqrt{\frac{\pi}{2}} = 1000 \times 1.2533 = 1253.3 \text{ hours}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Weibull Distribution Computations

**Given:** $\alpha = 5000$ hr, $\beta = 2.5$

**(i) Reliability at $t = 3000$ hours:**

$$R(3000) = \exp\!\left[-\left(\frac{3000}{5000}\right)^{2.5}\right] = \exp\!\left[-(0.6)^{2.5}\right]$$

Compute $(0.6)^{2.5} = (0.6)^2 \cdot (0.6)^{0.5} = 0.36 \times 0.7746 = 0.2789$.

$$R(3000) = \exp[-0.2789] = 0.7567$$

**(ii) B(10) Life:**

$$t_{B(10)} = \alpha \left[-\ln(0.9)\right]^{1/\beta} = 5000 \times [0.10536]^{1/2.5}$$

Compute $(0.10536)^{0.4}$:

Taking $\ln(0.10536) = -2.2504$; multiplying by $0.4$: $-0.9002$; exponentiating: $e^{-0.9002} = 0.4066$.

$$t_{B(10)} = 5000 \times 0.4066 = 2033 \text{ hours}$$

**(iii) MTTF:**

$$\text{MTTF} = \alpha \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1 + \frac{1}{\beta}\right) = 5000 \cdot \Gamma(1.4)$$

From Gamma tables: $\Gamma(1.4) \approx 0.8873$.

$$\text{MTTF} = 5000 \times 0.8873 = 4436.5 \text{ hours}$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Paper]`

> **(a)** For a component following a Linear hazard model with $h(t) = 0.0002 + 2 \times 10^{-7} t$ (per hour), calculate:
> - (i) The reliability at $t = 500$ hours.
> - (ii) The cumulative hazard at $t = 1000$ hours.
> - (iii) The probability density function value at $t = 750$ hours.
> 
> **[7 Marks]**
>
> **(b)** During a life test of 20 identical components, 14 failed with the following failure times (in hours): 120, 250, 380, 510, 640, 770, 900, 1030, 1160, 1290, 1420, 1550, 1680, 1810. The remaining 6 were censored at 2000 hours. Assuming the underlying failure distribution is Exponential:
> - (i) Find the MLE of the failure rate $\lambda$.
> - (ii) Compute the MTTF and the reliability for a 500-hour mission.
> - (iii) Construct a 90% two-sided confidence interval for $\lambda$.
> 
> **[7 Marks]**

---

### MODEL SOLUTION — QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Linear Hazard Model Computations

**Given:** $h(t) = h_0 + bt$ where $h_0 = 0.0002 \text{ hr}^{-1}$ and $b = 2 \times 10^{-7} \text{ hr}^{-2}$.

**(i) Reliability at $t = 500$ hours:**

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left[-h_0 t - \frac{b t^2}{2}\right]$$

Substituting:

$$R(500) = \exp\!\left[-(0.0002)(500) - \frac{(2 \times 10^{-7})(500)^2}{2}\right]$$

$$= \exp\!\left[-0.1 - \frac{(2 \times 10^{-7})(250000)}{2}\right] = \exp\!\left[-0.1 - 0.025\right] = \exp[-0.125]$$

$$R(500) = 0.8825$$

**(ii) Cumulative Hazard at $t = 1000$ hours:**

$$H(1000) = h_0 t + \frac{b t^2}{2} = (0.0002)(1000) + \frac{(2 \times 10^{-7})(10^6)}{2} = 0.2 + 0.1 = 0.3$$

**(iii) PDF at $t = 750$ hours:**

$$f(t) = h(t) \cdot R(t)$$

**Compute $h(750)$:**

$$h(750) = 0.0002 + (2 \times 10^{-7})(750) = 0.0002 + 0.00015 = 0.00035 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

**Compute $R(750)$:**

$$H(750) = (0.0002)(750) + \frac{(2 \times 10^{-7})(750)^2}{2} = 0.15 + 0.05625 = 0.20625$$

$$R(750) = \exp[-0.20625] = 0.8137$$

**Therefore:**

$$f(750) = (0.00035)(0.8137) = 2.848 \times 10^{-4} \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

---

#### Part (b) — MLE and Confidence Interval for Exponential

**Given:** $n = 20$, $r = 14$ failures, total observed failure time $T_r = 120 + 250 + 380 + 510 + 640 + 770 + 900 + 1030 + 1160 + 1290 + 1420 + 1550 + 1680 + 1810 = 12510$ hours. Censoring time for 6 survivors: $6 \times 2000 = 12000$ hours.

**Total time on test:**

$$T_{\text{total}} = 12510 + 12000 = 24510 \text{ hours}$$

**(i) MLE of failure rate $\lambda$:**

$$\hat{\lambda} = \frac{r}{T_{\text{total}}} = \frac{14}{24510} = 5.712 \times 10^{-4} \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

**(ii) MTTF and Reliability:**

$$\widehat{\text{MTTF}} = \frac{1}{\hat{\lambda}} = \frac{24510}{14} = 1750.7 \text{ hours}$$

$$R(500) = \exp[-\hat{\lambda} \cdot 500] = \exp[-(5.712 \times 10^{-4})(500)] = \exp[-0.2856] = 0.7516$$

**(iii) 90% Two-Sided Confidence Interval for $\lambda$:**

For Exponential with $r$ failures, the confidence interval uses the Chi-Square distribution:

$$\frac{2r \hat{\lambda}}{\chi^2_{(r, \alpha/2)}} \le \lambda \le \frac{2r \hat{\lambda}}{\chi^2_{(r, 1-\alpha/2)}}$$

For $r = 14$, $\alpha = 0.10$ (90% CI), we need $\chi^2_{(14, 0.05)}$ and $\chi^2_{(14, 0.95)}$:

From Chi-Square tables:
$$\chi^2_{(14, 0.05)} = 23.685, \quad \chi^2_{(14, 0.95)} = 6.571$$

**Lower bound:**

$$\lambda_L = \frac{2 \times 14 \times 5.712 \times 10^{-4}}{23.685} = \frac{0.015994}{23.685} = 6.752 \times 10^{-4} \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

**Upper bound:**

$$\lambda_U = \frac{2 \times 14 \times 5.712 \times 10^{-4}}{6.571} = \frac{0.015994}{6.571} = 24.34 \times 10^{-4} \text{ hr}^{-1}$$

$$\boxed{6.752 \times 10^{-4} \le \lambda \le 24.34 \times 10^{-4} \text{ hr}^{-1}}$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 
> 1. **Confusing $\alpha$ (scale) with $\alpha$ (significance level):** In Weibull problems, $\alpha$ is the scale parameter; in hypothesis testing, $\alpha$ is the significance level. They are **different contexts**. Always check the problem statement.
> 
> 2. **Forgetting to exponentiate:** Many students correctly compute $H(t)$ but forget that $R(t) = e^{-H(t)}$. Always apply the negative exponential at the end.
> 
> 3. **Wrong Gamma function values:** $\Gamma(1.5) = \dfrac{\sqrt{\pi}}{2}$, $\Gamma(2) = 1$, $\Gamma(3) = 2$. Memorize these basic Gamma identities.
> 
> 4. **Miscalculating MTTF for Linear Hazard:** There is no closed-form MTTF for the general Linear Hazard model. Only when $b = 0$ (exponential case) does MTTF = $1/h_0$.
> 
> 5. **Using the wrong Chi-Square tail:** For 90% two-sided CI, use $\alpha/2 = 0.05$ and $1-\alpha/2 = 0.95$. Reversing these bounds gives inverted confidence intervals.
> 
> 6. **Neglecting units:** Always carry units (hr, hr$^{-1}$, hr$^{-2}$) through derivations. KTU examiners explicitly check this.

---

## TOPIC RECAP — High-Density Revision Checklist

> **Print this section. Memorize the boxed facts. You will thank yourself on exam day.**

- **Hazard Function Definition:** $h(t) = \dfrac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\dfrac{d}{dt}\ln R(t)$
- **Master Inversion Formula:** $R(t) = \exp\!\left[-\displaystyle\int_{0}^{t} h(\tau)\, d\tau\right]$
- **Linear Hazard:** $h(t) = h_0 + bt$, $R(t) = \exp[-h_0 t - bt^2/2]$, **no closed-form MTTF**, reduces to Exponential when $b = 0$.
- **Rayleigh Hazard:** $h(t) = \dfrac{t}{\sigma^2}$, $R(t) = \exp[-t^2/(2\sigma^2)]$, $\text{MTTF} = \sigma\sqrt{\pi/2}$, equivalent to **Weibull with $\beta = 2$**.
- **Weibull Hazard:** $h(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\alpha}(t/\alpha)^{\beta-1}$, $R(t) = \exp[-(t/\alpha)^{\beta}]$, $\text{MTTF} = \alpha\,\Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$.
- **B(x) Life:** $t_{B(x)} = \alpha[-\ln(1 - x/100)]^{1/\beta}$.
- **Shape Parameter Effect:** $\beta < 1$ = infant mortality; $\beta = 1$ = constant (exponential); $\beta > 1$ = wear-out; $\beta = 2$ = Rayleigh.
- **MLE for Exponential (Censored):** $\hat{\lambda} = \dfrac{r}{\sum t_i + (n-r)T_{\text{censor}}}$.
- **Confidence Interval for $\lambda$:** Uses Chi-Square distribution with $2r$ degrees of freedom.
- **Reliability Tests:** Fixed Sample (Binomial / Chi-Square) vs Sequential (SPRT) — choose based on cost and risk tolerance.
- **Engineering Mapping:** Linear = gradual wear-out; Rayleigh = fatigue cracks; Weibull = universal fit.
- **Special Trick:** $h(t) \cdot R(t) = f(t)$. If you know any two, you can compute the third.

---

**END OF MODULE 4 NOTES — HAZARD MODELS**

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PERUT636 - QUALITY AND RELIABILITY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Failure Data Analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Failure Data Analysis (FDA)** is the systematic statistical and engineering process of collecting, classifying, modelling, and interpreting data pertaining to the time-to-failure (or number-of-cycles-to-failure) of components, sub-assemblies, or systems, in order to quantify their reliability characteristics, identify failure mechanisms, predict future performance, and support decisions on warranty, maintenance, and design improvement.

In reliability engineering, failure data forms the **empirical backbone** of every reliability prediction. Without well-organised data, even the most elegant mathematical reliability model (Weibull, Exponential, Lognormal) is reduced to a *theoretical abstraction* with no engineering value.

### 1.2 Categories of Failure Data

KTU syllabus requires the engineer to distinguish between the *quality* of the input data because each type mandates a different analytical tool.

| Data Class | Description | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Complete Data** | Every unit has failed by the end of the test. | Life-testing 50 bulbs until all burn out. |
| **Right-Censored (Suspended) Data** | Some units are still running at test termination. | 20 motors, 12 failed, 8 still alive at 5,000 h. |
| **Interval-Censored (Grouped) Data** | Failure known only to lie within a window. | Inspection of bearings every 250 h. |
| **Left-Censored Data** | Failure occurred before observation began. | Field warranty returns with unknown service time. |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (The "Hospital Triage" Model)

> [!TIP]
> Imagine a **hospital emergency ward** that records the exact time every admitted patient either *recovers* (success) or *dies* (failure). Some patients are still under treatment when the doctor's shift ends — these are the **suspended cases**. A statistician cannot simply *ignore* the still-admitted patients (that would bias the death-rate upward), nor can they assume all survivors would have died. Failure Data Analysis is precisely the statistical "triage" that uses mathematical methods (Kaplan–Meier, Actuarial, Weibull plotting) to **fairly extract the maximum reliability information** from a mix of completed and incomplete patient records.

### 1.4 Why Failure Data Analysis is a *KTU High-Weightage Topic*

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Module 4 of PERUT636 transforms abstract reliability functions $R(t)$, $F(t)$, $h(t)$ and $\lambda(t)$ into **numerically quantifiable engineering parameters**. The board examiner's perspective: *a student who can analyse a small dataset of 10–20 failure times and produce a usable Weibull shape parameter $\beta$ and scale parameter $\eta$ is ready to work in any Industry 4.0 reliability group.*

### 1.5 Engineering Significance (Physical Constants & Metrics)

- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures):** $\theta = \int_0^\infty R(t)\,dt$ (h or cycles).
- **MTTF (Mean Time To Failure):** for non-repairable items, identical to MTBF.
- **Hazard Rate $\lambda(t)$:** instantaneous failure rate, **units = 1/time**.
- **Reliability Function $R(t)$:** **dimensionless** probability $\in [0,1]$.
- **B10 Life:** time at which **10% of the population has failed** — heavily used in bearing and aerospace specs.
- **L10 Life:** classical bearing rating life (now superseded by L10 in ISO 281:2007).
- **Characteristic Life $\eta$ (Weibull):** time at which 63.2% of items have failed.
- **Acceleration Factor (AF):** dimensionless multiplier relating stress levels, governed by the **Arrhenius equation** for temperature:

$$ AF = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{k_B}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_{use}}-\frac{1}{T_{acc}}\right)\right] $$

where $E_a$ is the activation energy (eV), $k_B$ is the **Boltzmann constant = $8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K**, and $T$ is absolute temperature (K).

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Seven Logical Steps of Failure Data Analysis

A KTU board answer that demonstrates clear methodological structure scores far higher than a vague recollection of formulas. Use the following seven-step pipeline as the analytical backbone.

1. **Data Acquisition & Cleaning** — record the exact failure times (or suspension times), batch/serial numbers, operating conditions, and censoring flags.
2. **Data Classification** — sort into complete, right-censored, and interval-censored subsets.
3. **Choice of Non-Parametric vs Parametric Approach**:
   - Use **non-parametric** (Kaplan–Meier, Actuarial) when no distribution can be assumed *a priori* or sample size $n$ is small ($n<20$).
   - Use **parametric** (Weibull, Exponential, Lognormal) when $n \ge 20$ and physical failure physics supports a model.
4. **Ranking of Failures** — assign failure order numbers $i = 1, 2, \dots, r$ and compute the plotting position $F_i$.
5. **Probability Plotting** — plot the failure data on the appropriate probability paper; perform linear regression to extract distribution parameters.
6. **Parameter Estimation Refinement** — use **Maximum Likelihood Estimation (MLE)** to refine the graphical estimates; MLE is *asymptotically efficient, unbiased, and consistent*.
7. **Goodness-of-Fit & Confidence Bounds** — apply the **Anderson–Darling** or **Kolmogorov–Smirnov** statistic; report two-sided 90% confidence bounds on $R(t)$.

### 2.2 The Why-How of Plotting Positions

> [!TIP]
> **Plotting position** = the empirical estimate of the cumulative failure probability $F(t_i)$ assigned to the $i$-th ordered failure. Different formulas are used because each represents a different assumption about the *prior* probability that a unit will fail in the next instant.

The three most-used KTU-grade formulas are:

- **Mean Rank (Brom's standard for non-censored data):**

$$ F_i^{MR} = \frac{i}{n+1} $$

- **Median Rank (preferred — Hazen / Benard approximation, more accurate for small $n$):**

$$ F_i^{MdR} = \frac{i - 0.3}{n + 0.4} $$

- **Mean Rank with Suspensions (used when censoring exists, in conjunction with Kaplan–Meier):**

$$ F_i^{MR,s} = \frac{i}{N_{i}+1} $$

where $N_i$ is the *risk set* (number still operating just before the $i$-th failure).

### 2.3 The "Why" of Censoring Correction

A censored observation is *informative* — it tells us the unit survived until time $t_{s}$. If we naïvely ignored it, our **hazard rate would be biased upward**. Methods like the **Actuarial method** (intervals) and **Kaplan–Meier product-limit** (point failures) preserve this information by using the *conditional survival probability* in each interval.

### 2.4 Parametric Distribution Candidates — Selection Logic

| Failure Physics | Recommended Distribution | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| Random, memoryless failures | **Exponential** | Constant hazard $\lambda(t)=\lambda$ |
| Wear-out, fatigue, crack growth | **Weibull** ($\beta > 1$) | Increasing hazard captures degradation |
| Early-life (infant mortality) | **Weibull** ($\beta < 1$) | Decreasing hazard |
| Repair-time, multiplicative stress | **Lognormal** | Multiplication of small random effects → log-normal |
| Many independent weak-link failures | **Extreme-Value / Weibull** | Minima of many random variables |

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Examination Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Memorise the entries in the table below. They collectively cover $\ge 90\%$ of the numerical questions asked in PERUT636 module-end and ESE papers.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Reliability function | $R(t) = 1 - F(t)$ | dimensionless |
| 2 | Exponential CDF | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda > 0$, $1/\text{time}$ |
| 3 | Exponential reliability | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | dimensionless |
| 4 | Exponential PDF | $f(t) = \lambda e^{-\lambda t}$ | $1/\text{time}$ |
| 5 | Exponential MTTF | $MTTF = 1/\lambda$ | time |
| 6 | Weibull CDF | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $t \ge 0$, $\beta, \eta > 0$ |
| 7 | Weibull PDF | $f(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | $1/\text{time}$ |
| 8 | Weibull reliability | $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$ | dimensionless |
| 9 | Weibull hazard | $h(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | $1/\text{time}$ |
| 10 | Weibull characteristic life | $t_{0.632} = \eta$ | time |
| 11 | Weibull MTTF | $MTTF = \eta \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta)$ | time; $\Gamma$ = gamma fn. |
| 12 | Lognormal CDF | $F(t) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{\ln t - \mu}{\sigma}\right)$ | $\Phi$ = standard normal CDF |
| 13 | Lognormal MTTF | $MTTF = e^{\mu + \sigma^{2}/2}$ | time |
| 14 | Median Rank | $F_i = (i - 0.3)/(n + 0.4)$ | empirical CDF |
| 15 | Mean Rank | $F_i = i/(n+1)$ | empirical CDF |
| 16 | Arrhenius AF | $AF = \exp\!\left[\frac{E_a}{k_B}\!\left(\frac{1}{T_u}-\frac{1}{T_a}\right)\right]$ | dimensionless |
| 17 | Kaplan–Meier | $\hat{R}(t_k) = \prod_{t_i \le t_k}\!\left(1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i}\right)$ | step function |
| 18 | Two-sided 90% CI on $R$ | $R_{L,U} = R^{\exp(\pm z_{0.95} \cdot \text{SE})}$ | log-transformed CI |
| 19 | Weibull shape $\beta$ (graphical) | $\beta = \text{slope of } \ln\!\ln\!\left(\frac{1}{1-F}\right)$ vs $\ln t$ | dimensionless |
| 20 | Weibull scale $\eta$ (graphical) | $\eta = e^{-\text{intercept}/\beta}$ | time |

**Important Conventions Used in the Table**

- $\Gamma(\cdot)$ denotes the **Gamma function**, e.g., $\Gamma(1)=1$, $\Gamma(1.5)=\sqrt{\pi}/2$, $\Gamma(2)=1$.
- $\Phi(\cdot)$ denotes the **Standard Normal Cumulative Distribution Function** (look up in the table at the back of any engineering statistics text).
- The hazard rate $h(t)$ and failure rate $\lambda(t)$ are used interchangeably; in KTU examination scripts always state your assumed definition.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Aerospace turbine blade manufacturers** use Weibull analysis of fatigue data to certify **B1 / B10 life** specifications.
- **Automotive OEM warranty departments** apply Lognormal fitting to field-failure kilometre data, allowing forecast of monthly warranty costs.
- **Power plant operators** apply Exponential / Weibull hazard models to schedule **condition-based maintenance** and **risk-based inspection** (RBI).
- **Semiconductor fabs** use Arrhenius-accelerated life testing to project **10-year field reliability from 1,000-hour laboratory tests**.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Weibull Parameters from a Probability Plot

The Weibull cumulative distribution function is:

$$ F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} $$

**Step 1 — Rearrange to isolate the exponential term:**

$$ 1 - F(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} $$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm on both sides:**

$$ \ln\!\bigl[1 - F(t)\bigr] = -(t/\eta)^{\beta} $$

**Step 3 — Multiply by $-1$ and take the natural logarithm a second time (this is the "double-log" trick):**

$$ \ln\!\left\{-\ln\!\bigl[1 - F(t)\bigr]\right\} = \beta \ln t - \beta \ln\eta $$

**Step 4 — Define new variables for linearisation:**

$$ Y = \ln\!\left\{-\ln\!\bigl[1 - F(t)\bigr]\right\}, \qquad X = \ln t $$

**Step 5 — Recognise the linear equation $Y = mX + c$:**

$$ m = \beta, \qquad c = -\beta \ln\eta $$

**Step 6 — Estimate the slope $m$ and intercept $c$ by least-squares regression of $(X_i, Y_i)$ pairs, then recover the Weibull parameters:**

$$ \hat{\beta} = m, \qquad \hat{\eta} = e^{-c/m} $$

**Engineering Interpretation**

- A slope $\hat{\beta} > 1$ indicates **wear-out** (hazard increases with time — typical of bearings, fatigue, dies).
- A slope $\hat{\beta} = 1$ reduces the Weibull model to the **Exponential** distribution.
- A slope $\hat{\beta} < 1$ indicates **infant mortality** (hazard decreases with time — typical of early software bugs or manufacturing defects that surface quickly).

### 3.2 Derivation of the Maximum Likelihood Estimates for the Weibull Distribution (Complete Data)

Consider $n$ independent, identically distributed (i.i.d.) failure times $t_1, t_2, \dots, t_n$ with Weibull pdf:

$$ f(t_i) = \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-(t_i/\eta)^{\beta}} $$

**Step 1 — Write the likelihood function as the product of individual pdfs:**

$$ L(\beta, \eta) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} \frac{\beta}{\eta}\left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1} e^{-(t_i/\eta)^{\beta}} $$

**Step 2 — Take the natural logarithm to obtain the log-likelihood $\ln L$:**

$$ \ln L = n \ln\beta - n\beta \ln\eta + (\beta - 1)\sum_{i=1}^{n}\ln t_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta} $$

**Step 3 — Partial derivative w.r.t. $\eta$, set to zero for MLE of $\eta$:**

$$ \frac{\partial \ln L}{\partial \eta} = -\frac{n\beta}{\eta} + \frac{\beta}{\eta^{\beta + 1}}\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i^{\beta} = 0 $$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\hat{\eta}$:**

$$ \hat{\eta} = \left(\frac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^{n} t_i^{\hat{\beta}}\right)^{1/\hat{\beta}} $$

**Step 5 — Partial derivative w.r.t. $\beta$, set to zero:**

$$ \frac{\partial \ln L}{\partial \beta} = \frac{n}{\beta} - n \ln\eta + \sum_{i=1}^{n}\ln t_i - \sum_{i=1}^{n}\left(\frac{t_i}{\eta}\right)^{\beta}\ln t_i + \frac{\ln\eta}{\eta^{\beta}}\sum_{i=1}^{n}t_i^{\beta} = 0 $$

**Step 6 — Iterative Solution:**

No closed-form expression exists for $\hat{\beta}$; **Newton–Raphson iteration** (or commercial solvers, e.g., ReliaSoft Weibull++) is required. The student must show:

$$ \hat{\beta}_{k+1} = \hat{\beta}_{k} - \left[\frac{\partial^{2}\ln L}{\partial\beta^{2}}\right]_{\beta_k}^{-1} \cdot \left[\frac{\partial \ln L}{\partial \beta}\right]_{\beta_k} $$

### 3.3 Kaplan–Meier Product-Limit Estimator (with Suspensions)

Let there be $r$ distinct failure times $t_{(1)} < t_{(2)} < \dots < t_{(r)}$ and let $n_i$ denote the *number at risk* immediately before $t_{(i)}$, and $d_i$ the number of failures at $t_{(i)}$.

**Step 1 — The conditional probability of surviving past $t_{(i)}$ given survival up to $t_{(i)}^{-}$:**

$$ \hat{p}_i = 1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i} $$

**Step 2 — The Kaplan–Meier estimator multiplies the conditional survival probabilities up to time $t$:**

$$ \hat{R}(t) = \prod_{i : t_{(i)} \le t}\left(1 - \frac{d_i}{n_i}\right) $$

**Step 3 — Greenwood's formula for the variance of $\hat{R}(t)$ (used for confidence intervals):**

$$ \widehat{\text{Var}}\!\bigl[\hat{R}(t)\bigr] = \bigl[\hat{R}(t)\bigr]^{2} \sum_{i : t_{(i)} \le t} \frac{d_i}{n_i(n_i - d_i)} $$

**Step 4 — The two-sided $100(1-\alpha)\%$ confidence interval for $R(t)$ on the log–log scale:**

$$ R_L(t) = \bigl[\hat{R}(t)\bigr]^{\exp\!\left(\frac{z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \widehat{\text{SE}}}{\ln\hat{R}(t)}\right)} $$

$$ R_U(t) = \bigl[\hat{R}(t)\bigr]^{\exp\!\left(\frac{-z_{\alpha/2} \cdot \widehat{\text{SE}}}{\ln\hat{R}(t)}\right)} $$

where $\widehat{\text{SE}} = \sqrt{\widehat{\text{Var}}\!\bigl[\hat{R}(t)\bigr]}/\hat{R}(t)$ and $z_{\alpha/2}$ is the standard normal quantile (1.645 for 90% CI, 1.96 for 95% CI).

### 3.4 Worked Example — Median Rank / Weibull Plot from $n=8$ Failures

Suppose eight bearings are tested to failure with times (in hours):

$$ t = \{ 1200, 1850, 2300, 2700, 3400, 4200, 5500, 7100 \} $$

**Step 1 — Compute the median rank $F_i = (i - 0.3)/(n + 0.4) = (i - 0.3)/8.4$ for $i = 1, \dots, 8$:**

| $i$ | $t_i$ (h) | $F_i$ | $Y_i = \ln[-\ln(1-F_i)]$ | $X_i = \ln t_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1200 | 0.0833 | -2.418 | 7.090 |
| 2 | 1850 | 0.2024 | -1.412 | 7.523 |
| 3 | 2300 | 0.3214 | -0.812 | 7.740 |
| 4 | 2700 | 0.4405 | -0.366 | 7.901 |
| 5 | 3400 | 0.5595 | +0.0004 | 8.131 |
| 6 | 4200 | 0.6786 | +0.360 | 8.343 |
| 7 | 5500 | 0.7976 | +0.738 | 8.613 |
| 8 | 7100 | 0.9167 | +1.190 | 8.868 |

**Step 2 — Linear regression $Y = mX + c$ on the eight $(X_i, Y_i)$ pairs:**

$$ \bar{X} = 8.026, \qquad \bar{Y} = -0.340 $$

$$ S_{xx} = \sum (X_i - \bar X)^2 = 2.260, \qquad S_{xy} = \sum (X_i - \bar X)(Y_i - \bar Y) = 5.044 $$

$$ m = S_{xy}/S_{xx} = 5.044/2.260 = 2.232, \qquad c = \bar Y - m\bar X = -0.340 - (2.232)(8.026) = -18.249 $$

**Step 3 — Recover the Weibull parameters:**

$$ \hat{\beta} = m = 2.23, \qquad \hat{\eta} = e^{-c/m} = e^{18.249/2.232} = e^{8.176} \approx 3557 \text{ h} $$

**Step 4 — Compute the B10 life (time at which 10% of the population has failed, i.e., $F(t) = 0.10$):**

$$ 0.10 = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}} \;\Rightarrow\; (t/\eta)^{\beta} = -\ln(0.90) = 0.10536 $$

$$ t = \eta (0.10536)^{1/\beta} = 3557 \times (0.10536)^{1/2.23} = 3557 \times 0.3854 = 1371 \text{ h} $$

**Step 5 — Compute the MTTF (numerical value using the gamma function):**

$$ MTTF = \eta \Gamma(1 + 1/\beta) = 3557 \times \Gamma(1.448) $$

Since $\Gamma(1.448) \approx 0.886$ (from gamma tables),

$$ MTTF \approx 3557 \times 0.886 \approx 3152 \text{ h} $$

**Engineering Conclusion:** With $\hat{\beta} = 2.23 > 1$, the bearings exhibit *clear wear-out* behaviour. The plant should schedule preventive replacement at or before the **B10 life of 1,371 h** to maintain high fleet reliability.

### 3.5 Acceleration Factor — Numerical Application

A chip is rated for 10 years at 55 °C. Accelerated testing is performed at 125 °C. Given $E_a = 0.7$ eV and $k_B = 8.617 \times 10^{-5}$ eV/K:

$$ T_u = 55 + 273.15 = 328.15 \text{ K}, \qquad T_a = 125 + 273.15 = 398.15 \text{ K} $$

$$ AF = \exp\!\left[\frac{0.7}{8.617 \times 10^{-5}}\left(\frac{1}{328.15} - \frac{1}{398.15}\right)\right] $$

$$ \frac{1}{328.15} - \frac{1}{398.15} = 0.003048 - 0.002512 = 5.36 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1} $$

$$ AF = \exp\!\left[\frac{0.7 \times 5.36 \times 10^{-4}}{8.617 \times 10^{-5}}\right] = \exp(4.355) \approx 77.9 $$

**Interpretation:** 1 hour at 125 °C is equivalent to 77.9 hours at 55 °C. To demonstrate 10 years = 87,600 h of field life, an accelerated test of only $87{,}600 / 77.9 \approx 1{,}124$ h is sufficient (≈ 47 days).

### 3.6 Analytic Decision Matrix — Which Method to Use

| Question in Hand | Recommended Tool | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| All units failed, $n$ small, no distribution assumed | **Median rank + probability paper** | Simple, visually transparent |
| All units failed, $n \ge 20$, wear-out suspected | **Weibull MLE** | Asymptotic efficiency, supports CI |
| Many suspensions, point-failure data | **Kaplan–Meier** | Uses the censored information |
| Interval inspection data, grouped failures | **Actuarial method** | Designed for grouped/censored bins |
| Test under multiple stress levels | **Arrhenius + Weibull** | Combines physics with statistics |
| Confidence intervals required on $R(t)$ | **MLE + Fisher Information Matrix** | Yields both point and interval estimates |
| Comparing two designs | **Likelihood Ratio Test (LRT)** | Rigorous hypothesis testing |

### 4.3 Weibull Probability Plot Schematic (Schematic Description)

The Weibull probability paper is constructed so that the X-axis is logarithmic in time and the Y-axis is the *double-log* transformed cumulative failure probability. On this special paper, a Weibull distribution plots as a *straight line* — slope $= \beta$, intercept $= -\beta \ln \eta$.

| Plot Element | Meaning on Weibull Paper |
|---|---|
| **X-axis label** | $\ln t$ (natural log of time) |
| **Y-axis label** | $\ln[-\ln(1-F)]$ (double-log transform) |
| **Slope of the line** | Weibull shape parameter $\beta$ |
| **Y-intercept (extrapolated to $t=1$)** | $-\beta \ln\eta$ |
| **Point at $F=0.632$** | The characteristic life $\eta$ |
| **Point at $F=0.10$** | B10 life (read on X-axis) |

A non-linear pattern on this paper is the diagnostic signature of *the wrong distribution has been chosen*. A concave-up curve suggests Lognormal; a concave-down curve suggests that the data may contain a mixture of two failure modes (a "dogleg" plot).
